project_goal.value: React Native mobile shopping app for mid-size fashion retailer migrating from Magento backend to headless commerce architecture
project_goal.rationale: Strategic migration to enable app-first mobile commerce with modern checkout flows

target_users.value: Fashion shoppers on iOS and Android browsing curated collections, managing wishlists, and completing purchases via Apple Pay and Google Pay
target_users.rationale: Primary consumer audience identified in discovery workshops

project_class.rationale: Native mobile app as primary client; no web storefront in scope for this phase

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, payments, analytics, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Full commerce capability set including payment wallets and Magento integration layer

complexity_level.rationale: Dual-runtime migration with live commerce traffic, wallet payments, and legacy system bridge

risk_level.rationale: Card tokenization, live payment processing, and migration risk from legacy Magento

data_sensitivity.rationale: Handles payment methods, card tokens, and purchase history

external_exposure.rationale: Consumer-facing app on public app stores; browsing available without login

access_model.value: Guest browsing allowed; Cognito account required for checkout, wishlists, and order history; OAuth 2.0 PKCE for mobile
access_model.rationale: Friction-free discovery with authentication gate at purchase intent

feature_scope.value: Style collection browsing, product detail pages, wishlists, cross-session cart, checkout with Apple Pay and Google Pay, order tracking, return requests
feature_scope.rationale: Core commerce loop identified in product discovery

mvp_scope.value: Browse collections, add to cart, checkout with Stripe card and Apple Pay, order confirmation; migration of 6 months of Magento customer data
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for app store launch and migration cut-over

security_baseline.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A-EP: no raw card data on server, Stripe.js tokenization on device, TLS 1.3, certificate pinning on mobile, WAF on API Gateway
security_baseline.rationale: Card tokenization model satisfies SAQ A-EP scope reduction

frontend_stack.value: React Native 0.74 with TypeScript, Expo Router for navigation, React Query for server state
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform mobile with TypeScript end-to-end and file-system routing

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Fastify on ECS Fargate; separate Magento Bridge service for legacy API translation
backend_stack.rationale: High-throughput headless API with isolated legacy bridge for 6-month parallel run

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for commerce data; Redis 7 for cart sessions and inventory cache
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for orders and profiles; Redis for sub-millisecond cart access

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1; CloudFront for product image delivery; app distributed via App Store and Google Play
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with CDN for product imagery

privacy_retention_policy.value: GDPR: 30-day right-to-erasure SLA for EU shoppers; order records retained 7 years for tax compliance; session data purged after 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: EU shopper base requires GDPR compliance; tax law drives order retention

future_scope.value: AI outfit recommendations, loyalty points, social wishlist sharing, web storefront reusing same headless API
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap pending app launch success

constraints.value: Migration must support 6-month parallel run with Magento; app store release in 16 weeks; budget under $3000/month post-launch
constraints.rationale: Parallel run needed for gradual traffic cutover; commercial timeline fixed

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on Fargate tasks; Datadog RUM for mobile checkout funnel; custom events for cart abandonment and payment failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Unified observability across API and mobile client

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; feature flags for gradual Magento traffic migration; blue-green deployment for major API releases
execution_preference.rationale: Risk management via incremental traffic shifting and zero-downtime deploys

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A-EP for card tokenization via Stripe; GDPR for EU shopper data including right to erasure and data portability
compliance_context.rationale: Card payment and EU user base trigger both PCI and GDPR obligations

project.goal: Headless commerce mobile app replacing Magento with parallel migration path
project.target_users: Fashion shoppers on iOS and Android

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for cart and UI state

backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.0; Magento Bridge as separate Fargate service

security.auth: Cognito with OAuth 2.0 PKCE for mobile
security.payments: Stripe tokenization, Apple Pay, Google Pay
security.transport: TLS 1.3, certificate pinning in React Native

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for orders, profiles, wishlists
data.cache: Redis 7 for cart sessions and product availability

reasoner_reviews.product: The migration strategy must balance app store launch timeline against data completeness. The 6-month parallel run is sound for gradual cutover but requires cart session bridging to prevent duplicate orders when a shopper switches surfaces. Wishlist deduplication during the Magento data import is underspecified and must be addressed in the data migration runbook.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Fastify on Fargate is appropriate for the headless commerce API at this scale. The Magento Bridge should be a separate Fargate service with independent auto-scaling since legacy XML parsing has a very different CPU profile than JSON commerce API calls. Redis cart sessions need a merge-on-login strategy to handle carts created before authentication.
reasoner_reviews.security: Stripe SAQ A-EP compliance requires explicit documentation that no card data reaches the Fastify server at any point. The checkout flow must show Stripe.js creating the PaymentMethod on device, with only a payment_intent_id sent to the backend. Certificate pinning in React Native with a backup pin prevents payment interception on rooted devices.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 16-week timeline is achievable if Magento Bridge development begins in parallel with the core commerce API in sprint 1. The $3000/month budget is tight; CloudFront caching for product images and right-sized Fargate tasks are essential to stay within budget.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The round 1 plan failed to specify the cart session merge-on-login flow. A shopper with Magento guest cart items who signs in to the new app would lose those items. This merge workflow must be detailed in both the API design and workflows sections before this plan can be approved.

frontend.app_structure: Expo Router file-system navigation with (auth) and (shop) route groups; browsing routes accessible to guests, checkout and account routes behind Cognito auth guard component
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home feed with style collection carousels, Collection viewer with filter rail (size/color/price), Product Detail with image carousel and size selector, Cart drawer, Checkout (address, payment method, Apple Pay sheet), Order Confirmation, Order History, Return Request form
frontend.state_management: React Query for product catalog, orders, and wishlist data with stale-while-revalidate; Zustand cart store synced to Redis session on authentication; optimistic updates on wishlist toggle to prevent UI flicker
frontend.performance_notes: Expo Image component with AVIF/WebP format detection for product imagery; collection pages prefetch next page at 70% scroll depth; cart badge count cached in Zustand to avoid round-trip on every navigation render
frontend.frontend_risks: React Native certificate pinning updates require app store re-submission; 90-day pin rotation window with 30-day overlap between old and new pins must be planned in advance

backend.service_design: Commerce API service (Fastify, handles catalog, cart, orders, returns) and Magento Bridge service (separate Fastify instance, translates Magento REST v2 responses to internal domain models during 6-month parallel run)
backend.api_patterns: REST with versioned routes /api/v1; POST /checkout/intents accepts cart_session_key and payment_method_id, returns client_secret only; no card data field accepted on any endpoint
backend.business_modules: ProductModule (catalog, collection curation), CartModule (Redis-backed sessions with TTL, merge-on-login), CheckoutModule (Stripe PaymentIntents, Apple Pay domain association), OrderModule (fulfillment state machine, carrier webhook receiver), ReturnModule (RMA workflow)
backend.failure_handling: Idempotency keys required on all checkout and order endpoints; Stripe webhook events processed via SQS to decouple retry logic; Magento Bridge failures isolated from commerce API via circuit breaker

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow in React Native; guest session tokens issued as short-lived JWTs (7-day expiry) for cart persistence without account creation
security.authorization_model: Shopper role for self-service order and profile access; Admin role via back-office with separate Cognito group claim; resource ownership enforced on all order and payment_method queries
security.secrets_management: Stripe secret key and webhook signing secret stored in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation; Magento OAuth credentials rotated quarterly; ECS tasks use IAM task roles with no hardcoded credentials in container images
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting on checkout endpoints at 10 requests/minute per IP via ALB listener rules; Stripe Radar for fraud scoring on each PaymentIntent; WAF rule blocking requests containing card-number-pattern strings in request body

data.entities: product_listings (id UUID, sku VARCHAR UNIQUE, name, brand_id, category_path, price_cents INT, inventory_qty INT, images_json JSONB, status), style_collections (id UUID, title, curator_note, product_ids UUID[], published_at), shopper_profiles (id UUID, cognito_sub UNIQUE, email_hash, shipping_addresses_json, gdpr_erasure_requested_at), wishlists (id UUID, shopper_id FK, sku_id FK, added_at), cart_sessions (session_key VARCHAR in Redis HASH, shopper_id nullable, items_json, expires_at TTL 30 days), checkout_orders (id UUID, shopper_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id, status ENUM, line_items_json, created_at), return_requests (id UUID, order_id FK, reason_code, item_skus[], status ENUM, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for transactional commerce data; Redis 7 ElastiCache for cart sessions with 30-day TTL; S3 for product images served via CloudFront
data.retention_and_deletion: GDPR erasure: anonymize shopper_profiles PII within 30 days of erasure request; order records retained 7 years with PII replaced by pseudonymous IDs; cart sessions auto-expire at Redis TTL

devops.deployment_topology: Two ECS Fargate services: commerce-api (2-8 tasks, CPU 60% auto-scaling target) and magento-bridge (1-3 tasks, SQS queue-depth scaling); CloudFront distribution for product images and API passthrough caching
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, Prettier, Jest unit tests, Testcontainers integration tests against real PostgreSQL and Redis, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update with health-check gate requiring minimum 100% healthy tasks
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecars on both Fargate services; Datadog RUM SDK in React Native tracking checkout funnel events; custom metrics: payment_intent_failure_rate, cart_migration_merge_success_rate, magento_bridge_latency_p99
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling update with automatic rollback on consecutive health-check failures; LaunchDarkly feature flags to shift traffic percentage back to Magento if commerce API degrades during parallel run

ISS-001.title: Cart session migration from Magento not specified
ISS-001.detail: The round 1 plan had no mechanism for merging a Magento guest cart into the new Redis-backed cart on first sign-in to the new app, risking cart loss and duplicate orders during the 6-month parallel run.

history.note: No merge-on-login logic documented in workflows or API design

ISS-002.detail: The round 1 plan did not explicitly confirm that zero card data reaches the Fastify server. The checkout flow must document that only a Stripe payment_intent_id is sent to the backend.

history.note: Auditor flagged missing explicit PCI data flow in security section

previous_plan.title: Fashion Mobile Commerce Headless Migration Plan v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native 0.74 app backed by Fastify headless commerce API on ECS Fargate, with Magento Bridge service for 6-month parallel run. Supports browsing, wishlists, cart, and Apple Pay checkout.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React Native client calls Fastify REST API on ECS Fargate; PostgreSQL 16 for orders and profiles; Redis 7 for cart sessions; separate Magento Bridge service isolates legacy API calls.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Cognito PKCE auth, Stripe tokenization for PCI scope, TLS 1.3, WAF on ALB. GDPR erasure endpoint planned but not detailed.
previous_plan.data_model: product_listings, style_collections, shopper_profiles, wishlists, cart_sessions, checkout_orders, return_requests in PostgreSQL; cart_sessions in Redis 7.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses two blocking issues: ISS-002 (PCI card data path) requires documenting the Stripe.js on-device tokenization flow so only payment_intent_id reaches Fastify; ISS-001 (cart migration) requires a merge-on-login workflow via the Magento Bridge. All other architecture from round 1 is sound and carried forward. The fix_report documents exactly which sections changed for each issue.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit PCI checkout data flow to workflows and security_and_compliance: Stripe.js runs on device and creates a PaymentMethod; app sends only payment_intent_id to POST /checkout/intents; Fastify confirms amount and status with Stripe server-to-server via secret key in Secrets Manager; no PAN, CVV, or card number is logged or stored anywhere in the Fastify service or PostgreSQL.
fix_report.expected_outcome: PCI SAQ A-EP scope confirmed by auditor; explicit tokenization-only data flow traceable through all plan sections

fix_report.action_taken: Added Cart Migration workflow: on Cognito sign-in, app calls GET /migration/cart with cognito_token and legacy_session_cookie; Magento Bridge fetches guest cart from Magento REST v2; Commerce API loads current Redis cart by guest session_key; merges items where SKU not already present; writes unified cart to Redis under shopper_id key with 30-day TTL; idempotency header prevents double-merge on retry.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Shopper cart contents preserved during 6-month parallel run; no duplicate orders on cutover; merge logged for audit

target_output.title: Fashion Mobile Commerce Headless Migration Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: React Native 0.74 app on iOS and Android backed by a Fastify headless commerce API on AWS ECS Fargate, migrating from a Magento monolith over a 6-month parallel run. Supports style collection browsing, cross-session cart persistence, wishlist management, and checkout via Stripe, Apple Pay, and Google Pay with full PCI SAQ A-EP compliance. A dedicated Magento Bridge service isolates legacy XML API translation and handles cart session merge-on-login, ensuring no shopper cart is lost during the cutover period.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-service architecture: React Native client on iOS and Android communicates over TLS 1.3 to a Fastify Commerce API on ECS Fargate behind an ALB with WAF. A separate Magento Bridge Fargate service translates Magento REST v2 calls during the parallel run. CloudFront delivers product images from S3 with 90% cache hit target. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ stores orders, profiles, and wishlists; Redis 7 ElastiCache handles cart sessions with 30-day TTL. PCI SAQ A-EP: Stripe.js and the React Native Stripe SDK tokenize card data entirely on device; only a Stripe payment_intent_id is sent to Fastify; no card data transits or is stored on any server. Cognito handles OAuth 2.0 PKCE for authenticated flows; guest JWTs enable cart persistence before account creation.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, Expo Router, React Query, Zustand, Stripe React Native SDK 0.37, @invertase/react-native-apple-authentication. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 4, Prisma ORM, Stripe Node SDK, pino for structured logging. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF (OWASP ruleset), CloudFront, Route 53, ACM, S3, Cognito user pool, AWS Secrets Manager, SQS for Stripe webhooks. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, ECR. Observability: Datadog APM sidecars, Datadog RUM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Style Collection Browsing: curated grid and editorial layout with infinite scroll and faceted filters by size, color, and price range. Product Detail: high-res image carousel, size selector with real-time inventory count, wishlist toggle with optimistic UI update. Cross-Session Cart: Redis-persisted cart keyed by guest session token, migrated to shopper_id on Cognito sign-in via merge-on-login flow. Checkout Engine: Stripe PaymentIntent with Apple Pay and Google Pay native sheets; PCI-clean server path. Order Tracking: fulfillment state timeline with carrier webhook integration. Returns: shopper-initiated RMA with reason codes. Magento Migration: cart merge-on-login and historical customer data import.
target_output.system_components: ALB with WAF routing to ECS Fargate Commerce API cluster (2-8 tasks, CPU 60% auto-scale); ECS Fargate Magento Bridge (1-3 tasks, SQS queue-depth scaling, decommissioned post-cutover); RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode; S3 product image bucket fronted by CloudFront; Cognito user pool with PKCE configuration; AWS Secrets Manager for Stripe and Magento credentials; SQS queue for Stripe webhook event processing; Datadog APM sidecar containers.
target_output.workflows: Checkout PCI Flow: shopper selects size and taps 'Buy Now'; React Native Stripe SDK calls stripe.createPaymentMethod on device using card form or Apple Pay sheet (card data stays on device and Stripe's servers only); app sends POST /api/v1/checkout/intents with body containing cart_session_key, payment_method_id, and shipping_address; Fastify creates a Stripe PaymentIntent server-to-server using the Stripe secret key from Secrets Manager; Fastify returns client_secret to the app; app calls stripe.confirmPayment(client_secret) to complete 3DS or wallet auth on device; on Stripe success event, app calls POST /api/v1/orders to persist the order; no PAN, CVV, expiry, or card BIN is ever transmitted to or logged by Fastify. Cart Migration on Sign-In: shopper taps sign-in, Cognito PKCE flow completes in React Native web view; app sends GET /api/v1/migration/cart with Authorization: Bearer cognito_token and X-Magento-Session header; Magento Bridge authenticates against Magento REST v2 and fetches the guest cart by legacy session ID; Commerce API loads the current Redis cart by guest session_key; merges Magento cart items where the SKU is not already in the Redis cart (preserving new-app quantities on conflict); writes the unified cart to Redis keyed by shopper_id with a 30-day TTL; returns the merged cart to the app; idempotency enforced via X-Idempotency-Key header to prevent double-merge on network retry.
target_output.data_model: product_listings (id UUID PK, sku VARCHAR UNIQUE, name TEXT, brand_id UUID FK, category_path ltree, price_cents INT, compare_price_cents INT, inventory_qty INT, images_json JSONB, status ENUM active/archived). style_collections (id UUID PK, title TEXT, curator_note TEXT, product_ids UUID[], published_at TIMESTAMPTZ). shopper_profiles (id UUID PK, cognito_sub VARCHAR UNIQUE, email_hash VARCHAR, shipping_addresses_json JSONB, gdpr_erasure_requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). wishlists (id UUID PK, shopper_id UUID FK, sku_id UUID FK, added_at TIMESTAMPTZ, UNIQUE(shopper_id, sku_id)). cart_sessions stored as Redis HASH at key cart:{session_key} with fields items_json, shopper_id (nullable), updated_at, TTL 30 days. checkout_orders (id UUID PK, shopper_id UUID FK, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, status ENUM pending/paid/shipped/returned/cancelled, line_items_json JSONB, shipping_address_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). return_requests (id UUID PK, order_id UUID FK, reason_code VARCHAR, item_skus TEXT[], status ENUM requested/approved/received/refunded, label_url TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1. GET /collections?page=N returns paginated style collections with product preview thumbnails. GET /products/:sku returns product detail with current inventory_qty. GET /cart/:session_key returns cart items with live prices. PUT /cart/:session_key/items body {sku_id, qty} upserts item quantity. DELETE /cart/:session_key/items/:sku removes line item. POST /checkout/intents body {cart_session_key, payment_method_id, shipping_address} creates Stripe PaymentIntent server-side, returns {payment_intent_id, client_secret, amount_cents}; no card data accepted. POST /orders body {payment_intent_id, cart_session_key} confirms order after Stripe client-side confirmation. GET /migration/cart (auth required) triggers Magento cart merge-on-login. GET /orders?shopper_id=X paginated order history. POST /returns creates RMA. All authenticated routes require Cognito JWT Bearer; guest routes accept short-lived session JWT.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI SAQ A-EP controls: React Native Stripe SDK and Stripe.js handle all card data entirely on device and Stripe CDN; Fastify accepts only payment_intent_id on checkout endpoint; Stripe secret key stored in AWS Secrets Manager; no card number, CVV, expiry, or BIN logged anywhere in application logs or database; WAF rule blocks requests with card-number-pattern strings in body. GDPR controls: shopper PII stored as email_hash in PostgreSQL; full email retained in Cognito only; erasure endpoint anonymizes shopper_profiles PII within 30-day SLA using async SQS job; data portability endpoint exports order and wishlist history. Auth: Cognito PKCE with refresh token rotation on every use; guest JWTs expire in 7 days; admin routes require separate Cognito group claim. Certificate pinning in React Native with backup pin; 90-day rotation window with 30-day overlap planned in release calendar.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline per service: ESLint, Prettier check, Jest unit tests, Testcontainers integration tests against real PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7, Docker multi-stage build, ECR push with image digest pinning, ECS rolling deploy with minimum_healthy_percent=100. Environments: dev (single-AZ Fargate, RDS single instance, Magento Bridge pointing to Magento dev instance), staging (multi-AZ mirror with realistic Magento staging data), production (multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c, ECS auto-scaling 2-8 tasks for commerce-api, 1-3 for magento-bridge, RDS Multi-AZ PostgreSQL with read replica for analytics queries, ElastiCache cluster mode). CodeDeploy blue-green for major releases with 10-minute bake time. Secrets Manager rotation: Stripe webhook secret 30-day rotation, Magento OAuth credentials 90-day rotation. LaunchDarkly feature flag controls percentage of mobile sessions routed to new headless API vs Magento storefront (starting at 5% on launch day, ramping to 100% over 6 months).
target_output.observability: Datadog APM sidecar on both ECS services tracing request flows including Magento Bridge calls. Datadog RUM SDK in React Native tracking checkout funnel: collection_view, product_view, add_to_cart, checkout_start, payment_intent_created, stripe_confirm_called, order_confirmed. Custom Datadog metrics: payment_intent_failure_rate (PagerDuty alert >2%), cart_migration_merge_success_rate (alert <95%), magento_bridge_latency_p99 (alert >500ms), cart_abandonment_rate by collection. CloudWatch alarms on RDS connection utilization >80% and Redis eviction rate >0.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2400/month post-launch: commerce-api Fargate $800 (4 tasks average), magento-bridge $200 (1-2 tasks during 6-month parallel run, decommissioned at cutover saving $200/month), RDS Multi-AZ PostgreSQL $500, ElastiCache Redis $150, CloudFront $200, S3 $50, Cognito $100, misc $400. Auto-scales to ~$3000/month at 10x traffic via ECS target tracking. CloudFront caching at 90% hit ratio for product images minimizes origin bandwidth. Reserved Fargate baseline (2 tasks) reduces compute cost 30% vs on-demand.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Fastify Commerce API with product catalog, cart with Redis sessions, Cognito PKCE auth, Stripe checkout with documented PCI SAQ A-EP flow, Magento Bridge with cart merge-on-login, CI/CD with Testcontainers; React Native app with collection browsing, cart, and Stripe checkout. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Wishlists, Apple Pay and Google Pay native sheets, order history, Datadog RUM checkout funnel instrumentation. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Returns RMA workflow, GDPR erasure and data portability endpoints, LaunchDarkly traffic ramp tooling. Phase 4 (post-launch 6 months): Magento traffic migration 5% to 100% via feature flag; Magento Bridge decommission at full cutover.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout React Native and Fastify services. Fastify schema-based request validation with ajv enforced on all endpoints; no card-related fields permitted in any request schema. Jest for unit tests; Testcontainers for integration tests against real PostgreSQL and Redis. PR gate requires lint, tests, and 80% coverage on Commerce API route handlers. ADR required for any change to the checkout or PCI data flow. pino structured JSON logging with correlation IDs; no console.log in any environment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Magento Bridge becomes a long-term dependency if traffic cutover stalls. Mitigation: LaunchDarkly dashboard with traffic percentage control; contractual 6-month decommission timeline; Bridge has its own health dashboard and SLA. Risk: React Native Stripe SDK update breaks Apple Pay flow requiring app store re-submission. Mitigation: lock Stripe SDK to minor version in package.json; test Apple Pay in staging before each update. Tradeoff: Redis cart sessions over PostgreSQL for performance at checkout scale; cart data is low-durability (shopper re-adds) vs orders which use RDS Multi-AZ. Risk: GDPR erasure 30-day SLA at high request volume. Mitigation: async SQS-based erasure job, not synchronous API response.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Stripe chosen over Adyen for SAQ A-EP scope simplicity at current transaction volume. Apple Pay domain verification file served from CloudFront. Guest cart TTL set to 30 days in Redis to align with GDPR session retention. Magento Bridge will use Magento REST v2 with OAuth 1.0a credentials in Secrets Manager. LaunchDarkly chosen for feature flags to support granular traffic percentage control during migration ramp.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for fashion retail mobile commerce. Resolves critical PCI data flow gap (ISS-002) and Magento cart merge-on-login (ISS-001).

project_goal.value: High-throughput product catalog API serving millions of SKUs for a multi-brand wholesale marketplace with real-time inventory signals, bulk price updates, and faceted partner search
project_goal.rationale: Core data service powering wholesale commerce for retail partner integrations

target_users.value: Wholesale brand catalog managers uploading and maintaining SKU data; retail partner systems querying catalog with faceted filters and inventory availability
target_users.rationale: Dual audience: ingest side (brands) and consumption side (partners)

project_class.rationale: Headless API service with no frontend; consumed by brand portals and partner integrations

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: API-first with bulk ingestion batch jobs and partner-facing REST endpoints

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of SKUs, bulk 500k-record ingestion, sub-100ms faceted search at scale

risk_level.rationale: Internal catalog data; partner access is contractually bounded; no cardholder data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Wholesale pricing and catalog data is confidential but not personal or financial in the regulatory sense

external_exposure.rationale: API accessed by vetted retail partners under contractual data usage agreements; not open to anonymous internet traffic

access_model.value: Custom JWT with service account credentials per partner; brand catalog managers use API keys; all requests require valid token with partner_id or brand_id claim
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service integration pattern; no human SSO required

feature_scope.value: Brand catalog ingestion via CSV/SFTP, bulk SKU upsert API, faceted search with inventory confidence scores, price tier resolution per partner, completion webhooks, attribute schema management
feature_scope.rationale: Core ingest-and-serve data platform features

mvp_scope.value: Bulk CSV ingestion, Elasticsearch-backed faceted search, partner API key auth, price tier resolution, completion webhook delivery
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for first 5 brand and 10 partner onboardings

security_baseline.value: mTLS for partner API calls, JWT service accounts with 1-hour expiry, API key hashing at rest, rate limiting per partner_id, all data in transit via TLS 1.3
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing exposure requires mTLS per contract and JWT rotation for service accounts

frontend_stack.value: none - API service only
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI layer; brands use SFTP feeds or REST; partners consume REST API

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin on GKE Autopilot; batch ingestion workers as separate Go services
backend_stack.rationale: Go chosen for throughput on high-volume SKU processing and low-latency search routing

data_platform.value: Elasticsearch 8 for faceted search index; PostgreSQL 16 for canonical SKU and partner data; Redis 7 for price tier cache and rate limiting counters
data_platform.rationale: Dual-store pattern: Elasticsearch for search, Postgres for source of truth

privacy_retention_policy.value: Catalog data retained indefinitely as product master; superseded price tiers archived for 2 years; bulk import job logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No personal data; catalog master data is perpetual business record

project.goal: High-throughput wholesale catalog API with bulk ingestion and partner faceted search
project.target_users: Brand catalog managers and retail partner systems
project.timeline: Q2 delivery for first brand and partner cohort

backend.framework: Gin for REST API; custom batch worker for ingestion

security.auth: Custom JWT with service accounts; API keys for brand ingestion; mTLS for partner API calls
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for all connections; mTLS enforced at Kubernetes ingress for partner routes

data.db: Elasticsearch 8 for search index, PostgreSQL 16 for canonical data
data.cache: Redis 7 for price tier resolution and rate limiting counters

reasoner_reviews.product: Four rounds of iteration have converged on a stable design. The dual-store pattern (Elasticsearch for search, PostgreSQL as canonical source) is well-established and the price tier resolution logic is now correctly isolated in the Redis cache layer. The remaining concern is the reindexing zero-downtime strategy for Elasticsearch alias rotation, which previous rounds have noted but not fully resolved.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Go batch worker with Kafka-backed ingestion queue is the right choice for processing 500k SKU CSV files. The alias-based Elasticsearch reindexing (write to new index, alias-flip) must be explicitly detailed in the plan since it is the main operational risk for zero-downtime catalog refreshes. The current plan references it but does not detail the alias flip procedure or rollback.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS enforcement at the Kubernetes ingress for partner routes is correctly scoped. The JWT service account 1-hour expiry with rotation is appropriate. API keys for brand ingest should be stored as HMAC-SHA256 hashes in PostgreSQL, never in plaintext. The rate limiting per partner_id in Redis must prevent a single partner from saturating the Elasticsearch cluster.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: p95 <100ms search latency is achievable with proper Elasticsearch shard configuration and Redis price tier caching. The 500k SKU ingestion within 4 hours requires the batch worker to sustain ~35 records/second including validation, normalization, and dual-write to Postgres and Elasticsearch. Previous audit confirmed this is feasible with the current worker concurrency model.

backend.service_design: Catalog API service (Go/Gin, handles search, SKU reads, partner auth) and Ingestion Worker service (Go, processes bulk feed jobs from Cloud Storage via Pub/Sub notifications, writes to PostgreSQL and Elasticsearch)
backend.api_patterns: REST at /v1; GET /v1/search with query params category, brand_id, price_min, price_max, in_stock, page, limit returns faceted results with inventory confidence scores; GET /v1/skus/:id; POST /v1/ingestion/jobs for bulk import trigger; GET /v1/ingestion/jobs/:job_id for status polling
backend.business_modules: SearchModule (Elasticsearch query builder with partner price tier injection), IngestionModule (CSV validation, normalization, dual-write orchestration), PriceTierModule (Redis-cached partner-specific price resolution), WebhookModule (completion notification delivery with retry), PartnerAuthModule (JWT validation, mTLS verification, rate limit enforcement)
backend.scaling_notes: GKE Autopilot horizontal pod autoscaling on CPU 70% for Catalog API; Ingestion Worker scales on Pub/Sub undelivered message count; Elasticsearch 8 cluster on 6 data nodes with 3 primary shards per index and 1 replica; shard allocation awareness across GKE node pools

security.auth_design: Partner API calls require mTLS client certificate plus JWT signed by internal CA; brand ingestion uses HMAC-SHA256 hashed API keys stored in PostgreSQL api_keys table; JWT service accounts rotate every 1 hour via internal token endpoint
security.authorization_model: JWT claims include partner_id or brand_id; Catalog API enforces row-level access: partners see only SKUs in their contracted brand catalog; brands can only write to their own brand_catalog records
security.secrets_management: Internal CA certificate stored in GCP Secret Manager; API key generation uses crypto/rand in Go with HMAC-SHA256 storage; no plaintext secrets in Kubernetes manifests; Workload Identity for GKE pods to access GCP services
security.abuse_prevention: Redis sliding window rate limiter per partner_id: 1000 search requests/minute; ingestion jobs throttled to 1 concurrent job per brand; Pub/Sub dead-letter topic for failed ingestion messages after 5 retries

data.entities: brand_catalogs (id UUID, brand_name, contact_email, api_key_hash, created_at), sku_variants (id UUID, brand_catalog_id FK, sku_code VARCHAR UNIQUE, name, category_path, attributes_json JSONB, status ENUM active/discontinued), inventory_positions (id UUID, sku_id FK, warehouse_id, qty_available, qty_reserved, confidence_score FLOAT, updated_at), price_tiers (id UUID, sku_id FK, partner_id FK, price_cents INT, effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE), attribute_schemas (id UUID, category_path, schema_json JSONB, version INT), bulk_import_jobs (id UUID, brand_id FK, source_gcs_path, status ENUM queued/processing/completed/failed, records_processed INT, errors_json JSONB, started_at, completed_at), search_indices (index_name, alias_name, status ENUM building/active/archived, sku_count, created_at), partner_api_keys (id UUID, partner_id FK, key_hash VARCHAR, scopes TEXT[], expires_at, last_used_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL as canonical store for all entities; Elasticsearch 8 index with per-category custom analyzers for text search; Redis 7 for price tier cache (partner_id:sku_id keys, 5-minute TTL) and rate limiting counters
data.consistency_notes: Dual-write on ingestion: PostgreSQL write first (source of truth), then Elasticsearch index; on PostgreSQL failure, Pub/Sub message nacked for retry; Elasticsearch write failures are non-blocking (async) with eventual consistency via nightly full reindex job
data.migration_notes: Elasticsearch alias rotation: new index built in background, alias atomically flipped on completion, old index deleted after 24-hour grace period; zero downtime maintained via read alias pointing to active index throughout

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster with two deployments: catalog-api (3-10 pods, CPU HPA) and ingestion-worker (1-5 pods, Pub/Sub HPA); Cloud Load Balancing with partner mTLS termination at ingress-nginx
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build pipeline: go vet, staticcheck, unit tests, integration tests against Testcontainers Elasticsearch and PostgreSQL, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy; canary deployment via Argo Rollouts for search API changes (10% traffic for 30 minutes before full cutover)
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus operator on GKE scraping custom metrics from catalog-api and ingestion-worker; Grafana dashboards: search latency p50/p95/p99 by partner, ingestion throughput records/sec, Elasticsearch cluster health, webhook delivery success rate; Alertmanager rules for p95 latency >100ms and ingestion backlog >10k records
devops.cost_controls: GKE Autopilot pay-per-pod; Committed Use Discounts for Elasticsearch data nodes; Cloud Storage Nearline for archived bulk import logs >90 days; Redis instance sized at m1.medium with eviction policy allkeys-lru

ISS-001.title: Elasticsearch alias rotation procedure undefined
ISS-001.detail: Across 3 prior rounds, the plan has referenced zero-downtime reindexing via alias rotation but never detailed the specific alias flip sequence, failure handling, or rollback procedure. Without this, operators cannot safely perform catalog refreshes.

Round 2 plan added alias mention but no operational detail
Round 3 plan still lacks alias flip sequence and rollback

ISS-002.title: Price tier cache invalidation on bulk update not addressed
ISS-002.detail: When a brand submits a bulk price update affecting thousands of SKUs, the Redis price tier cache (partner_id:sku_id keys) must be invalidated or the stale prices will be served to partners for up to 5 minutes. No invalidation strategy has been specified.

history.note: Raised in round 2 audit; round 3 plan did not address it
Still absent in round 3 plan

previous_plan.title: Wholesale Catalog API Architecture v3
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22/Gin API on GKE Autopilot with Elasticsearch 8 for faceted search and PostgreSQL 16 as canonical catalog store. Bulk ingestion via CSV/SFTP with Pub/Sub-triggered Go worker. Partner access via mTLS + JWT service accounts.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Catalog API and Ingestion Worker as separate GKE deployments. Dual-store: PostgreSQL canonical, Elasticsearch search index. Redis for price tier cache. mTLS at ingress for partner routes. Alias-based reindexing mentioned but not operationalized.
previous_plan.data_model: brand_catalogs, sku_variants, inventory_positions, price_tiers, attribute_schemas, bulk_import_jobs, search_indices, partner_api_keys in PostgreSQL; SKU documents in Elasticsearch.
previous_plan.observability: Prometheus metrics from GKE pods; Grafana dashboards for search latency and ingestion throughput; Alertmanager for SLA breach.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 must resolve two persistent issues: ISS-001 (Elasticsearch alias rotation procedure, 3 rounds outstanding) and ISS-002 (Redis price tier cache invalidation on bulk price update, 2 rounds). The alias rotation fix requires detailing the full lifecycle: create new index, bulk reindex from PostgreSQL, atomic alias ADD+REMOVE, count verification, 24-hour grace period before old index deletion, and rollback to old alias on failure. The cache invalidation fix requires a Lua script approach for atomic UNLINK of affected partner_id:sku_id keys triggered on ingestion job completion. Everything else in the round 3 plan is sound.

fix_report.action_taken: Added detailed Elasticsearch alias rotation procedure to deployment_and_operations and workflows: (1) create new versioned index with updated mappings, (2) trigger _reindex API call from active index, (3) verify document count matches source, (4) atomic POST /_aliases with ADD new_index and REMOVE old_index actions in single request, (5) verify search responses via health check, (6) schedule old index deletion after 24-hour grace period; rollback procedure: if step 4 fails or health check fails within 5 minutes, atomic alias REMOVE new_index ADD old_index restores prior state.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Operators can execute catalog reindex with zero downtime; rollback procedure documented and tested in staging; ISS-001 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Added Redis price tier cache invalidation strategy: on bulk_import_job completion for jobs containing price_update record types, Ingestion Worker executes a Lua script that iterates affected sku_ids and issues Redis UNLINK commands for all partner_id:sku_id keys in a single pipeline; for jobs affecting >10k SKUs, keys are invalidated in batches of 1000 to avoid blocking the Redis event loop; invalidation logged to Cloud Logging for audit.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Partners receive updated prices within one Redis TTL cycle after bulk price update; no stale price tier served beyond the next cache miss; ISS-002 resolved

target_output.title: Wholesale Product Catalog API Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Go 1.22 with Gin on GKE Autopilot serving millions of SKUs for a multi-brand wholesale marketplace. Dual-store design: PostgreSQL 16 as the canonical catalog record, Elasticsearch 8 as the faceted search index. Bulk brand ingestion via CSV/SFTP feeds processed by a Go Ingestion Worker triggered via Cloud Pub/Sub. Retail partners access the catalog via mTLS-authenticated REST endpoints with per-partner price tier resolution from Redis. This revision fully operationalizes the Elasticsearch alias rotation procedure for zero-downtime reindexing and specifies atomic Redis cache invalidation on bulk price updates.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two GKE Autopilot deployments: Catalog API (Go/Gin, 3-10 pods HPA on CPU 70%) serving partner search and SKU read requests, and Ingestion Worker (Go, 1-5 pods HPA on Pub/Sub message count) processing brand feed files. Cloud Storage staging bucket receives SFTP uploads; Pub/Sub ObjectFinalize notification triggers Ingestion Worker jobs. PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL is the canonical store; Elasticsearch 8 is the search-optimized read model. Redis 7 caches partner-specific price tier resolutions with 5-minute TTL and is invalidated atomically on bulk price updates. Cloud Load Balancing with ingress-nginx handles mTLS termination for partner routes; internal routes use JWT service account tokens. Argo Rollouts manages canary deployments for search API changes.
target_output.technology_stack: API: Go 1.22, Gin 1.9, olivere/elastic v8 client, lib/pq PostgreSQL driver, go-redis v9. Ingestion: Go 1.22 worker, cloud.google.com/go/pubsub, encoding/csv, custom schema validator. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16, Elasticsearch 8 on GCE (6 data nodes, 3 masters), Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore, Cloud Storage, Cloud Load Balancing, ingress-nginx with mTLS. CI/CD: Cloud Build, Artifact Registry, Argo Rollouts. Observability: Prometheus operator, Grafana, Alertmanager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Brand Catalog Ingestion: SFTP upload or direct CSV POST triggers Pub/Sub job; Ingestion Worker validates headers against attribute_schema, normalizes category paths, dual-writes to PostgreSQL and Elasticsearch, delivers completion webhook. Faceted Partner Search: partners query /v1/search with category, brand, price range, and in_stock filters; Elasticsearch returns ranked results enriched with partner-specific prices from Redis. SKU Detail: GET /v1/skus/:id returns full attribute set with current inventory_positions confidence score. Price Tier Management: brands submit price tier matrices per partner segment; Redis caches resolved prices per partner_id:sku_id. Attribute Schema Registry: brands register custom attribute schemas per category; validation enforced during ingestion. Ingestion Job Monitoring: brands poll job status; webhook delivered on completion or failure.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Load Balancer with ingress-nginx (mTLS termination for /v1/* partner routes), GKE Autopilot Catalog API deployment (3-10 pods, Go/Gin), GKE Autopilot Ingestion Worker deployment (1-5 pods, Pub/Sub consumer), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (canonical store, read replica for analytics), Elasticsearch 8 cluster (6 data nodes on n2-standard-4, 3 master nodes, index alias management), Redis 7 Cloud Memorystore (price tier cache and rate limiting counters), Cloud Storage bucket (SFTP staging), Pub/Sub topic for ingestion job events with ObjectFinalize trigger, Pub/Sub dead-letter topic for failed jobs, GCP Secret Manager for CA cert and service credentials.
target_output.workflows: Bulk Brand Ingestion: brand uploads CSV to Cloud Storage via SFTP or POST /v1/ingestion/jobs; Pub/Sub ObjectFinalize event triggers Ingestion Worker; worker validates each row against attribute_schema for the row category_path; validates required fields and data types; upserts sku_variants and inventory_positions to PostgreSQL in batches of 500; for rows with price_update type, also upserts price_tiers; publishes Elasticsearch bulk index request for each batch; on completion, updates bulk_import_job status, delivers webhook to brand endpoint. Elasticsearch Alias Rotation (Zero-Downtime Reindex): operator triggers reindex job via admin CLI; (1) create new versioned index catalog_v{N+1} with updated mappings; (2) POST /_reindex from catalog_v{N} to catalog_v{N+1} with reindex API; (3) verify document count: catalog_v{N+1}.count must equal catalog_v{N}.count within 0.01% tolerance; (4) atomic alias update: POST /_aliases body {actions:[{add:{index:catalog_v{N+1},alias:catalog_active}},{remove:{index:catalog_v{N},alias:catalog_active}}]}; (5) run search health check against catalog_active alias; (6) if health check passes, schedule catalog_v{N} deletion after 24 hours; (7) rollback: if step 4 fails or health check fails within 5 minutes, execute {actions:[{remove:{index:catalog_v{N+1},alias:catalog_active}},{add:{index:catalog_v{N},alias:catalog_active}}]} to restore prior index. Faceted Partner Search: partner sends GET /v1/search?category=footwear&brand_id=uuid&in_stock=true&page=1; Catalog API validates JWT and extracts partner_id; builds Elasticsearch bool query with term filters on category_path and brand_id, filter on inventory_positions.qty_available>0; for each result SKU, resolves price from Redis key partner_id:sku_id (cache hit) or PostgreSQL price_tiers (cache miss, populate Redis); returns paginated JSON with inventory confidence scores.
target_output.data_model: brand_catalogs (id UUID PK, brand_name TEXT, contact_email TEXT, api_key_hash VARCHAR, webhook_url TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). sku_variants (id UUID PK, brand_catalog_id UUID FK, sku_code VARCHAR UNIQUE, name TEXT, category_path ltree, attributes_json JSONB, status ENUM active/discontinued, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). inventory_positions (id UUID PK, sku_id UUID FK, warehouse_id TEXT, qty_available INT, qty_reserved INT, confidence_score FLOAT CHECK 0-1, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). price_tiers (id UUID PK, sku_id UUID FK, partner_id UUID FK, price_cents INT, effective_from DATE, effective_to DATE, UNIQUE(sku_id, partner_id, effective_from)). attribute_schemas (id UUID PK, category_path ltree, schema_json JSONB, version INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). bulk_import_jobs (id UUID PK, brand_id UUID FK, source_gcs_path TEXT, status ENUM queued/processing/completed/failed, records_processed INT, errors_json JSONB, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). search_indices (index_name TEXT PK, alias_name TEXT, status ENUM building/active/archived, sku_count INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). partner_api_keys (id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, key_hash VARCHAR, scopes TEXT[], expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_used_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Redis keys: price tier cache at partner_id:sku_id (string, 5-min TTL); rate limit counter at ratelimit:partner_id:minute (integer, 60s TTL).
target_output.api_design: REST at /v1. GET /v1/search?category=&brand_id=&price_min=&price_max=&in_stock=&page=&limit= returns {results:[{sku_id, sku_code, name, price_cents, inventory_confidence, attributes}], total, page}. GET /v1/skus/:id returns full SKU with all attributes and current inventory. POST /v1/ingestion/jobs body {brand_id, source_gcs_path, job_type: full_replace|incremental|price_update} creates ingestion job, returns {job_id, status:queued}. GET /v1/ingestion/jobs/:job_id returns {job_id, status, records_processed, errors_json, completed_at}. POST /v1/price-tiers/bulk body {brand_id, partner_id, tiers:[{sku_code, price_cents, effective_from, effective_to}]} batch upserts price tiers and triggers Redis invalidation. GET /v1/attribute-schemas/:category returns schema JSON for validation. All partner routes require mTLS client cert and JWT Bearer; brand routes require HMAC API key header X-Brand-API-Key.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS at ingress-nginx: partner routes require valid client certificate signed by internal CA stored in GCP Secret Manager; ingress controller validates cert chain on every request. JWT service accounts: signed by internal ECDSA key with 1-hour expiry; partner_id and allowed scopes encoded as claims; token endpoint rotates signing key every 30 days. API key security: brand API keys generated with Go crypto/rand 32 bytes; stored as HMAC-SHA256 hash in partner_api_keys table; never logged in plaintext. Rate limiting: Redis sliding window counter per partner_id at 1000 search requests/minute; Ingestion Worker allows 1 concurrent job per brand_id via Redis distributed lock. Audit logging: all ingestion job events and price tier changes written to Cloud Logging with structured JSON for SOC 2 evidence.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Build pipeline: go vet, staticcheck linting, unit tests with testify, integration tests via Testcontainers Elasticsearch 8 and PostgreSQL 16, Docker multi-stage build, Artifact Registry push with SHA256 digest pinning, GKE rolling deploy via kubectl set image. Argo Rollouts canary for Catalog API: 10% traffic for 30 minutes with p95 latency SLO gate before full promotion. Environments: dev (GKE Autopilot single-region, Cloud SQL dev instance, single Elasticsearch node), staging (production-mirror with anonymized catalog data snapshot), production (GKE Autopilot us-central1, Cloud SQL HA, Elasticsearch 6-data-node cluster). Elasticsearch alias rotation procedure: documented in runbook and enforced via admin CLI tool that executes the 7-step procedure with automated health checks and rollback on failure. Ingestion job monitoring: Pub/Sub dead-letter topic alerts on message age >1 hour via Alertmanager.
target_output.observability: Prometheus operator on GKE scraping metrics from catalog-api and ingestion-worker pods. Grafana dashboards: search latency p50/p95/p99 per partner_id, ingestion throughput records/sec, Elasticsearch cluster health (green/yellow/red), Elasticsearch index alias status, webhook delivery success rate. Custom Go metrics: catalog_search_latency_seconds histogram by partner, ingestion_records_processed_total counter, price_cache_hit_ratio gauge, alias_rotation_duration_seconds. Alertmanager rules: p95 search latency >100ms (page on-call), ingestion Pub/Sub backlog >10k messages (warn), Elasticsearch cluster status yellow (warn), cluster status red (page).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot pay-per-pod: estimated $1800/month for baseline 3 catalog-api pods and 1 ingestion-worker pod. Elasticsearch cluster $2400/month for 6 n2-standard-4 data nodes (Committed Use Discount reduces 30%). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL $600/month HA. Redis Cloud Memorystore $200/month. Cloud Storage $50/month. Total baseline ~$5100/month scaling to ~$8000/month at 10x search traffic via HPA. Ingestion burst is bounded by 4-hour window constraint and does not require permanent additional capacity.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 weeks): Catalog API with search, SKU read, JWT auth, mTLS; PostgreSQL schema; Elasticsearch index with alias management; Cloud Build CI/CD; first brand ingestion via CSV POST. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Ingestion Worker with Pub/Sub trigger, SFTP support, dual-write, completion webhooks; price tier management with Redis cache; ISS-002 Redis invalidation on price updates. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Partner faceted search with price tier injection; rate limiting; attribute schema registry; Argo Rollouts canary. Phase 4 (2 weeks): Elasticsearch alias rotation runbook operationalized with admin CLI; full load test at 500k SKU ingestion; staging sign-off.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with explicit version pinning; golangci-lint in Cloud Build with errcheck, staticcheck, and govet enabled. All Elasticsearch queries must go through the SearchModule query builder; no raw query strings inline in handlers. Integration tests use Testcontainers for real Elasticsearch and PostgreSQL; no mocking of storage layer in integration tests. Alias rotation procedure changes require a new runbook version and staging rehearsal before production. Price tier cache invalidation Lua scripts stored in the repo under scripts/redis/ and tested against Redis 7 in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Elasticsearch reindex duration exceeds maintenance window for very large catalogs (>10M SKUs). Mitigation: throttle reindex API with requests_per_second parameter; monitor reindex progress via _tasks API; alias remains on old index throughout so search is unaffected. Risk: Redis price tier cache becomes a hotspot for a brand with thousands of partners. Mitigation: shard Redis key space by brand_id prefix; monitor keyspace hit rate per brand. Tradeoff: dual-write consistency (Postgres first, Elasticsearch async) means search index may lag by seconds on high ingestion load; acceptable for catalog data where near-real-time is sufficient. Risk: Pub/Sub dead-letter overflow if Ingestion Worker is down for extended period. Mitigation: Alertmanager page at 10k undelivered messages; Ingestion Worker is stateless and restarts automatically in GKE.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Elasticsearch chosen over OpenSearch for Elastic's vector search roadmap alignment with future ML enrichment feature. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL chosen over AlloyDB due to team familiarity and simpler replication model at current scale. SFTP ingestion uses Cloud Storage SFTP endpoint (Preview) rather than a custom SFTP server. Pub/Sub chosen over Kafka for GCP-native managed throughput without cluster operations overhead.

metadata.notes: Round 4 for wholesale catalog API. Resolves persistent ISS-001 (Elasticsearch alias rotation) and ISS-002 (Redis price tier cache invalidation on bulk updates).

reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Typer with SQLite is the right choice for this CLI. The main gap in round 1 was the missing error output format contract: operators integrating feedctl into CI pipelines need predictable exit codes and machine-readable error structures. The round 1 plan also did not specify how validation rules are sourced for offline validation - they must be cached locally from the aggregator.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key stored in a TOML config with 0600 permissions is standard and appropriate. The CLI should never echo the API key to stdout or include it in verbose logs. httpx should be configured with a fixed CA bundle rather than relying on system trust stores to prevent MITM on misconfigured operator machines.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The offline validation requirement means channel schema rules must be cached locally after a feedctl channel sync command. The round 1 plan omitted this sync step, leaving the validation command unable to function offline without pre-fetched rules.
reasoner_reviews.product: The feedctl CLI's core value is operator productivity: bulk feed operations that previously required manual portal clicks should run in under 10 seconds from the terminal. The channel sync and validate subcommands are the highest-frequency commands and must have clear, parseable output for shell pipeline integration. Error messages must include the channel name and feed ID to allow operators to correlate failures with specific marketplace integrations.

backend.service_design: Single Python package feedctl with command modules: push.py, validate.py, diff.py, log.py, channel.py, config.py; shared db.py for SQLite access and api.py for aggregator HTTP client
backend.api_patterns: aggregator REST API calls via httpx with 30-second timeout and 3 retries with exponential backoff; API key passed as X-API-Key header; responses parsed into Pydantic models
backend.business_modules: FeedValidator (loads channel_configs schema, validates CSV rows against rules, emits ValidationError list), FeedDiffer (loads previous sku_snapshot for channel, computes added/removed/changed rows), FeedPusher (validates first, computes diff, submits only changed records to POST /v1/feeds/:channel/records, logs result to push_logs), ChannelSync (fetches validation_rules from aggregator, caches in channel_configs)
backend.failure_handling: Non-zero exit codes on validation failure (exit 1) and network error (exit 2) and auth failure (exit 3); --json flag emits machine-readable error array for CI pipelines

security.auth_design: API key loaded from ~/.feedctl/config.toml; config file created with 0600 permissions by feedctl init; key never printed to stdout or included in --verbose log output; key masked as **** in any debug traces
security.secrets_management: feedctl init creates config directory with os.makedirs(mode=0o700); config file written with os.open(path, O_WRONLY|O_CREAT, 0o600); no environment variable fallback to prevent accidental CI log exposure

data.retention_and_deletion: sku_snapshots older than 90 days pruned automatically at start of each push command; push_logs retained until operator runs feedctl log purge --older-than N days

ISS-001.title: Offline validation requires channel schema cache not specified
ISS-001.detail: The round 1 plan documented feedctl validate as working offline but did not specify how channel validation schemas are fetched and cached locally. Without a feedctl channel sync command or equivalent, offline validation has no rules to evaluate against.

history.note: Offline validation requirement not met by round 1 design

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 resolves two issues: ISS-001 requires adding a feedctl channel sync command that fetches validation rules from the aggregator API and caches them in the channel_configs and validation_rules SQLite tables, enabling fully offline validate and diff. ISS-002 requires documenting a clear exit code contract so CI pipelines can distinguish failure types. Plan quality is weak per the manifest - sections are present but at minimum depth, reflecting a routine CLI tool without high architectural complexity.

fix_report.action_taken: Added feedctl channel sync command to CLI design and workflows: fetches validation rules JSON from GET /v1/channels/:channel/schema on the aggregator API and upserts into validation_rules SQLite table; channel_configs.last_synced_at updated; validate command checks last_synced_at and warns if rules older than 7 days.
fix_report.expected_outcome: feedctl validate and feedctl diff work fully offline after at least one feedctl channel sync has been run; ISS-001 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Documented exit code contract in development_guidelines and api_design: 0=success, 1=validation_failure (rows failed schema rules), 2=network_error (aggregator API unreachable), 3=auth_failure (invalid or expired API key), 4=config_error (missing or malformed config.toml); --json flag outputs machine-readable error array with rule_code, row_number, and message fields.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CI pipelines can reliably branch on exit code; feedctl usable in automated feed publishing scripts; ISS-002 resolved

target_output.title: Multi-Channel Feed Management CLI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Python 3.12 Typer-based CLI tool for e-commerce operators managing product feeds across Amazon, eBay, and Google Shopping channels. Operators push, validate, and diff feed CSV files against the aggregator REST API from a terminal. Local SQLite stores channel schemas, SKU snapshots, and push logs. After feedctl channel sync pre-caches validation rules, validate and diff commands run fully offline.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Python package feedctl installed via pip or pipx. Command routing via Typer with sub-command groups: feed (push, validate, diff), channel (sync, list), log (show, purge). Shared modules: db.py (SQLite connection manager), api.py (httpx client with API key injection and retry), config.py (TOML config loader with 0600 permission enforcement). Local SQLite at ~/.feedctl/feedctl.db stores all state. No hosted infrastructure; all processing is local except aggregator API calls during push and channel sync.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Typer 0.12, httpx 0.27, Rich 13 for terminal output, tomllib (stdlib) for config parsing, sqlite3 (stdlib) for local storage, Pydantic 2 for API response models and validation rule schemas, ruff for linting, mypy for type checking, pytest for tests, GitHub Actions for CI and PyPI publish.
target_output.functional_feature_map: feedctl init: creates ~/.feedctl/ with 0700 permissions, writes config.toml with 0600 permissions. feedctl channel sync --channel amazon: fetches schema JSON from aggregator, caches in validation_rules table, updates channel_configs.last_synced_at. feedctl feed validate --channel amazon feed.csv: loads validation rules from SQLite, validates each row, emits error table or JSON array. feedctl feed diff --channel amazon feed.csv: loads last sku_snapshot, computes added/removed/changed counts, prints diff summary. feedctl feed push --channel amazon feed.csv: validate, diff, submit changed records via API, write to push_logs. feedctl log show --channel amazon: displays push history table. feedctl log purge: removes push_logs and snapshots older than N days.
target_output.system_components: Typer CLI router, FeedValidator module (loads channel rules from SQLite, validates row-by-row), FeedDiffer module (compares input CSV against sku_snapshots using sha256 row hashing), FeedPusher module (orchestrates validate then diff then API submit), ChannelSync module (fetches schema from aggregator and upserts validation_rules), SQLite database at ~/.feedctl/feedctl.db, httpx client with API key header and TLS 1.3.
target_output.workflows: Offline Validation Flow: operator runs feedctl feed validate --channel amazon feed.csv; CLI loads validation_rules for channel 'amazon' from SQLite; if last_synced_at older than 7 days, prints stale-rules warning; FeedValidator iterates CSV rows, checks each against rule definitions (required fields, data types, enum values, string lengths); emits Rich table of failures with row number and rule_code, or JSON array with --json flag; exits with code 1 if any row fails, 0 if all pass. Feed Push Flow: operator runs feedctl feed push --channel amazon feed.csv; validate runs first; FeedDiffer loads previous sku_snapshot for channel and computes sha256 hash per row; identifies added, removed, changed rows; submits only changed records to POST /v1/feeds/amazon/records via httpx; writes push result to push_logs; saves new sku_snapshot rows; exits 0 on success, 2 on network error, 3 on 401 response.
target_output.data_model: feed_manifests (id INTEGER PK, channel TEXT, file_path TEXT, record_count INT, sha256_hash TEXT, created_at TEXT). channel_configs (id INTEGER PK, channel TEXT UNIQUE, schema_version TEXT, last_synced_at TEXT). sku_snapshots (id INTEGER PK, manifest_id INT FK, sku_id TEXT, row_hash TEXT, pushed_at TEXT). validation_rules (id INTEGER PK, channel TEXT, rule_code TEXT, field_name TEXT, rule_type TEXT CHECK 'required'/'type'/'enum'/'maxlen', rule_params TEXT, updated_at TEXT). diff_reports (id INTEGER PK, manifest_id INT FK, added_count INT, removed_count INT, changed_count INT, report_json TEXT, created_at TEXT). push_logs (id INTEGER PK, manifest_id INT FK, channel TEXT, status TEXT, records_submitted INT, api_response_json TEXT, pushed_at TEXT).
target_output.api_design: Aggregator REST API calls via httpx. GET /v1/channels/:channel/schema returns validation rules JSON; called by feedctl channel sync; response cached in validation_rules table. POST /v1/feeds/:channel/records body {records:[{sku_id, fields}]} submits changed SKU records; response {job_id, accepted_count, rejected_count} logged to push_logs. GET /v1/feeds/:channel/status/:job_id polls async submission status. API key sent as X-API-Key header on all requests. Exit codes: 0=success, 1=validation_failure, 2=network_error (httpx.NetworkError or timeout), 3=auth_failure (HTTP 401 or 403), 4=config_error (missing config.toml or missing api_key field).
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key stored in ~/.feedctl/config.toml written with 0600 permissions via os.open with O_CREAT and mode 0o600. Config directory created with 0700 permissions. API key never printed to stdout, never included in --verbose log output, never written to push_logs; masked as [redacted] in any debug traces. httpx configured with default CA bundle (certifi); verify=True enforced; no option to disable TLS verification. No sensitive data in SQLite beyond aggregator API responses which contain only catalog data.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No hosted infrastructure. GitHub Actions CI: pytest on Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 matrix, mypy --strict, ruff check, build sdist and wheel via python -m build. PyPI publish triggered on git tag v* via OIDC trusted publisher (no long-lived PyPI token). Operators install via pip install feedctl or pipx install feedctl. feedctl --version reports installed version. Upgrade via pip install --upgrade feedctl.
target_output.observability: Rich library for terminal output: progress bars during push and channel sync, colored error/warning/success indicators, table output for validation errors and push logs. --verbose flag logs httpx request/response details (with API key redacted). --json flag outputs all command results as machine-readable JSON for CI pipeline consumption. Exit codes follow POSIX convention for reliable CI branching.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost; local execution only. SQLite performance is sufficient for catalogs up to 1M rows; diff computation using sha256 row hashing handles 100k-row CSVs in under 10 seconds on modern hardware. PyPI distribution via GitHub Actions OIDC has no incremental cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (2 weeks): feedctl init, config management, feedctl channel sync, feedctl feed validate with offline rules; PyPI test publish. Phase 2 (2 weeks): feedctl feed diff with sha256 snapshot comparison, feedctl feed push, feedctl log show and purge; exit code contract; PyPI production publish. Phase 3 (future): CI mode flag for non-interactive use in GitHub Actions, multi-brand workspace support.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type hints throughout; mypy --strict in CI. ruff for linting and formatting. Typer app with explicit callback for version flag. All SQLite access through db.py context manager; no inline SQL in command modules. pytest with tmp_path fixture for SQLite tests; no network calls in unit tests (httpx mocked via respx). Exit code contract: 0=success, 1=validation_failure, 2=network_error, 3=auth_failure, 4=config_error; enforced via typer.Exit(code=N) in all error paths.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: channel validation rules drift if operator forgets to run feedctl channel sync. Mitigation: validate command warns when last_synced_at is older than 7 days. Tradeoff: SQLite over a dedicated local database for simplicity; acceptable for single-user CLI tool. Risk: Windows path handling for ~/.feedctl differs from Unix; Mitigation: use pathlib.Path.home() throughout, test in GitHub Actions Windows runner.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Typer chosen over Click directly for cleaner type-hint-based CLI definition. httpx chosen over requests for async support in future CI mode. SQLite chosen over local JSON files for structured querying of push history. PyPI distribution via OIDC trusted publisher avoids long-lived token management.

metadata.notes: Round 2 for feed management CLI. Weak quality per manifest - functional but thin. Resolves ISS-001 (offline validation schema cache) and ISS-002 (exit code contract).
