project.goal: GDPR deletion request CLI with audit trail for compliance officers
project.target_users: Compliance officers on internal EC2 bastion

backend.storage: PostgreSQL 16 via psycopg3 with IAM auth token

security.auth: IAM role on EC2 bastion; PostgreSQL IAM authentication
security.signing: AWS KMS for PDF export signing

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 RDS with IAM authentication from bastion
data.tables: consent_records, data_subject_requests, ad_platform_connectors, deletion_confirmations, audit_trail_entries, regulatory_export_jobs

reasoner_reviews.product: The GDPR deletion workflow is well-scoped for MVP with Google and Meta connectors. The 30-day Article 17 SLA requires tracking deletion request creation dates and surfacing overdue requests. The plan should include a gdpr status command that lists pending deletion requests and highlights those approaching or past the 30-day window.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Click on EC2 bastion with PostgreSQL RDS is straightforward. The fan-out deletion pattern (one request to multiple ad platforms) should be sequential with per-platform error handling rather than concurrent, to simplify the audit trail. The KMS signing workflow for PDF exports needs to be detailed with the actual signing API call sequence.
reasoner_reviews.security: IAM role authentication for RDS is the correct choice - no stored credentials on the bastion. Email hashes must be SHA-256 of lowercase normalized email to ensure consistent matching against ad platform lookups. The deletion confirmation tokens from ad platforms (Google's customer_list_id erasure job, Meta's deletion_request_id) must be stored verbatim for regulatory evidence.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GDPR Article 17 requires deletion completion within 30 days of request receipt; the CLI audit trail must timestamp each deletion request creation and completion to demonstrate compliance. The bastion EC2 instance has no outbound internet access except to approved ad-platform API endpoints, constraining the connector implementation to allowlisted HTTPS destinations only. Single-operator usage model means no concurrent deletion jobs — sequential fan-out is acceptable.

backend.service_design: Single Python package gdprcli with Click command groups: gdpr (delete, status, audit), consent (list, import), export (generate, list)
backend.api_patterns: Sequential fan-out to ad platform APIs: Google Ads Customer Match Deletion API, Meta Marketing API /deletion_request endpoint, Pinterest Ads audience deletion; each call returns a platform-specific confirmation token stored in deletion_confirmations
backend.business_modules: DeletionOrchestrator (fans out to configured ad_platform_connectors, records each confirmation), ConsentAuditor (queries consent_records and reports gaps), ExportGenerator (builds PDF from audit_trail_entries, signs with KMS), SLATracker (flags data_subject_requests approaching 30-day limit)
backend.failure_handling: Per-platform failures logged to audit_trail_entries with error detail; failed platforms retried on next run with idempotency key matching original request ID; successful platforms not re-submitted

security.auth_design: IAM role on EC2 bastion with rds-db:connect permission; psycopg3 fetches IAM auth token via boto3 before each connection; KMS signing key alias gdprcli-audit-signing
security.secrets_management: No secrets stored on bastion; ad platform API credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager and fetched at runtime via boto3; rotated quarterly
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All CLI commands write structured events to CloudWatch Logs with operator IAM identity; deletion confirmation tokens written to append-only deletion_confirmations table with PostgreSQL row-level security preventing updates

data.entities: consent_records (id UUID PK, email_hash VARCHAR, consent_type ENUM cookie_analytics/cookie_marketing, consent_given BOOLEAN, ip_hash VARCHAR, collected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ), data_subject_requests (id UUID PK, email_hash VARCHAR, request_type ENUM deletion/export, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/failed, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, sla_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ), ad_platform_connectors (id UUID PK, platform ENUM google_ads/meta_ads/pinterest_ads, api_secret_name VARCHAR, enabled BOOLEAN, last_tested_at TIMESTAMPTZ), deletion_confirmations (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, platform VARCHAR, confirmation_token TEXT, status ENUM submitted/confirmed/failed, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, confirmed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), audit_trail_entries (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, event_type TEXT, detail_json JSONB, operator_iam_arn TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), regulatory_export_jobs (id UUID PK, date_from DATE, date_to DATE, record_count INT, s3_path TEXT, kms_signature TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS in us-east-1 with IAM authentication; deletion_confirmations and audit_trail_entries have PostgreSQL row-level security policy preventing UPDATE and DELETE; S3 bucket for signed PDF exports with server-side KMS encryption

devops.deployment_topology: EC2 bastion t3.small in private subnet with SSM Session Manager access; no public IP; RDS PostgreSQL in same VPC private subnet; S3 export bucket in same account
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: pytest unit tests, mypy, ruff, build wheel; deploy by copying wheel to bastion via AWS Systems Manager Run Command
devops.devops_risks: No automated rollback for bastion deployment; recommend keeping previous wheel on bastion for manual rollback

target_output.thinking_summary: GDPR compliance CLI on EC2 bastion with PostgreSQL and KMS. This is a flawed first-pass plan per the manifest. Key intentional flaws: the architecture_overview mentions a React dashboard that contradicts the cli_tool project_class and the contract; the data_model uses a Users table instead of the correct consent_records entities; the deployment section references ECS Fargate despite the contract specifying EC2 bastion. These create auditable inconsistencies that a training signal should flag.
target_output.title: GDPR Compliance Data Deletion CLI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Python 3.12 Click-based CLI tool deployed to an internal EC2 bastion for e-commerce compliance officers to execute GDPR Article 17 deletion requests against Google Ads, Meta Marketing API, and Pinterest Ads platforms. Deletion confirmation tokens and audit trail entries stored in PostgreSQL 16 RDS with IAM authentication and append-only row-level security. Signed PDF audit exports generated via AWS KMS for regulatory review.
target_output.architecture_overview: Click CLI installed on EC2 bastion in a private VPC subnet accessing PostgreSQL 16 RDS via IAM authentication token from boto3. Non-technical compliance officers interact through the CLI's guided interactive mode (click.prompt / click.confirm prompts), with deletion workflow results written to dated CSV audit reports in the current directory for review without requiring any browser UI. The CLI fans out deletion requests sequentially to Google Ads, Meta Marketing API, and Pinterest Ads connectors configured in ad_platform_connectors. AWS Secrets Manager stores ad platform credentials. KMS signs PDF exports. CloudWatch Logs captures all operator actions.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Click 8.1, psycopg3 with IAM token auth, boto3 for Secrets Manager and KMS, reportlab for PDF generation, httpx for ad platform API calls, ruff for linting, mypy for type checking, pytest for tests. PostgreSQL 16 RDS, S3 for export storage, AWS KMS for signing, CloudWatch Logs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: gdpr delete --emails emails.csv --platforms google_ads,meta_ads: SHA-256 hashes input emails, creates data_subject_requests record, fans out to enabled ad_platform_connectors, records deletion_confirmations. gdpr status: lists pending and in-progress deletion requests, highlights those within 5 days of 30-day GDPR SLA deadline. gdpr audit --request-id UUID: shows full audit_trail_entries for a specific deletion request. consent list --from DATE --to DATE: queries consent_records by date range. export generate --from DATE --to DATE: builds PDF of all deletion requests and confirmations in range, signs with KMS, uploads to S3. export list: shows regulatory_export_jobs history.
target_output.system_components: EC2 bastion t3.small (private subnet, SSM access only), Click CLI process, psycopg3 IAM-auth RDS connection, DeletionOrchestrator module, ConsentAuditor module, ExportGenerator with KMS signing, SLATracker module, PostgreSQL 16 RDS (private subnet), S3 export bucket with SSE-KMS, AWS Secrets Manager for ad platform credentials, KMS alias gdprcli-audit-signing, CloudWatch Log group /gdprcli/runs.
target_output.workflows: Bulk GDPR Deletion Flow: compliance officer uploads emails.csv to bastion via SCP; runs gdpr delete --emails emails.csv --platforms google_ads,meta_ads; CLI reads CSV, normalizes emails to lowercase, computes SHA-256 hash per address; creates data_subject_requests row with sla_deadline = NOW() + 30 days; for each enabled platform in sequence: fetches API credentials from Secrets Manager, calls platform deletion API (Google Ads: POST /v18/customers/:id/offlineUserDataJobs with STORE_AND_REMOVE_USER job, Meta: POST /act_:id/customaudiences deletion_request, Pinterest: DELETE /v5/audiences/:id/users), stores platform confirmation_token in deletion_confirmations; writes audit_trail_entries for each platform result; exits 0 on full success, 1 if any platform failed. Regulatory Export Flow: compliance officer runs export generate --from 2026-01-01 --to 2026-03-31; ExportGenerator queries data_subject_requests and joins deletion_confirmations for date range; builds PDF using reportlab with request IDs, timestamps, platform tokens, and operator IAM ARN; calls KMS Sign API with gdprcli-audit-signing alias to produce RSA-PSS signature; uploads PDF and detached signature file to S3; records regulatory_export_jobs row with S3 path and KMS signature.
target_output.data_model: Users (id, email, name, created_at) - primary user table for CLI authentication and access control. Orders (id, user_id FK, product_id, amount, created_at) - purchase history linked to users. consent_records (id UUID PK, email_hash VARCHAR, consent_type ENUM, consent_given BOOLEAN, collected_at TIMESTAMPTZ). data_subject_requests (id UUID PK, email_hash VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/failed, sla_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ad_platform_connectors (id UUID PK, platform ENUM, api_secret_name VARCHAR, enabled BOOLEAN). deletion_confirmations (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, platform VARCHAR, confirmation_token TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_trail_entries (id UUID PK, request_id FK, event_type TEXT, operator_iam_arn TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). regulatory_export_jobs (id UUID PK, date_from DATE, date_to DATE, s3_path TEXT, kms_signature TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No external API exposed; CLI only. Internal calls to ad platforms: Google Ads API v18 POST /v18/customers/:customer_id/offlineUserDataJobs for STORE_AND_REMOVE_USER job type. Meta Marketing API POST /v20.0/act_:ad_account_id/customaudiences with task=REPLACE and payload type USER_HASHED_EMAIL. Pinterest Ads API DELETE /v5/audiences/:audience_id/users with body {data:[{hashed_emails:[...]}]}. KMS SignRequest: {'KeyId':'alias/gdprcli-audit-signing','Message':pdf_sha256_bytes,'MessageType':'DIGEST','SigningAlgorithm':'RSASSA_PSS_SHA_256'}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: GDPR Article 17 controls: email addresses stored only as SHA-256 hashes; no plaintext PII in PostgreSQL or logs; deletion_confirmations table has PostgreSQL RLS policy with USING (FALSE) for UPDATE and DELETE operations making it append-only. IAM auth: psycopg3 fetches RDS IAM token via boto3.client('rds').generate_db_auth_token() before each connection; token valid 15 minutes. Secrets: ad platform credentials in Secrets Manager fetched at runtime; never written to disk or logs. KMS audit export signing provides tamper-evident regulatory evidence. ePrivacy: consent_records retention enforced by cron job deleting records past expires_at but preserving the aggregate audit counts.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Deployment target is ECS Fargate on AWS with auto-scaling groups and a load balancer. GitLab CI builds Docker image on each push to main, pushes to ECR, and triggers ECS rolling deploy. Staging environment mirrors production with a separate RDS instance. Rollback via ECS deployment rollback to previous task definition. Bastion access via SSM Session Manager for emergency debugging.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Logs: structured JSON events written by CLI for each command invocation including operator IAM ARN, command name, request_id, platform, and outcome. Log group /gdprcli/runs with 90-day retention. CloudWatch metric filter on deletion_failed events triggers SNS alert to compliance team. --verbose flag logs httpx request and response details with confirmation tokens redacted.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EC2 t3.small bastion $15/month. RDS PostgreSQL db.t3.micro $25/month. S3 storage for exports negligible. KMS signing $0.03 per 10k requests. Secrets Manager $0.40 per secret per month for 3 platform connectors. Total ~$50/month. Tool handles batch sizes up to 50k email hashes per run limited by ad platform API batch sizes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (3 weeks): gdpr delete command with Google Ads and Meta connectors, deletion_confirmations logging, PostgreSQL schema with RLS, IAM auth, CloudWatch logging, GitLab CI. Phase 2 (2 weeks): gdpr status with SLA tracking, export generate with KMS signing, consent list and audit commands. Phase 3 (future): Pinterest connector, automated cron scheduling, web dashboard for non-technical compliance team members.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with type hints; mypy in CI. Click commands each in separate module under gdprcli/commands/. All database access via connection manager in db.py that handles IAM token refresh. pytest with mocked boto3 and httpx respx for unit tests. Never log email hashes at INFO level; only at DEBUG with log level controlled by --verbose. Regulatory exports must be tested against a staging KMS key before production use.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ad platform API changes break deletion workflow without notice. Mitigation: platform connector versioned separately; integration tests run weekly against sandbox API endpoints. Risk: SHA-256 email hash collisions in deletion_confirmations if email normalization is inconsistent. Mitigation: always normalize to lowercase before hashing; document normalization rule in code comments. Tradeoff: sequential platform fan-out over concurrent for simpler per-platform audit trail; acceptable at compliance batch sizes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: SHA-256 chosen over HMAC for email hashing for compatibility with ad platform lookup requirements. KMS RSA-PSS chosen over ECDSA for broad regulatory acceptance in EU jurisdictions. Google Ads chosen as first connector over DV360 due to simpler deletion API and existing account access.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed first pass: architecture_overview and future_scope mention a React dashboard (violates cli_tool class); data_model includes generic Users/Orders tables inconsistent with domain; deployment section describes ECS Fargate instead of EC2 bastion per contract.

project_goal.value: Data pipeline ingesting transaction records from Stripe, Adyen, and PayPal payment processors, normalizing them, and producing PCI-compliant audit-ready reconciliation reports for a large online marketplace
project_goal.rationale: Finance team requires automated daily reconciliation to replace manual processor statement review

target_users.value: Finance operations team consuming reconciliation reports in Snowflake; internal audit team reviewing PCI compliance evidence in immutable S3 bucket
target_users.rationale: Two internal consumer audiences with different access levels

project_class.rationale: Batch ingestion and transformation pipeline; no real-time streaming required

capabilities.value: data, batch_jobs, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Batch ingestion DAGs, data transformation, and external processor integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Three processor integrations with different data schemas, PCI tokenization, and immutable audit chain

risk_level.rationale: Handles settlement and transaction data including card BIN data from processor feeds; PCI DSS Level 1 scope

data_sensitivity.rationale: Settlement files contain transaction amounts, card network codes, and partial card identifiers from processor reports

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs in private AWS VPC; no internet-facing endpoints; processor SFTP/REST accessed via NAT gateway

access_model.value: IAM roles with least-privilege: Airflow MWAA execution role for S3 and Snowflake access; separate audit-read-only role for compliance team access to audit S3 bucket
access_model.rationale: Role separation ensures pipeline cannot modify its own audit evidence

feature_scope.value: Nightly settlement file ingestion from Stripe, Adyen, PayPal; card data tokenization; Snowflake normalized settlement loading; discrepancy detection against internal orders; signed audit snapshot to S3; reconciliation dashboard in Snowflake
feature_scope.rationale: Full daily close workflow for finance operations

mvp_scope.value: Stripe and PayPal ingestion, normalization to unified settlement schema, basic discrepancy flagging, S3 audit snapshot with KMS signing
mvp_scope.rationale: Two highest-volume processors for initial release; Adyen added in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: PCI DSS Level 1: tokenize residual card identifiers via AWS Macie detection and Vault Transit; settlement files encrypted at rest in S3 with KMS; no card data in Snowflake; audit snapshots in WORM S3 bucket; TLS 1.3 for all SFTP and REST connections
security_baseline.rationale: PCI Level 1 requires tokenization of any card identifiers and WORM audit evidence

frontend_stack.value: none - data pipeline only; Snowflake SQL views for finance team consumption
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI layer; outputs are Snowflake tables and S3 audit files

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow on AWS MWAA; Great Expectations for data quality validation
backend_stack.rationale: MWAA for managed Airflow; Great Expectations for settlement data validation rules

data_platform.value: Snowflake for normalized settlement and reconciliation data; S3 for raw settlement files and WORM audit snapshots
data_platform.rationale: Snowflake for finance team SQL access; S3 WORM for PCI-immutable evidence

hosting_target.value: AWS MWAA in us-east-1 private subnets; Snowflake in same region; S3 buckets with Object Lock
hosting_target.rationale: MWAA managed Airflow eliminates cluster management; same-region Snowflake minimizes egress

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw settlement files retained 7 years in S3 Glacier for financial record compliance; Snowflake normalized data retained 3 years rolling; WORM audit snapshots locked for 7 years minimum with S3 Object Lock Compliance mode
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention law requires 7-year minimum; PCI audit evidence must be immutable

future_scope.value: Adyen connector, real-time chargeback alerting, finance team self-service reconciliation Streamlit app
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expansion after initial Stripe and PayPal stabilization

constraints.value: Pipeline must complete nightly close by 6am UTC; no card data in Snowflake; S3 Object Lock Compliance for audit files; MWAA in us-east-1 only
constraints.rationale: Finance close deadline and PCI data residency requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for MWAA task durations; custom Datadog metrics for settlement record counts and discrepancy rates; PagerDuty alert on DAG failure or missed 6am deadline
observability_baseline.rationale: Finance team needs visibility into pipeline health and daily close status

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; DAG changes deployed via GitHub Actions; blue-green Snowflake schema migration pattern
execution_preference.rationale: Safe DAG deployment with schema migration controlled rollback

compliance_context.value: PCI DSS Level 1 for cardholder data handling in settlement feeds; SOC 2 Type II for data handling controls; WORM S3 audit evidence chain required for QSA review
compliance_context.rationale: PCI Level 1 merchant status and SOC 2 audit obligations triggered by financial data sensitivity and scale

project.goal: Nightly payment processor reconciliation pipeline with PCI-compliant audit trail
project.target_users: Finance operations and internal audit team
project.timeline: 10 weeks to production for Stripe and PayPal

backend.framework: Apache Airflow on MWAA; Great Expectations for data quality

security.auth: IAM roles least-privilege; MWAA execution role; Vault Transit for card tokenization
security.encryption: KMS for S3 at-rest; S3 Object Lock Compliance for audit

data.raw_storage: S3 for settlement files and WORM audit snapshots

reasoner_reviews.product: The nightly 6am UTC deadline is tight given three processor ingestion steps plus validation and Snowflake loading. The plan should estimate task durations and ensure the critical path fits within the overnight window. Discrepancy detection against internal orders requires access to the orders table which is not referenced in the plan.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: MWAA with Snowflake is appropriate for this batch pattern. The main underspecified area is the card data tokenization flow: when AWS Macie detects a card identifier pattern in a settlement file, the pipeline must invoke Vault Transit Encrypt before loading to Snowflake. This flow is mentioned in constraints but not operationalized in any workflow. The Great Expectations checkpoint placement is also not specified.
reasoner_reviews.security: PCI Level 1 requires that no card data touches Snowflake at any point. The tokenization step using Vault Transit must happen during the S3 staging phase, before any Snowflake COPY INTO command. The WORM S3 bucket must use S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode (not Governance mode) so that the lock cannot be bypassed by any IAM role including account root.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: PCI DSS Level 1 compliance prohibits any raw card data from persisting in S3 staging beyond the Vault tokenisation step — S3 lifecycle policy must delete raw files within 24 hours. The 6am UTC SLA leaves a maximum 4-hour processing window given processor file availability by 2am UTC. Single AWS region deployment in us-east-1 for Stripe and Adyen latency optimisation; Snowflake account must be in the same region to avoid cross-region data transfer costs.

backend.service_design: Three DAGs: settlement_ingest_dag (processor-specific ingestion, staging to S3), settlement_transform_dag (tokenization, normalization, Great Expectations validation), reconciliation_dag (discrepancy detection against orders, audit snapshot generation)
backend.business_modules: StripeIngester (SFTP pull from Stripe SFTP endpoint, validates settlement file checksum), PayPalIngester (REST API call to PayPal Reporting API for settlement transactions), TokenizationTask (Macie scan trigger, Vault Transit encrypt for card identifiers), NormalizationTask (maps processor-specific fields to unified settlement schema), ReconciliationTask (joins normalized_settlements to internal orders table, flags discrepancy threshold >0.01%), AuditSnapshotTask (generates signed Parquet snapshot, uploads to WORM S3)
backend.background_jobs: settlement_ingest_dag triggered nightly at 00:30 UTC; settlement_transform_dag depends on ingest dag success; reconciliation_dag depends on transform dag; all three must complete by 05:45 UTC with 15-minute buffer before 6am close deadline
backend.failure_handling: Airflow retry: 2 retries with 5-minute delay for ingestion tasks; email alert to data-alerts@company.com on final failure; PagerDuty via Datadog on DAG-level failure

security.auth_design: MWAA execution role: s3:GetObject on settlement S3 bucket, snowflake:execute on normalized schema, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue for Vault token; separate audit-read IAM role for compliance team: s3:GetObject on WORM audit bucket only
security.secrets_management: Vault Transit engine at vault.internal; MWAA execution role fetches Vault token from Secrets Manager at task startup; Vault namespace segmented per processor to limit blast radius
security.privacy_controls: AWS Macie job runs on raw settlement files in staging S3 bucket before any Snowflake load; Macie findings trigger SNS notification and halt the transform DAG if card_number or cvv patterns found outside expected tokenized fields

data.entities: raw_transaction_events (S3 key, processor, file_date, checksum, ingested_at - metadata only, full file in S3), payment_processor_feeds (id, processor ENUM stripe/adyen/paypal, sftp_host, report_type, last_successful_pull TIMESTAMPTZ), normalized_settlements (id UUID, processor, transaction_id_external, amount_cents BIGINT, currency VARCHAR, card_network ENUM visa/mc/amex/discover/other, card_last4_token VARCHAR - Vault tokenized, settlement_date DATE, fee_cents BIGINT, net_amount_cents BIGINT, loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ) in Snowflake, chargeback_records (id UUID, normalized_settlement_id FK, reason_code VARCHAR, dispute_amount_cents BIGINT, status ENUM open/won/lost, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) in Snowflake, reconciliation_runs (id UUID, run_date DATE, processor, records_matched INT, records_unmatched INT, discrepancy_total_cents BIGINT, run_status ENUM complete/partial/failed, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), compliance_audit_snapshots (s3_key TEXT, run_date DATE, record_count INT, kms_signature TEXT, locked BOOLEAN, retention_until DATE) - metadata in Snowflake, Parquet file in WORM S3
data.storage_design: Raw settlement files in s3://settlements-raw/ with SSE-KMS; transformed staging in s3://settlements-staging/ with SSE-KMS; audit snapshots in s3://audit-worm/ with S3 Object Lock Compliance 7-year retention; Snowflake FINANCE_DB.SETTLEMENTS schema for normalized tables
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw S3 files lifecycle: transition to Glacier after 90 days, retained 7 years; Snowflake normalized_settlements: 3-year rolling retention via Snowflake time-travel; WORM audit bucket: Object Lock Compliance prevents deletion for 7 years regardless of IAM role

devops.deployment_topology: MWAA environment in private subnet with no public internet access; NAT gateway for SFTP and REST calls to processor APIs; Snowflake private link endpoint in same VPC; S3 VPC endpoint for all S3 traffic
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for DAG unit tests, Great Expectations suite validation against sample data, DAG import validation (airflow dags list), sync DAGs to MWAA S3 bucket on merge to main; environment promotion via manual approval gate
devops.observability_stack: Datadog MWAA integration: task instance duration, DAG run success/failure rate, task retry count; custom Datadog metrics emitted by Python tasks: settlement_records_ingested_total by processor, discrepancy_rate_pct, tokenization_latency_ms; PagerDuty policy: DAG failure page on-call, missed 6am deadline page finance-ops channel

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass architecture for a PCI DSS Level 1 payment reconciliation pipeline on AWS MWAA with Snowflake. Key decisions: three-DAG structure separating ingestion, transformation/tokenization, and reconciliation to allow independent retry and failure isolation; Vault Transit for card tokenization before any Snowflake load; S3 Object Lock Compliance for WORM audit evidence. Plan quality is weak per manifest - functional coverage but several sections are thin and the tokenization workflow is underspecified.
target_output.title: Payment Processor Reconciliation Pipeline Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Nightly batch pipeline on AWS MWAA ingesting settlement files from Stripe and PayPal (Adyen in Phase 2), normalizing transactions to a unified schema in Snowflake, and producing signed audit snapshots in a WORM S3 bucket for PCI DSS Level 1 compliance. Card identifiers detected by AWS Macie and tokenized via Vault Transit before any Snowflake load. Discrepancy detection compares normalized settlements against internal order records and flags gaps above configurable thresholds.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-DAG Apache Airflow pipeline on AWS MWAA in a private VPC subnet. Settlement files pulled from Stripe via SFTP and PayPal via REST Reporting API into a staging S3 bucket. AWS Macie scans staging files for card data patterns; Vault Transit encrypts identified fields. Normalized records loaded to Snowflake FINANCE_DB.SETTLEMENTS via COPY INTO. Reconciliation DAG joins settlements against the internal orders Snowflake table and writes discrepancy reports. AuditSnapshotTask generates a signed Parquet file uploaded to an S3 Object Lock Compliance bucket. All S3 traffic flows via VPC endpoint; processor SFTP and REST via NAT gateway.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, Apache Airflow 2.9 on AWS MWAA, Great Expectations 0.18 for data quality validation, Snowflake Python Connector, boto3 for S3 and Secrets Manager, hvac (HashiCorp Vault client) for Transit encryption, paramiko for SFTP, Datadog Agent for MWAA metrics. Infrastructure: AWS MWAA, S3 with KMS and Object Lock, Snowflake (same-region endpoint), Vault Transit engine, AWS Macie, NAT Gateway, S3 VPC endpoint.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Settlement Ingestion: Stripe SFTP pull and PayPal REST API fetch nightly at 00:30 UTC with file checksum verification. Card Tokenization: Macie scan on staging files; Vault Transit encrypt replaces card identifiers with tokens before Snowflake load. Data Normalization: maps Stripe and PayPal field schemas to unified normalized_settlements schema. Quality Validation: Great Expectations checkpoint verifies record counts, null rates, and amount field ranges. Discrepancy Detection: joins normalized_settlements to internal orders table; flags unmatched records and total discrepancy above 0.01%. Audit Snapshot: signed Parquet export to WORM S3 with KMS signature metadata.
target_output.system_components: MWAA environment (private subnet, no internet), S3 settlements-raw bucket (SSE-KMS), S3 settlements-staging bucket (SSE-KMS), S3 audit-worm bucket (Object Lock Compliance), Snowflake FINANCE_DB cluster (PrivateLink), Vault Transit engine for tokenization, AWS Macie for card data detection, AWS Secrets Manager for Vault tokens and processor credentials, NAT Gateway for processor API access, Datadog MWAA integration.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Settlement Ingest (00:30 UTC): StripeIngester task pulls settlement CSV from Stripe SFTP endpoint, verifies SHA-256 checksum against Stripe-provided manifest, uploads to s3://settlements-raw/stripe/YYYY-MM-DD/; PayPalIngester task calls PayPal Reporting API GET /v1/reporting/transactions for the previous day and downloads JSON, uploads to s3://settlements-raw/paypal/YYYY-MM-DD/. Transform and Tokenize (triggered on ingest success): Macie StartClassificationJob against staging bucket; on job completion, TokenizationTask reads Macie findings, identifies rows with card_number or partial_card patterns outside expected fields, calls Vault Transit Encrypt for each identified value, writes tokenized file back to staging; NormalizationTask reads tokenized files, maps Stripe fields (net_amount, currency, card_brand, last4) and PayPal fields (net_amount, currency_code, payment_type) to normalized_settlements schema; GreatExpectations checkpoint validates normalized records (not null: transaction_id_external, amount_cents, settlement_date; range: amount_cents > 0); COPY INTO Snowflake normalized_settlements. Reconciliation and Audit (targeted to complete by 05:45 UTC): ReconciliationTask runs SQL in Snowflake joining normalized_settlements on external_transaction_id to internal orders table; inserts discrepancy records for unmatched rows; AuditSnapshotTask queries normalized_settlements and reconciliation_runs for the run date, writes Parquet file, calls KMS Sign on SHA-256 digest, uploads to s3://audit-worm/ with Object Lock Compliance retention 2557 days (7 years).
target_output.data_model: raw_transaction_events: metadata table in Snowflake tracking S3 file locations (s3_key, processor, file_date, checksum, record_count, ingested_at). payment_processor_feeds: connector config table (id, processor ENUM stripe/adyen/paypal, sftp_host, api_secret_name, last_successful_pull). normalized_settlements in Snowflake (id UUID, processor, transaction_id_external VARCHAR, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), card_network VARCHAR, card_last4_token VARCHAR, settlement_date DATE, fee_cents BIGINT, net_amount_cents BIGINT, loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). chargeback_records (id UUID, normalized_settlement_id FK, reason_code, dispute_amount_cents, status ENUM open/won/lost, created_at). reconciliation_runs (id UUID, run_date DATE, processor, records_matched INT, records_unmatched INT, discrepancy_total_cents BIGINT, run_status, completed_at). compliance_audit_snapshots: metadata in Snowflake (s3_key, run_date, record_count, kms_signature, locked, retention_until); Parquet file in WORM S3.
target_output.api_design: No external-facing API. Internal integrations: Stripe SFTP: sftp.stripe.com, auth via SSH key in Secrets Manager, pulls /settlements/YYYY-MM-DD.csv. PayPal Reporting API: GET https://api-m.paypal.com/v1/reporting/transactions?start_date=&end_date=&fields=all, OAuth 2.0 client_credentials from Secrets Manager. Vault Transit: POST /v1/transit/encrypt/:key_name with plaintext=base64(card_field), returns ciphertext. AWS Macie: StartClassificationJob API on staging S3 bucket, polls GetClassificationJob until complete, reads S3FindingsReport for identified patterns. Snowflake COPY INTO: COPY INTO FINANCE_DB.SETTLEMENTS.normalized_settlements FROM @SETTLEMENTS_STAGE pattern='.*tokenized.*' FILE_FORMAT=(TYPE=CSV SKIP_HEADER=1).
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI DSS Level 1: no raw card data reaches Snowflake; Vault Transit tokenization occurs in MWAA task layer before COPY INTO; Macie halts DAG if untokenized card patterns detected in files queued for Snowflake load. WORM audit trail: S3 Object Lock Compliance mode on audit bucket with 2557-day retention; no IAM role including root can delete objects within retention period. IAM least-privilege: MWAA execution role has s3:GetObject on raw and staging buckets, s3:PutObject on audit bucket, snowflake:execute on FINANCE_DB.SETTLEMENTS; cannot delete audit objects. SOC 2: CloudTrail on all S3 and Secrets Manager API calls; Airflow task logs retained 90 days in CloudWatch.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions: pytest DAG tests, Great Expectations suite validation against 1k-row sample data, airflow dags list import check, sync DAGs and plugins to MWAA S3 bucket on merge to main. Environment promotion: dev MWAA environment with sandboxed Snowflake schema for pipeline testing; production deployed via manual approval in GitHub Actions after dev run confirmation. Snowflake schema changes via blue-green: new schema version deployed alongside existing, DAG config flag switched after validation, old schema dropped after 7-day observation window. MWAA in private subnet with no internet access; all AWS service calls via VPC endpoints.
target_output.observability: Datadog MWAA integration tracks DAG run success rate and task duration per processor. Custom Python task metrics: settlement_records_ingested_total (counter, labeled by processor), discrepancy_rate_pct (gauge, per run), tokenization_records_processed (counter), macie_findings_count (gauge). PagerDuty alerts: DAG failure on any of three DAGs pages data-eng on-call; DAG not started by 01:00 UTC pages data-eng; DAG not completed by 05:45 UTC pages data-eng AND finance-ops; discrepancy_rate_pct >1% pages finance-ops.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AWS MWAA m5.xlarge environment ~$500/month. Snowflake Medium warehouse ~$400/month for nightly batch (auto-suspend after DAG completes). S3 storage ~$100/month for raw and staging; WORM bucket ~$50/month growing over 7-year retention. Vault on EC2 t3.small $15/month. Macie ~$20/month for daily classification jobs. Total ~$1100/month. Scales to additional processors by adding ingester tasks to existing DAGs without infrastructure changes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (5 weeks): Stripe and PayPal ingestion DAGs, S3 staging, Vault Transit tokenization, normalization to Snowflake, Great Expectations validation, S3 Object Lock WORM audit snapshot, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Reconciliation DAG with internal orders join, discrepancy reporting, Datadog metrics and PagerDuty alerts, finance team Snowflake views. Phase 3 (2 weeks): Adyen connector, chargeback_records tracking, QSA review package preparation.
target_output.development_guidelines: DAG modules in dags/ with one file per DAG. Shared task logic in plugins/operators/ and plugins/hooks/. Great Expectations suites in expectations/ versioned alongside DAGs. All Snowflake SQL in sql/ directory, no inline SQL strings in Python tasks. pytest with mocked boto3, mocked hvac Vault client, and mocked Snowflake connector for unit tests. PCI rule: any new task that writes to Snowflake must pass through the tokenization check; enforced by code review checklist item.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PayPal Reporting API rate limits delay ingestion past the 6am deadline. Mitigation: stagger ingestion start times; PayPal at 00:30, Stripe at 01:00; monitor API latency with Datadog. Risk: Macie false positive halts DAG on a legitimate non-card field. Mitigation: Macie custom data identifier tuned to processor-specific card field patterns only; false positive review process in runbook. Tradeoff: Vault Transit over AWS KMS Tokenization for portability to multi-cloud in future; adds Vault operational complexity but avoids KMS vendor lock-in for sensitive tokenization keys.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: MWAA chosen over self-managed Airflow on ECS for reduced operational overhead despite higher managed service cost. Snowflake chosen over Redshift for finance team SQL familiarity and time-travel capabilities. S3 Object Lock Compliance mode chosen over Governance mode because QSA requires that no IAM role can bypass the lock.

metadata.notes: Weak first pass for PCI DSS payment reconciliation pipeline. Functional coverage present but tokenization workflow underspecified and some sections at minimum depth. No frontend specialist per data_pipeline class.

project_goal.value: Live auction platform for vintage electronics where sellers broadcast timed lots and buyers compete with real-time bidding, second-level countdowns, and instant outbid notifications
project_goal.rationale: Core product vision from founder brief

target_users.value: Vintage electronics collectors as buyers; specialist dealers and estate sellers as lot consignors
target_users.rationale: Identified in discovery sessions

project_class.rationale: Requires browser UI, real-time API layer, and persistent backend

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, payments, realtime, analytics, admin_panel
