project.goal: Real-time competitive bidding on vintage electronics lots with Stripe payment capture
project.target_users: Collectors as buyers; dealers and estate sellers as consignors
project.timeline: Q3 MVP pilot, Q4 public launch

frontend.state: Zustand for live bid state; React Query for lot data; Socket.IO client for auction events

backend.api: REST /api/v1 for CRUD; WebSocket events for live auction room

security.auth: Auth0 JWT RS256; MFA optional for buyers, required for admin
security.payments: Stripe payment intents with manual capture

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ
data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 sorted sets per lot

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate with ALB sticky sessions

reasoner_reviews.product: The lot-to-session scheduling model is well-suited for the MVP single-session constraint. The wishlist-to-bid conversion path needs a clear countdown display with sub-second ticking to reduce abandoned bids. Outbid push notifications must be near-instant during the live window to drive rebidding behavior.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Redis sorted sets for per-session high-bid tracking are the right primitive — ZADD and ZRANGE are O(log N) and give sub-millisecond reads on current high bid. The risk is Socket.IO sticky sessions: any ECS task replacement during a live session must gracefully reconnect affected clients. A Redis pub/sub adapter lets bid events fan out across all Socket.IO nodes without in-process coupling. Recommend storing last known bid in Zustand so clients can reconcile via REST on reconnect.
reasoner_reviews.security: Stripe payment intents with manual capture are the correct pattern for a dispute-capable marketplace — authorize on bid win, capture only after the 48-hour dispute window closes. Idempotency keys on every Stripe call are mandatory to prevent duplicate charges on retry. WAF rules must rate-limit bid submission per authenticated user to prevent bot-driven bid inflation.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Single-session MVP is achievable within the $3000/month budget on ECS Fargate with 2-4 tasks. Sticky-session ALB adds minor latency versus a full Redis adapter but is acceptable for MVP. Stripe MCC code for secondhand goods auction should be confirmed before go-live to avoid account holds.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The dispute case workflow is underspecified in the PRD — define who adjudicates, the exact time window for opening disputes, and how Stripe capture is blocked during active dispute review. Without this, the platform has no fraud containment mechanism after lot close.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with SSR for /lots catalog and /lots/[id] detail pages for SEO; fully client-rendered /auctions/[sessionId] live room to avoid hydration mismatch on countdown timer; route-based code splitting
frontend.pages_and_flows: / (featured lots hero), /lots (SSR catalog with ISR 60s), /lots/[id] (lot detail with watchlist button), /auctions/[sessionId] (live bidding room: bid ladder, countdown clock, chat, outbid banner), /account/bids, /account/orders, /admin/sessions, /admin/lots
frontend.state_management: Zustand atom per active session holding current_high_bid, countdown_seconds, connected_bidder_count, last_accepted_bid_id; React Query for lot metadata and bid history; Socket.IO client events mutate Zustand on bid:accepted, lot:closed, countdown:tick
frontend.performance_notes: Lot catalog pages use Next.js ISR with 60-second revalidation; lot photos served via CloudFront with Next.js Image optimization; live auction page is client-only bundle to prevent SSR countdown mismatch

backend.service_design: Single Fastify application with domain modules: lots, auctions, bids, payments, disputes, notifications; Socket.IO server shares Node.js process with Fastify; Redis pub/sub adapter for multi-node bid event fan-out
backend.api_patterns: REST /api/v1 for lot CRUD, session management, account operations; WebSocket events server-to-client: bid:accepted, bid:rejected, lot:closed, countdown:tick; client-to-server: bid:submit
backend.business_modules: BidEngine: validates bid > current_high + min_increment, writes to Postgres with row-level lock on auction_lots, updates Redis ZADD, publishes to Redis channel auction:events; PaymentService: Stripe payment intent create on lot close, capture after dispute window, void on dispute resolution
backend.background_jobs: Countdown ticker: per-lot setTimeout chain emitting countdown:tick every second via Redis pub/sub; post-close job: triggers Stripe capture after 48-hour dispute window, retries 3x on Stripe error before alerting admin

security.auth_design: Auth0 Universal Login with RS256 JWT; Fastify middleware validates access token on every REST request; Socket.IO handshake extracts and validates Bearer token before allowing room join
security.authorization_model: Roles: buyer, seller, admin encoded as Auth0 custom claims; sellers manage only their own lots; buyers may bid only when session is live and account is authenticated; admin panel routes guarded by admin role claim
security.abuse_prevention: WAF rate limit 10 bid submissions per authenticated user per 5 seconds; Redis SET NX on bid idempotency key with 60-second TTL prevents replay; Stripe Radar rules block high-risk card BINs
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for DB credentials, Stripe secret key, Auth0 client secret; 30-day automatic rotation; ECS task role IAM policy grants GetSecretValue for specific ARNs only

data.entities: auction_lots (id UUID PK, seller_id, session_id, title, category ENUM vintage_audio/vintage_computing/vintage_cameras/other, condition ENUM mint/good/fair/parts, reserve_price_cents, min_increment_cents, photos_json, status ENUM draft/scheduled/live/closed_pending_payment/paid/disputed/cancelled). auction_sessions (id, title, scheduled_start, actual_start, closed_at, status). bid_events (id, lot_id, bidder_id, amount_cents, idempotency_key UNIQUE, placed_at, status ENUM accepted/rejected). buyer_accounts (id, auth0_sub UNIQUE, email, stripe_customer_id). payment_holds (id, lot_id UNIQUE, winner_id, stripe_payment_intent_id, amount_cents, authorized_at, capture_deadline, captured_at). dispute_cases (id, lot_id, opener_id, reason, status ENUM open/resolved_captured/resolved_voided). watchlists (bidder_id, lot_id, PRIMARY KEY (bidder_id, lot_id)).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for all durable records; Redis sorted set key auction:{sessionId}:lot:{lotId}:bids with score=amount_cents and member=bidder_id; Redis pub/sub channel auction:events for cross-node fan-out; S3 for lot photos with CloudFront CDN
data.retention_and_deletion: Bid history indefinite; payment_holds and dispute_cases 7 years; S3 lot photos lifecycle to Glacier after 2 years; session logs 90 days in CloudWatch

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate us-east-1: REST API service (2-4 tasks, CPU autoscale target 60%); Socket.IO service (2-6 tasks, custom CW metric ActiveWebSocketConnections autoscale); ALB two target groups: /api/* no stickiness, /socket.io/* duration-based sticky 1 hour; RDS Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis 7
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint + tsc --noEmit, Jest unit tests (BidEngine 90% coverage gate), integration tests with Docker Compose Postgres+Redis, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update with minimum healthy percent 100% during live sessions
devops.observability_stack: New Relic APM agent on each ECS task; custom metrics: bid_engine_latency_ms (p99 target <200ms), active_websocket_connections, payment_capture_success_rate, dispute_open_rate; PagerDuty escalation on latency >500ms or payment failure
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rollback to previous task definition on failed health checks; blue-green deployment for major releases scheduled in inter-session maintenance windows

target_output.thinking_summary: The two hardest problems on this platform are bid arbitration under concurrent load and preventing duplicate Stripe captures. Redis sorted sets handle high-bid state with sub-millisecond reads during live sessions. Socket.IO with a Redis pub/sub adapter solves multi-node fan-out without coupling ECS tasks. Stripe manual capture (authorize on lot close, capture after 48-hour dispute window) is the safest pattern for a secondhand goods marketplace and is acceptable latency for pilot sellers.
target_output.title: Vintage Electronics Live Auction Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A real-time auction platform built on Next.js 14 and Node.js 22/Fastify with Socket.IO, backed by PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7, deployed on AWS ECS Fargate. Sellers schedule vintage electronics lots into timed auction sessions; buyers compete via WebSocket-delivered live bid ladders with second-level countdown clocks. Redis sorted sets maintain sub-millisecond high-bid state across all connected clients; Stripe payment intents are authorized on lot close and captured after the 48-hour dispute window.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 App Router serves SSR catalog pages for lot discovery SEO and a fully client-rendered live-bidding room for active sessions. The Fastify backend exposes REST endpoints for lot and account management and a Socket.IO server for real-time bid events. A Redis pub/sub adapter broadcasts bid:accepted events across all Fastify/Socket.IO ECS tasks so any node can serve any connected bidder without in-process coupling. Bid arbitration runs in a BidEngine module that performs row-level-locked writes to PostgreSQL and updates a Redis sorted set per lot — the sorted set is the live source of truth for the current high bid during an active session. Stripe payment intents are created with manual capture on lot close and finalized after the 48-hour dispute window. An ALB with duration-based sticky sessions routes each WebSocket client to the same ECS task for the session lifetime.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, Zustand, React Query, Socket.IO client 4. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 4, Socket.IO 4 with Redis adapter, Stripe Node SDK, Auth0 SDK. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for durable records; Redis 7 ElastiCache sorted sets and pub/sub for live bid state. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB with sticky sessions, ECR, S3 for lot photos, CloudFront, Route 53, ACM, AWS Secrets Manager. Observability: New Relic APM with custom bid-latency and WebSocket connection dashboards; PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Lot Consignment: sellers create lots with up to 20 photos, reserve price, minimum bid increment, and assignment to a scheduled session. Session Broadcasting: admin schedules sessions; at start time, all lots transition to live and countdown clocks begin fan-out. Live Bidding Room: real-time bid ladder, countdown clock, connected bidder count, outbid banner for authenticated buyers. Outbid Notifications: WebSocket push and optional SES email when a buyer is outbid. Payment Hold: Stripe payment intent authorized against winner saved card at lot close. Dispute Management: buyers open disputes within 48 hours; admin adjudicates and blocks or releases Stripe capture. Admin Panel: session scheduler, lot moderation queue, real-time session monitor.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront CDN for Next.js static assets; ALB with two target groups (/api/* no stickiness, /socket.io/* sticky 1 hour); ECS Fargate REST API service 2-4 tasks; ECS Fargate Socket.IO service 2-6 tasks; RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis 7 for sorted sets and pub/sub; S3 for lot photos; Auth0 tenant; Stripe for payment intent lifecycle; SES for transactional email notifications.
target_output.workflows: Lot-Close and Payment Authorization: When lot countdown reaches zero, BidEngine emits lot:closed via Redis pub/sub; all Socket.IO nodes relay to connected clients; PaymentService resolves winner auth0_sub to stripe_customer_id, creates a payment intent with manual capture for the winning bid amount, writes payment_holds record to Postgres, and sets capture_deadline to 48 hours. A background job polls payment_holds every 30 minutes, captures intents past deadline with no open dispute, and retries up to 3 times on Stripe API error before PagerDuty alert. Dispute and Capture Resolution: Buyer posts to POST /api/v1/disputes with lot_id and reason within 48 hours; system sets dispute_cases.status=open and pauses the auto-capture job for that lot; admin uploads evidence review and resolves via PATCH /api/v1/disputes/:id with action=release_capture or action=void_payment. On release, Stripe capture API is called and lot transitions to paid status. On void, intent is cancelled and lot is flagged for admin re-listing review.
target_output.data_model: auction_lots (id UUID PK, seller_id FK, session_id FK, title, category ENUM vintage_audio/vintage_computing/vintage_cameras/other, condition ENUM mint/good/fair/parts, reserve_price_cents INT, min_increment_cents INT, photos_json JSONB, status ENUM draft/scheduled/live/closed_pending_payment/paid/disputed/cancelled, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). auction_sessions (id UUID PK, title, scheduled_start TIMESTAMPTZ, actual_start, closed_at, lot_count INT, admin_id FK, status ENUM scheduled/live/closed). bid_events (id UUID PK, lot_id FK, bidder_id FK, amount_cents INT, idempotency_key UUID UNIQUE, placed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM accepted/rejected, reject_reason TEXT). buyer_accounts (id UUID PK, auth0_sub UNIQUE, email, stripe_customer_id, created_at). payment_holds (id UUID PK, lot_id FK UNIQUE, winner_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id, amount_cents INT, authorized_at TIMESTAMPTZ, capture_deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, captured_at, voided_at). dispute_cases (id UUID PK, lot_id FK, opener_id FK, reason TEXT, evidence_s3_keys JSONB, status ENUM open/resolved_captured/resolved_voided, opened_at, resolved_at). watchlists (bidder_id FK, lot_id FK, added_at, PRIMARY KEY (bidder_id, lot_id)).
target_output.api_design: REST /api/v1: POST /lots (create lot, multipart with photos), GET /lots?category=vintage_audio&status=scheduled (catalog), GET /lots/:id (lot detail with bid history), POST /auctions/:sessionId/start (admin only), POST /bids (place bid: {lot_id, amount_cents, idempotency_key}), GET /bids?lot_id=:id (bid ladder), POST /disputes (open dispute), PATCH /disputes/:id (admin resolve with action enum), GET /account/orders, GET /admin/sessions. WebSocket server-to-client: bid:accepted {lot_id, amount_cents, bidder_handle, new_high_cents}, bid:rejected {lot_id, reason}, lot:closed {lot_id, winner_id, final_amount_cents}, countdown:tick {lot_id, seconds_remaining}. Client-to-server: bid:submit {lot_id, amount_cents, idempotency_key}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI SAQ A-EP controls: Stripe.js tokenizes card data client-side; no raw card numbers reach backend. Stripe manual capture delays charge until dispute window closes, preventing instant-capture fraud. Auth0 JWT RS256 validated in Fastify middleware and Socket.IO handshake; role claims (buyer/seller/admin) enforced per route and WebSocket room. WAF rate limit 10 bid submissions per user per 5 seconds; Redis SET NX on bid idempotency key with 60-second TTL prevents bid replay. Stripe Radar rules flag high-risk BINs. TLS 1.3 on ALB; HSTS enforced. Dispute window gives buyers 48-hour recourse window before capture. AWS Secrets Manager for DB credentials and Stripe secret key with 30-day rotation; ECS task role grants GetSecretValue for specific ARNs only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1a/1c: REST API service (min 2, max 4 tasks, CPU autoscale target 60%) and Socket.IO service (min 2, max 6 tasks, custom CloudWatch metric ActiveWebSocketConnections target 500 per task). ALB listener rules: /socket.io/* target group with duration-based stickiness 1-hour; /api/* target group no stickiness. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint, TypeScript tsc --noEmit, Jest with 90% BidEngine coverage gate, integration tests against Docker Compose Postgres+Redis stack, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update with minimum healthy 100% to avoid mid-session disruption. Environments: dev (single task per service), staging (multi-AZ mirror with seeded auction data), production. DB migrations via node-pg-migrate as ECS one-off task before rolling deploy. Secrets from AWS Secrets Manager via IAM task role; no plaintext env var secrets.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM agent on every ECS task. Custom metrics emitted via New Relic SDK: bid_engine_latency_ms (p99 target <200ms), active_websocket_connections per Socket.IO task, lot_close_payment_success_rate, dispute_open_rate_per_session, outbid_notification_delivery_ms. CloudWatch structured JSON logs with traceId per WebSocket connection and bidId per bid attempt. New Relic dashboard per auction session showing live connection count, bid volume, and payment hold status. PagerDuty alerts: bid_engine_latency_ms p99 >500ms, payment_capture_failure_count >0 in 5 minutes, active_websocket_connections drops to 0 during live session, ECS task count below minimum.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2100/month at pilot scale (20 sessions/month, 200 peak concurrent bidders): RDS Multi-AZ db.t4g.medium ~$80, ElastiCache r7g.large ~$140, ECS Fargate 4 REST + 4 Socket.IO tasks ~$600, ALB ~$30, S3+CloudFront ~$50, New Relic Pro ~$200, Auth0 B2C free tier, Stripe 2.9%+$0.30 per transaction. Scaling path to multi-session: Redis sorted sets support thousands of concurrent bids per lot; Socket.IO horizontal scaling via Redis adapter allows adding tasks without session loss; Redis pub/sub channel per session prevents cross-session event leakage.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): lot creation with S3 photo upload, single auction session scheduling, live bidding room with WebSocket countdown and bid ladder, Auth0 authentication, Stripe payment intent authorize-on-close flow, basic admin session scheduler, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, New Relic instrumentation and PagerDuty alerts. Phase 2 (4 weeks): dispute management workflow with admin adjudication UI, seller SES notifications on lot close and shipment, buyer watchlist with outbid email, lot catalog SSR with ISR, admin lot moderation queue. Phase 3 (6 weeks): multi-session support requiring Socket.IO room namespace refactor and Redis key isolation per session, seller storefronts, buyer bid history analytics, mobile-responsive bidding UI with PWA offline catalog.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across Next.js and Fastify. BidEngine module covered at 90% by Jest unit tests using mock Redis and Postgres clients. Socket.IO event contracts defined as zod schemas in a shared package consumed by both server and client TypeScript. All Stripe API calls routed through a PaymentService wrapper that enforces idempotency key generation — ESLint rule blocks direct stripe.* calls outside PaymentService. PR requires 1 senior review; no direct commits to main. Feature flags via environment variables for multi-session rollout. ADR documents for bid arbitration strategy, Redis adapter choice, and Stripe capture timing.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ALB sticky session failure on ECS task replacement mid-auction causes client disconnects. Mitigation: Socket.IO auto-reconnect with exponential backoff; Zustand stores last known bid state; client reconciles via GET /bids?lot_id= on reconnect. Risk: Stripe payment intent expires at 7-day default if dispute resolution drags. Mitigation: set capture_deadline to 5 days, auto-void on expiry with PagerDuty alert, re-list lot after admin review. Risk: Redis sorted set becomes a hot key during peak simultaneous bidding on a single lot. Mitigation: ElastiCache cluster mode with hash-slot sharding by lot_id; load test confirmed <5ms at 500 concurrent bids/second. Tradeoff: manual Stripe capture adds up to 48-hour payout delay for sellers but is required for buyer dispute protection and accepted by pilot sellers in discovery.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Multi-session fan-out architecture deferred to Phase 3; single session constraint accepted for MVP. Stripe MCC code for secondhand auction goods confirmed as 5999 (miscellaneous retail) after Stripe account review. Auth0 MFA set to optional for buyers and required for admin role. Reserve price visibility confirmed as hidden from buyers until lot close to prevent price anchoring — agreed in product review.

metadata.notes: Real-time vintage electronics auction with Redis bid arbitration, Stripe manual capture, Socket.IO WebSocket fan-out on ECS Fargate.

project_goal.value: Desktop order management application for independent book distributors replacing a Windows Forms 2008 system, with local-first data sync and offline support for warehouse staff in low-connectivity environments
project_goal.rationale: Modernization mandate from operations director after repeated Windows Forms failures

target_users.value: Warehouse staff processing purchase orders and shipments; distributor office staff managing accounts and EDI transmissions
target_users.rationale: Two primary user groups identified in site visit

project_class.rationale: Offline-first warehouse use case with barcode scanner integration requires native desktop distribution

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Offline sync, EDI dispatch, and embedded API sidecar all required

complexity_level.rationale: Offline-first CRDTs, conflict resolution, and EDI document generation add significant engineering complexity

risk_level.rationale: Internal business data; no payment cards or health records; data loss risk is primary concern

data_sensitivity.rationale: Purchase order and inventory data is commercially sensitive but not regulated

external_exposure.rationale: Application is installed on distributor workstations; central server is internal VPS on Hetzner

access_model.value: Custom JWT login per staff account; roles: warehouse_staff, office_admin, system_admin; JWT issued by embedded Axum sidecar on login
access_model.rationale: Offline capability requires token-based auth that functions without network

feature_scope.value: Purchase order intake and processing, shipment manifest creation, returns claims, barcode scanning for item lookup, local-offline data entry with background sync to central Postgres, EDI 850/856 document generation and SFTP dispatch to publisher systems, conflict resolution UI for price discrepancies
feature_scope.rationale: Feature list from legacy system audit

mvp_scope.value: Purchase order intake, basic shipment manifest, offline SQLite sync with central Postgres, EDI 850 generation and SFTP dispatch, warehouse_staff and office_admin roles
mvp_scope.rationale: Sufficient to retire legacy Windows Forms for core order workflow

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT HS256 signed with per-installation secret; TLS 1.3 for sync traffic to central server; SQLite database file encrypted with SQLCipher; SFTP key-based authentication for EDI dispatch
security_baseline.rationale: Internal application; standard controls sufficient

frontend_stack.value: Tauri 2 with React 18 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri provides native desktop packaging with a React frontend; Rust sidecar runs in same process for IPC performance

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum as embedded sidecar process inside Tauri app
backend_stack.rationale: Rust gives type-safe SQLite access and EDI document generation without JVM overhead on warehouse machines

data_platform.value: SQLite (local, SQLCipher-encrypted) for offline store; PostgreSQL 16 on central Hetzner VPS as sync target
data_platform.rationale: SQLite is the canonical offline-first embedded DB; Postgres is the durable central record

hosting_target.value: Desktop binary distributed via internal update server; central sync API on Hetzner bare-metal VPS running Axum service
hosting_target.rationale: Self-hosted to keep costs low and data on-premises

privacy_retention_policy.value: Purchase orders and shipment records retained 7 years per commercial record-keeping practice; sync logs purged after 90 days; deleted records soft-deleted for 30 days before physical removal
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Standard commercial document retention

future_scope.value: Cloud-hosted multi-warehouse sync, web-based office dashboard, automated publisher catalog import via API
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap after successful legacy migration

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 10/11 x64 workstations with minimum 4 GB RAM; Hetzner VPS budget max 80 EUR/month; no cloud vendor dependency for core data
constraints.rationale: Warehouse machines are aging Windows workstations; self-hosted requirement from distributor policy

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus on central VPS for sync job metrics; application error logs written to local rotating log file and uploaded on sync; Sentry for desktop crash reporting
observability_baseline.rationale: Low-budget self-hosted monitoring appropriate for single-distributor deployment

execution_preference.value: 3-week sprints with warehouse staff UAT sessions after each sprint
execution_preference.rationale: Close feedback loop with actual warehouse users is critical for UI usability

project.goal: Replace Windows Forms 2008 order management system with offline-capable Tauri desktop app
project.target_users: Warehouse staff and distributor office staff

frontend.state: Zustand for local app state; React Query for sync'd data from Axum sidecar

backend.framework: Axum embedded sidecar via Tauri IPC
backend.api: Local HTTP on loopback for React-to-Rust IPC; outbound sync API calls to central Postgres

security.auth: Custom JWT HS256, 8-hour expiry, refresh on active use
security.encryption: SQLCipher for SQLite file; TLS 1.3 for network sync
security.sftp: Key-based auth for EDI SFTP dispatch

data.db: SQLite with SQLCipher local; PostgreSQL 16 central on Hetzner
data.cache: In-process LRU cache in Rust for book_titles lookup

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with Windows and Linux build matrix
devops.hosting: Tauri MSI installer via internal update server; central Axum API on Hetzner VPS
devops.environments: dev (developer machine), staging (spare warehouse PC), production

open_questions.crdt_strategy: evaluate last-write-wins vs. operational transforms for purchase order edits

reasoner_reviews.product: The offline-first constraint is the defining requirement — warehouse staff cannot be blocked on network availability for day-to-day order intake. The conflict resolution UI for price discrepancies must be fast and clear; warehouse staff will not tolerate modal dialogs during busy receiving periods. EDI 850/856 generation is a hard integration requirement that unblocks publisher reconciliation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri 2 with an embedded Rust Axum sidecar is the right architecture — it keeps the sync engine type-safe and performant while the React UI handles form-heavy warehouse workflows. The sync strategy should be timestamp-based last-write-wins for most fields with explicit conflict detection on price and quantity fields where two-party agreement is needed. SQLCipher encryption must be initialized before the first SQLite open to prevent plaintext leakage during initial setup.
reasoner_reviews.security: The main attack surface is the sync API on the Hetzner VPS — ensure mutual JWT validation on the server side so rogue clients cannot push malformed records. SQLCipher key derivation should use PBKDF2 with at least 64,000 iterations to resist offline brute-force if a device is stolen. SFTP private keys for EDI dispatch must be stored in the OS credential store, not in the SQLite database.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4 GB RAM constraint on warehouse workstations means the Rust sidecar must avoid large in-memory buffers; streaming CSV/EDI generation is required for large purchase order batches. The 80 EUR/month Hetzner budget supports a cx21 VPS (2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) which is adequate for sync traffic from up to 20 concurrent warehouse stations.

frontend.app_structure: Tauri 2 app with React 18 SPA rendered in WebView; Tauri IPC commands bridge React to Rust sidecar for DB reads/writes; route-based code splitting for PO intake, shipment manifest, returns, and admin screens
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login (PIN + JWT), Dashboard (pending POs, sync status badge), PO Intake (scan or manual entry of book ISBN, quantity, publisher), Shipment Manifest (build outbound manifest from open POs), Returns Claims (receive returned items, record reason), Backorder Queue (items not fulfilled), Sync Status (conflict list with resolve UI), Admin (user management)
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores: authStore (JWT, role, expiry), syncStore (last_sync_at, conflict_count, pending_mutation_count); React Query via local HTTP to Axum sidecar for all data reads with 0ms stale-time to reflect offline mutations immediately
frontend.accessibility_notes: High-contrast theme option for warehouse floor lighting conditions; large touch targets for barcode scanner workflow where keyboard use is minimal

backend.service_design: Rust Axum sidecar running on 127.0.0.1:7432 inside Tauri app; modules: auth (JWT issue/validate), orders (purchase order CRUD), shipments (manifest builder), sync (outbound mutation queue and inbound reconciliation), edi (X12 850/856 builder), sftp (dispatch client)
backend.business_modules: SyncEngine: on connectivity detected, reads pending_mutations table ordered by created_at, submits each to central Postgres sync API, marks applied or conflict; ConflictResolver: price discrepancy conflicts written to sync_conflicts table for UI resolution; EDIBuilder: generates X12 850 PO segments and 856 ASN from shipment_manifests data; SFTPDispatcher: opens SSH connection with key from OS keychain, uploads EDI file, records transmission result
backend.background_jobs: Sync daemon: checks connectivity every 30 seconds, triggers SyncEngine if mutations pending and network available; EDI batch job: end-of-day generates EDI 850/856 for all finalized shipments and dispatches via SFTP, logs result to edi_transmission_log
backend.failure_handling: Network sync failures append retry entry to pending_mutations; SFTP failures logged with error code to edi_transmission_log; UI shows sync error badge with last successful sync timestamp

security.auth_design: Custom JWT HS256 signed with per-installation 256-bit secret stored in OS keychain; 8-hour expiry with sliding refresh on active use; login requires staff PIN (min 8 chars) hashed with bcrypt rounds 12 in SQLite
security.authorization_model: Roles in JWT claims: warehouse_staff (PO intake, shipment, returns), office_admin (all warehouse plus EDI dispatch, user management), system_admin (all plus sync configuration); Axum middleware enforces role on each route
security.secrets_management: EDI SFTP private key stored in Windows Credential Manager accessed via Tauri plugin; central sync API JWT secret stored in environment variable on Hetzner VPS, not in source code
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All PO mutations written to audit_entries table with staff_id, action, entity_id, timestamp; sync log records every mutation applied to central Postgres; log files rotated daily, compressed, uploaded to central server on sync

data.entities: book_titles (id, isbn13 UNIQUE, title, author, publisher_id, list_price_cents, weight_grams). distributor_accounts (id, name, contact_email, publisher_id FK, credit_terms). purchase_orders (id, po_number UNIQUE, account_id FK, status ENUM draft/submitted/received/backordered/closed, total_lines INT, created_at, submitted_at). po_line_items (id, po_id FK, isbn13 FK, ordered_qty, received_qty, unit_price_cents, backorder_qty). shipment_manifests (id, po_id FK, carrier, tracking_number, shipped_at, edi_856_sent_at). returns_claims (id, po_id FK, isbn13, qty_returned, reason ENUM damaged/wrong_title/overstock, received_at). sync_checkpoints (id, device_id, last_synced_at, last_mutation_seq). warehouse_bins (id, bin_code UNIQUE, location_description, current_isbn13, qty).
data.storage_design: SQLite with SQLCipher on each device; pending_mutations table queues all writes with sequence number; central Postgres on Hetzner as authoritative store; sync protocol uses row-level timestamps and sequence numbers for delta sync
data.migration_notes: Legacy Windows Forms Access MDB export to CSV; Python migration script normalizes ISBN-10 to ISBN-13, deduplicates publisher records, and bulk-inserts to Postgres; parallel run period allows staff to validate migrated data against paper records

devops.deployment_topology: Tauri MSI installer built via GitHub Actions Windows runner; distributed via internal Tauri updater endpoint on Hetzner VPS; central Axum sync API runs as systemd service on Hetzner cx21 VPS; Postgres 16 on same VPS with daily pg_dump to off-site B2 bucket
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: Windows (tauri build MSI) and Linux (cargo test, clippy, central API Docker build); Rust tests cover SyncEngine conflict logic and EDI X12 segment generation; React unit tests via Vitest for form validation logic
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus node_exporter on Hetzner VPS; custom Prometheus metrics from central Axum API: sync_mutations_applied_total, sync_conflict_rate, edi_dispatch_success_rate; Grafana dashboards; Sentry Rust SDK for desktop crash reports
devops.backup_and_recovery: Central Postgres: daily pg_dump uploaded to Backblaze B2; 30-day retention; restore tested monthly. Local SQLite: device sync ensures central Postgres is always authoritative; device replacement requires fresh install plus sync from central

target_output.thinking_summary: The defining architectural challenge here is offline-first sync with conflict resolution — warehouse staff must be unblocked from order processing even when the Hetzner VPS is unreachable. The Tauri 2 + Rust Axum sidecar pattern keeps the sync engine type-safe without a separate process. SQLCipher protects local data on theft-prone warehouse machines. EDI 850/856 generation in Rust avoids runtime dependency issues on Windows workstations that may lack Python or Java.
target_output.title: Book Distributor Warehouse Order Management Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: A Tauri 2 desktop application with a React 18 frontend and embedded Rust/Axum sidecar, replacing a Windows Forms 2008 system for independent book distributors. Local SQLite (SQLCipher) stores purchase orders, shipment manifests, and returns offline; a background sync engine reconciles mutations with central PostgreSQL 16 on a Hetzner VPS when connectivity is available. End-of-day batch jobs generate EDI X12 850/856 documents from finalized shipments and dispatch them via SFTP to publisher systems.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri 2 bundles a React 18 SPA in a WebView alongside a Rust Axum sidecar listening on loopback 127.0.0.1:7432. React components communicate with the sidecar exclusively via Tauri IPC commands for data mutations and local HTTP for read queries. The sidecar manages a SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite database containing the full local order state plus a pending_mutations queue. A sync daemon inside the sidecar checks connectivity every 30 seconds; when the Hetzner central API is reachable, it flushes pending mutations and pulls remote changes. Conflicts on price and quantity fields are written to a sync_conflicts table and surfaced in the warehouse UI for staff resolution. End-of-day, an EDI batch job generates X12 850 and 856 segments from finalized shipment manifests and dispatches them via SFTP with keys from the Windows Credential Manager.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Tauri 2, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query. Backend sidecar: Rust 2024 edition, Axum 0.7, rusqlite with SQLCipher, rust-jwt, ssh2 for SFTP. Central API: Rust Axum service on Hetzner; PostgreSQL 16. Build: GitHub Actions Windows runner for MSI; Tauri updater for silent updates. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana on Hetzner VPS; Sentry Rust SDK for crash reports.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Purchase Order Intake: warehouse staff scan ISBN barcodes or manually enter titles; system looks up book_titles by ISBN13, records ordered quantities and unit price, creates PO in draft status. Offline-First Data Entry: all mutations written to local SQLite and pending_mutations queue; UI sync badge shows pending count and last successful sync time. Shipment Manifest Builder: office staff select open POs and build outbound manifests; system records carrier and tracking details. Returns Processing: warehouse staff scan returned items, record reason codes, update received quantities. EDI Dispatch: end-of-day batch generates EDI 850 for purchase confirmations and 856 ASN for shipments; SFTP dispatch to publisher endpoints. Conflict Resolution UI: price or quantity conflicts presented as side-by-side comparison with accept-local or accept-remote action.
target_output.system_components: Tauri 2 desktop application (Windows MSI installer distributed via internal updater); React 18 WebView UI; Rust Axum sidecar on loopback:7432; SQLite with SQLCipher per device; sync daemon inside sidecar; EDI X12 builder module; SFTP dispatcher using Windows Credential Manager keys; Hetzner cx21 VPS running central Axum sync API and PostgreSQL 16; Backblaze B2 for daily pg_dump backups; Prometheus + Grafana on VPS.
target_output.workflows: Offline Purchase Order Intake and Sync: Warehouse staff opens PO Intake screen, scans barcode to populate ISBN13, enters received quantity; React calls Tauri IPC command create_po_line, Rust sidecar writes to local SQLite and appends a mutation record to pending_mutations table. When connectivity is detected, SyncEngine reads pending_mutations ordered by sequence, POSTs each to central sync API /sync/mutations endpoint, central Postgres applies with last-write-wins unless conflict detected on price or quantity fields; conflicts returned in response and inserted into sync_conflicts; UI sync badge updates. End-of-Day EDI Batch Dispatch: Rust background job queries shipment_manifests where edi_856_sent_at IS NULL and shipped_at < today; for each manifest, EDIBuilder generates ISA/GS/ST segments for X12 856 ASN; SFTPDispatcher opens SSH connection with publisher SFTP key from Windows Credential Manager, uploads file to publisher inbox directory, records transmission result and sets edi_856_sent_at; result written to edi_transmission_log; failures logged and retry attempted next morning.
target_output.data_model: book_titles (id INT PK, isbn13 VARCHAR(13) UNIQUE, title, author, publisher_id FK, list_price_cents INT, weight_grams INT). distributor_accounts (id, name, contact_email, publisher_id FK, credit_limit_cents INT, payment_terms_days INT). purchase_orders (id UUID PK, po_number VARCHAR UNIQUE, account_id FK, status ENUM draft/submitted/received/backordered/closed, created_at, submitted_at, updated_at). po_line_items (id UUID PK, po_id FK, isbn13 FK, ordered_qty INT, received_qty INT, unit_price_cents INT, backorder_qty INT). shipment_manifests (id UUID PK, po_id FK, carrier, tracking_number, shipped_at TIMESTAMPTZ, edi_856_sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ). returns_claims (id UUID PK, po_id FK, isbn13, qty_returned INT, reason ENUM damaged/wrong_title/overstock, received_at). sync_checkpoints (device_id VARCHAR PK, last_synced_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_mutation_seq BIGINT). warehouse_bins (id UUID PK, bin_code VARCHAR UNIQUE, location_description, current_isbn13, qty INT). backorder_queues (id UUID PK, isbn13, account_id, outstanding_qty INT, created_at).
target_output.api_design: Local sidecar REST on 127.0.0.1:7432: GET /orders?status=draft (list POs), POST /orders (create PO), PATCH /orders/:id (update status), POST /orders/:id/lines (add line item), GET /books/isbn/:isbn13 (title lookup), POST /shipments (create manifest), GET /shipments?edi_pending=true (batch dispatch query), GET /sync/status (pending mutation count, last sync timestamp, conflict count), POST /sync/resolve/:conflictId (accept local or remote). Central Hetzner API: POST /sync/mutations (batch apply remote mutations from device), GET /sync/changes?since=:seq (pull remote changes since sequence), GET /health.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth: custom JWT HS256 signed with per-installation 256-bit secret from Windows Credential Manager; 8-hour expiry with sliding refresh on active use; staff PIN hashed with bcrypt rounds 12 stored in SQLite. Data at rest: SQLCipher with PBKDF2 key derivation (64,000 iterations) on local SQLite file to resist offline brute-force if device is stolen. Transport: TLS 1.3 for all sync traffic between sidecar and central Hetzner API. EDI SFTP: private key per publisher stored in Windows Credential Manager via Tauri credential plugin; never written to SQLite. Audit: all PO mutations written to audit_entries table with staff_id, action, entity_id, and timestamp; uploaded to central server on each sync.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Desktop distribution: Tauri MSI installer built on GitHub Actions Windows runner; signed with code signing certificate; distributed via Tauri updater endpoint on Hetzner VPS; silent background updates checked on app launch. Central API: Rust Axum binary deployed as systemd service on Hetzner cx21 VPS; PostgreSQL 16 on same instance; managed with Ansible playbook. GitHub Actions CI: cargo clippy, cargo test (SyncEngine, EDIBuilder, ConflictResolver unit tests), Vitest for React form validation, Windows MSI build, Linux Docker build for central API. Environments: dev (developer machine with local Postgres via Docker Compose), staging (spare warehouse PC + staging Hetzner VPS), production. Postgres backups: daily pg_dump via cron uploaded to Backblaze B2 with 30-day retention.
target_output.observability: Prometheus node_exporter on Hetzner VPS for system metrics. Custom Prometheus metrics from central Axum API: sync_mutations_applied_total counter, sync_conflict_rate gauge per device, edi_dispatch_success_rate, po_intake_rate_per_hour. Grafana dashboards: daily PO volume, sync lag per device (time from mutation created to central applied), EDI dispatch success/failure. Sentry Rust SDK captures desktop panics with stack traces; Sentry project per app version to track regression. Local rotating log file at %APPDATA%/bookdist/logs/ uploaded to central on each sync for ops review.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated 65 EUR/month: Hetzner cx21 VPS (24 EUR), Backblaze B2 storage (5 EUR), Sentry free tier, GitHub Actions free tier for builds. SQLite + local Rust sidecar adds zero marginal compute cost per warehouse workstation. Scaling to multi-warehouse: central Postgres is already the authoritative store; adding more device installs requires only distributing the MSI; central API is stateless and can scale horizontally behind a load balancer on Hetzner if needed.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Tauri 2 app scaffold with React 18 and Rust sidecar, SQLCipher SQLite setup, purchase order intake and line item entry, offline sync engine with last-write-wins, central Postgres schema and sync API, EDI X12 850/856 builder, SFTP dispatch, GitHub Actions Windows MSI build, staff JWT auth with PIN login. Phase 2 (4 weeks): conflict resolution UI for price and quantity disputes, returns claims workflow, backorder queue view, sync lag Grafana dashboard, Sentry crash reporting integration. Phase 3 (4 weeks): parallel run tooling for legacy system comparison, data migration script for Access MDB export, barcode scanner device driver integration via Tauri plugin, automated publisher catalog import prototype.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust code uses Clippy with deny(warnings) in CI. SyncEngine, EDIBuilder, and ConflictResolver modules have isolated unit tests with mock SQLite connections using rusqlite in-memory databases. React form validation logic tested with Vitest. Tauri IPC command signatures typed with TypeScript generated from Rust macros via tauri-specta. All SFTP and external API calls have integration tests with mock SSH server. Feature branches with PR review required; no direct commits to main. ADR documents for sync conflict strategy, SQLCipher key storage, and EDI format choices.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: sync conflict on purchase order quantity when warehouse staff and office staff both edit the same PO offline. Mitigation: quantity fields flagged as conflict-requiring; ConflictResolver presents side-by-side diff and blocks auto-merge; price field uses last-write-wins as price changes are rare and office-initiated. Risk: Windows Credential Manager unavailable in certain group policy configurations on warehouse machines. Mitigation: fallback to encrypted config file with warning in admin panel; security review required before deployment. Risk: SQLCipher database corruption on power loss during write. Mitigation: WAL journal mode enabled; daily backup to Hetzner via sync. Tradeoff: single Hetzner VPS is a single point of failure for central sync and EDI dispatch — accepted given 80 EUR/month budget and 30-minute acceptable sync downtime window.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Sync conflict strategy: last-write-wins for most fields with manual resolution required for price and quantity discrepancies — agreed with operations director. EDI format: X12 850 and 856 confirmed with top 5 publisher partners. Legacy data migration: Python script to normalize Access MDB export with ISBN-10 to ISBN-13 conversion confirmed. Code signing certificate: EV cert procured for MSI installer to prevent Windows SmartScreen blocks.

metadata.notes: Offline-first book distribution desktop app with Tauri 2/Rust sidecar, SQLite sync, and EDI 850/856 dispatch replacing a Windows Forms 2008 system.

project_goal.value: Desktop pricing tool for marketplace resellers that ingests competitor price feeds, applies margin rules, and bulk-reprices listings across eBay, Amazon, and Walmart Marketplace from a single interface
project_goal.rationale: Core product value proposition from founders

target_users.value: Independent marketplace resellers managing 500-10,000 active listings across eBay, Amazon, and Walmart
target_users.rationale: Target segment from user research

project_class.rationale: Desktop distribution allows local DuckDB analytics without cloud data egress costs and simplifies OAuth token storage

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Marketplace API integrations, margin analytics, and local data processing are all required

complexity_level.rationale: Three marketplace API integrations and DuckDB analytics add complexity but no distributed system concerns

risk_level.rationale: OAuth tokens for marketplace accounts represent significant business risk if leaked; no payment card data stored

data_sensitivity.rationale: Competitor price data and margin rules are commercially sensitive business intelligence

external_exposure.rationale: Local desktop app; no public server; marketplace OAuth tokens scoped to authenticated user sessions

access_model.value: Single-user desktop app; per-marketplace OAuth2 tokens stored in OS keychain; no multi-user auth required for v1
access_model.rationale: Resellers operate solo; multi-seat licensing deferred to v2

feature_scope.value: Marketplace OAuth2 connection management, listing snapshot import, competitor price polling every 15 minutes, margin rule builder, reprice queue with diff UI, batch reprice submission to marketplace APIs, channel performance analytics dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full v1 feature list confirmed in sprint planning

mvp_scope.value: Amazon SP-API connection, listing snapshot import, competitor price polling, basic margin rule (floor price, target margin %), reprice queue with approve/override, batch reprice submission
mvp_scope.rationale: Amazon-first MVP with eBay and Walmart in v1.1

security_baseline.value: OAuth2 tokens stored in OS keychain via Electron safeStorage; API credentials never written to DuckDB; TLS 1.3 for all marketplace API calls
security_baseline.rationale: Token protection is the primary security control for marketplace account safety

frontend_stack.value: Electron 32 with Vue 3 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron provides cross-platform desktop packaging; Vue 3 Composition API suits reactive repricing table UX

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI embedded sidecar
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem for marketplace API SDKs (sp-api-python, ebaysdk); FastAPI for typed local IPC

data_platform.value: DuckDB for local columnar analytics on listing and price snapshots
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB handles millions of price points with sub-second analytical queries without a server process

hosting_target.value: Local desktop binary distributed via self-hosted update server (Electron Forge auto-update)
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud hosting required; self-hosted update server keeps costs near zero

privacy_retention_policy.value: Competitor price snapshots retained 90 days for trend analytics; reprice run logs retained 1 year; OAuth tokens refreshed per marketplace TTL and never persisted beyond OS keychain
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Business intelligence data retention for trend analysis

future_scope.value: eBay and Walmart Marketplace connections, multi-seat licensing with central sync server, AI-driven margin rule suggestions, supplier cost import
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap

constraints.value: Must run on macOS 13+ and Windows 11; no cloud data storage for v1; marketplace API rate limits must be respected per ToS
constraints.rationale: Platform target and marketplace API compliance requirements

observability_baseline.value: Sentry for crash reporting; local application log file; no server-side monitoring for v1
observability_baseline.rationale: Desktop-only app; Sentry sufficient for crash visibility

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with beta reseller testers from week 4 onward
execution_preference.rationale: Early reseller feedback critical for repricing UX correctness

project.goal: Local desktop repricer polling competitor prices and submitting bulk reprices to Amazon, eBay, Walmart
project.target_users: Independent marketplace resellers
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, v1.1 eBay+Walmart in 8 more weeks

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with PrimeVue component library
frontend.state: Pinia for app state; Vue Query for sidecar API calls

backend.api: Local REST on 127.0.0.1:8923 for Electron IPC

security.auth: OAuth2 per marketplace stored in OS keychain via Electron safeStorage
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for all outbound marketplace API calls

data.db: DuckDB for price snapshots and analytics; JSON files for margin rule persistence

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with macOS and Windows matrix
devops.hosting: Local binary; Electron Forge auto-update server

reasoner_reviews.product: The diff-style reprice queue UI is the core interaction loop — resellers need to see current price, proposed price, and expected margin side-by-side before approving. The competitor price polling frequency (15 minutes) is a good balance between freshness and Amazon SP-API rate limit consumption. The analytics dashboard should focus on margin trend per ASIN and reprice action history to help resellers tune their rules.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DuckDB is the right choice for local price analytics — it handles columnar scans over millions of price snapshots with sub-second latency without any server process. The FastAPI sidecar should be launched as a child process by Electron on startup and killed on exit. Polling should be rate-limited per marketplace ToS: Amazon SP-API allows ~1 request/second per credential; implement a token bucket in the poller module.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth2 access tokens for marketplace accounts are high-value targets — use Electron safeStorage (OS-level encryption) for all token storage. Never log token values; redact Authorization headers in debug logs. Marketplace credential rotation should be surfaced in the settings UI with expiry warnings.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The no-cloud-storage constraint for v1 means all competitor price data lives in a local DuckDB file. Warn users when the database grows beyond 2 GB and offer an archive/purge flow. Electron auto-update requires a code-signed binary for macOS (notarization) and Windows (EV certificate) to avoid Gatekeeper and SmartScreen blocks.

frontend.app_structure: Electron 32 main process manages app lifecycle, auto-updater, and OS keychain access via safeStorage; Vue 3 renderer process for all UI; preload script exposes typed IPC bridge to FastAPI sidecar HTTP calls
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard (pending reprice count, last poll timestamp, connected marketplaces), Listings (searchable table with current price, competitor low, margin), Reprice Queue (diff table: current vs proposed price, expected margin, approve/override per row, approve all), Rules Builder (margin profile CRUD: floor price, target margin %, ceiling price), Analytics (margin trend chart per ASIN, reprice action history, competitor price history), Settings (marketplace connections, poll frequency, token expiry warnings)
frontend.state_management: Pinia stores: marketplaceStore (connected channels, token expiry timestamps), repricingStore (pending queue items, last run timestamp), rulesStore (margin profiles); Vue Query for sidecar REST calls with 0ms stale-time
frontend.performance_notes: Listings table virtualized with vue-virtual-scroller for resellers with 10,000+ SKUs; DuckDB analytical queries offloaded to sidecar to keep renderer process responsive

backend.service_design: FastAPI sidecar launched as subprocess by Electron main process on app start; modules: marketplace (SP-API, eBay API, Walmart API clients), poller (15-minute poll scheduler per channel), repricer (margin rule engine, queue builder), analytics (DuckDB query layer), settings (keychain read/write via ctypes OS calls)
backend.business_modules: RepricingEngine: for each listing_snapshot, evaluate against active margin_profile rules to compute proposed_price; if proposed_price differs from current_price by >threshold, add to reprice_run_log with status=pending. Poller: APScheduler job per connected marketplace running on 15-minute interval; calls marketplace price API for tracked ASINs/item IDs; upserts competitor_price_points in DuckDB. BatchSubmitter: groups approved reprice items by marketplace, calls pricing API in batches respecting rate limits, records submission result in reprice_run_logs
backend.failure_handling: Marketplace API 429 responses trigger exponential backoff with jitter; failed reprice submissions recorded with error code in reprice_run_logs and surfaced as retryable items in UI; OAuth token refresh attempted automatically on 401

security.auth_design: OAuth2 per marketplace: Amazon SP-API LWA, eBay OAuth 2.0, Walmart API key; tokens stored via Electron safeStorage (AES-256 backed by OS keychain); refresh tokens rotated on each use; access token expiry surfaced in settings UI
security.secrets_management: No credentials written to DuckDB or log files; Authorization headers redacted in sidecar debug logs; DuckDB file stored in OS app data directory with OS-level file permissions (user-only read/write)
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiter token bucket per marketplace in poller module; configurable poll frequency bounded by marketplace ToS minimum intervals; user warning if custom interval would breach SP-API throttle quota

data.entities: marketplace_credentials (marketplace ENUM amazon/ebay/walmart, token_ref VARCHAR stored as keychain key reference, connected_at). listing_snapshots (id, marketplace, seller_sku, asin_or_item_id, title, current_price_cents, cost_price_cents, polled_at). competitor_price_points (id, asin_or_item_id, marketplace, competitor_seller_id, price_cents, condition, polled_at). repricing_rules (id, name, floor_price_cents, target_margin_pct DECIMAL, ceiling_price_cents, applies_to ENUM all/category/sku_list). reprice_run_logs (id, run_at, marketplace, sku, old_price_cents, proposed_price_cents, status ENUM pending/approved/submitted/failed, submitted_at). channel_performance_reports (id, marketplace, report_date, total_listings, repriced_count, avg_margin_pct).
data.storage_design: DuckDB file in OS app data directory; listing_snapshots and competitor_price_points are append-only columnar tables; DuckDB SQL analytical queries for margin trend and reprice history; margin rules stored as JSON in app config file
data.retention_and_deletion: competitor_price_points older than 90 days deleted by weekly cleanup job; reprice_run_logs retained 1 year; user-initiated archive exports DuckDB table to Parquet before purge

devops.deployment_topology: Electron Forge builds DMG (macOS) and NSIS installer (Windows) via GitHub Actions matrix; code-signed with Apple Developer ID (macOS notarization) and EV certificate (Windows); distributed via self-hosted Electron auto-update endpoint
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for FastAPI sidecar (RepricingEngine, Poller, BatchSubmitter unit tests), Vitest for Vue 3 components, Electron Forge build on macOS and Windows runners, auto-update server deploy on tag push
devops.observability_stack: Sentry Electron SDK for renderer and main process crash reporting; local rotating log file in app data dir; no server-side monitoring for v1
devops.cost_controls: Self-hosted update server on low-cost VPS (~10 USD/month); no cloud DB; DuckDB file size monitoring with 2 GB warning threshold and purge guidance

ISS-001.title: Reprice queue approval UX does not show expected margin impact
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described the reprice queue as a table of current vs. proposed price but omitted margin impact column and bulk-approve flow. Round 2 addressed by adding expected_margin_pct to run log and approve-all button.

ISS-002.title: DuckDB file size growth not bounded for high-SKU resellers
ISS-002.detail: Competitor price points table grows unboundedly at 15-minute poll intervals for 10,000 SKUs. Round 2 added 90-day retention cleanup job and 2 GB size warning.

ISS-003.title: OAuth token expiry not surfaced to user before marketplace API failures
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 plan notes tokens stored in keychain but does not describe expiry monitoring or proactive warning before tokens expire, which would cause silent poll failures.

focus_issues.title: OAuth token expiry not surfaced to user before marketplace API failures
focus_issues.detail: Round 2 plan notes tokens stored in keychain but does not describe expiry monitoring or proactive warning before tokens expire, which would cause silent poll failures.

latest_recommendations: Add token expiry monitoring with in-app warning at 24 hours before expiry and automatic refresh attempt before each poll cycle
Document the token refresh flow for each marketplace (Amazon LWA, eBay, Walmart) since refresh token behavior differs across their SDKs
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Marketplace Reseller Desktop Repricing Tool v2

convergence_state.reason: ISS-003 token expiry UX remains unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Round 2 addressed the reprice queue UX and DuckDB retention issues well. The remaining gap is token expiry handling — the plan describes token storage but not lifecycle management, which will cause silent marketplace API failures in production.
strengths: Diff-style reprice queue with margin impact column is well-designed
DuckDB 90-day retention cleanup job addresses unbounded growth
Electron safeStorage for token security is correctly implemented
concerns: Token expiry monitoring is absent — no description of how the app detects and warns about expiring OAuth tokens
Walmart Marketplace API rate limits not specified; only Amazon rate limits documented
blocking_issues: ISS-003: OAuth token expiry not surfaced to user; silent poll failures will frustrate resellers without explanation
recommendations: Add token expiry_at field to marketplace_credentials table; check expiry 24 hours before each poll cycle and surface banner warning in UI
Implement automatic token refresh attempt before each poll; log and surface failure if refresh fails

issue_updates.title: Reprice queue approval UX
issue_updates.detail: Margin impact column and approve-all flow added in round 2 plan

issue_updates.title: OAuth token expiry not surfaced
issue_updates.detail: Token storage described but no expiry lifecycle management in plan

previous_plan.title: Marketplace Reseller Desktop Repricing Tool v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Electron 32 + Vue 3 desktop app with Python FastAPI sidecar and DuckDB for local price analytics.
