previous_plan.executive_summary: Tauri 2 + Svelte 5 desktop app with Rust Axum sidecar and SQLCipher SQLite. Barcode scanning for item intake, multi-bin location tracking, consignor settlement auto-calculation, database-layer period locking via SQLite trigger, USB backup with SHA-256 verification.
previous_plan.data_model: consignor_accounts (split_pct), consigned_items, location_bins, transfer_events, settlement_periods (status ENUM draft/calculated/locked), payout_records (no split_pct_snapshot — ISS-004), sale_transactions.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: SQLite trigger prevents payout_records updates when period locked. SQLCipher AES-256. bcrypt PIN hashing.
previous_plan.workflows: Barcode intake to bin assignment. SettlementEngine calculates period payouts. Manager approves and locks period. PDF report generated (using live split_pct — bug per ISS-004).

best_plan.title: Consignment Boutique Desktop Inventory and Settlement App v3
best_plan.executive_summary: Tauri 2 + Svelte 5 desktop app with Rust Axum sidecar and SQLCipher SQLite. Barcode scanning for item intake, multi-bin location tracking, consignor settlement auto-calculation, database-layer period locking via SQLite trigger, USB backup with SHA-256 verification.
best_plan.data_model: consignor_accounts (split_pct), consigned_items, location_bins, transfer_events, settlement_periods (status ENUM draft/calculated/locked), payout_records (no split_pct_snapshot — ISS-004), sale_transactions.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: SQLite trigger prevents payout_records updates when period locked. SQLCipher AES-256. bcrypt PIN hashing.
best_plan.workflows: Barcode intake to bin assignment. SettlementEngine calculates period payouts. Manager approves and locks period. PDF report generated (using live split_pct — bug per ISS-004).

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 fix is ISS-004: the PDF payout report must use a snapshotted consignor split percentage, not the live value from consignor_accounts, because consignor terms can change between settlement calculation and period lock. The fix requires adding a split_pct_snapshot column to payout_records and populating it at SettlementEngine calculation time. The PDF generator must be updated to read split_pct_snapshot from payout_records. This is a data model and workflow change with corresponding PDF report and settlement engine impacts.

fix_report.action_taken: Added split_pct_snapshot DECIMAL(5,2) column to payout_records table; SettlementEngine.calculate() now reads consignor_accounts.split_pct at calculation time and writes it to payout_records.split_pct_snapshot; PDF generator updated to use payout_records.split_pct_snapshot for all historical reports; data migration for existing payout_records joins to consignor_accounts.split_pct as best-effort backfill with NULL for records where consignor rate is unknown
fix_report.expected_outcome: PDF payout reports will correctly reflect the consignor split rate in effect at settlement calculation time; consignor disputes over rate changes between calculation and lock are eliminated; audit trail is complete for 7-year retention requirement

target_output.title: Consignment Boutique Desktop Inventory and Settlement Application
target_output.executive_summary: A Tauri 2 desktop application with Svelte 5 frontend and embedded Rust/Axum sidecar, backed by SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite, for multi-location consignment boutiques replacing spreadsheet-based tracking. Barcode scanning drives item intake and location bin assignment; the SettlementEngine auto-calculates consignor payouts at period close, snapshotting each consignor's split percentage at calculation time so PDF payout reports are historically accurate even if terms change. Settlement periods are locked via a SQLite database trigger after manager approval, making payout records immutable. Daily backups to USB drive include SHA-256 checksum verification.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri 2 bundles a Svelte 5 SPA in a WebView alongside a Rust Axum sidecar on loopback:7891. Svelte components communicate with the sidecar via Tauri IPC commands for mutations and local HTTP for reads. The sidecar manages the SQLCipher SQLite database containing consignor accounts, consigned items, location bins, sale transactions, and settlement records. A SQLite trigger enforces immutability on payout_records when the parent settlement_period.status is locked — this cannot be bypassed through the application layer. The SettlementEngine module snapshots each consignor's split_pct into payout_records.split_pct_snapshot at calculation time so historical reports are independent of live account data. USB barcode scanners are captured as keyboard events in the WebView scan input field. The BackupService copies the SQLite file to the configured USB drive path and verifies integrity via SHA-256 checksum.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Tauri 2, Svelte 5 with runes, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS. Backend sidecar: Rust 2024 edition, Axum 0.7, rusqlite with SQLCipher, printpdf crate for PDF generation, bcrypt crate for PIN hashing. Data: SQLite with SQLCipher WAL mode, local file. Distribution: Tauri NSIS installer for Windows 10/11; GitLab CI for builds; USB or LAN share distribution. Observability: local rotating log file; no external monitoring.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Item Intake: staff scans barcode via USB HID scanner (keyboard event capture); sidecar performs barcode_registry lookup; if known, shows item details; if new, opens consignor item registration form with bin assignment. Location Management: inventory browser filtered by bin; transfer screen for moving items between bins with transfer_events audit record. Settlement Period: manager creates period with date range; SettlementEngine calculates payouts reading sale_transactions, consigned_items, and snapshotting consignor split_pct; manager reviews per-consignor breakdown, approves, and locks period. PDF Payout Reports: generated from locked payout_records using split_pct_snapshot for each line item; includes consignor name, item list, sale prices, split rate at calculation, and net payout. Backup: daily automated or manual USB backup with SHA-256 integrity check.
target_output.system_components: Tauri 2 NSIS installer (Windows 10/11); Svelte 5 WebView renderer; Rust Axum sidecar on loopback:7891; SQLCipher SQLite in WAL mode; SQLite trigger for settlement lock enforcement; printpdf Rust crate for PDF generation; BackupService with checksum verification; GitLab CI build pipeline.
target_output.workflows: Barcode Item Intake: staff focuses scan input field on Item Intake screen; barcode scanner emits keystrokes; Svelte input handler captures barcode string on Enter key; calls POST /intake/scan with barcode value; sidecar queries barcode_registry, returns item details and consignor name if found; staff confirms bin assignment or corrects; sidecar writes consigned_items record and barcode_registry entry; item status set to available. Settlement Period Calculation with Split Snapshot: manager creates settlement period via POST /settlement/periods; SettlementEngine queries sale_transactions within date range joined to consigned_items; for each sale, reads consignor_accounts.split_pct at query time and writes payout_records row with split_pct_snapshot populated; aggregates payout_amount_cents = sale_price_cents * split_pct_snapshot / 100; period status set to calculated. Manager reviews per-consignor totals on Settlement screen; calls POST /settlement/periods/:id/approve; Axum route sets period.status=locked; SQLite trigger activated — any subsequent UPDATE on payout_records for this period returns SQLITE_CONSTRAINT error. PDF generated from payout_records using split_pct_snapshot field; exported to file via Tauri save dialog.
target_output.data_model: consignor_accounts (id UUID PK, name, contact_email, phone, split_pct DECIMAL(5,2), agreement_signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at). consigned_items (id UUID PK, barcode VARCHAR UNIQUE, consignor_id FK, title, description, category, condition, asking_price_cents INT, current_bin_id FK, status ENUM available/sold/returned/transferred, intake_at TIMESTAMPTZ). location_bins (id UUID PK, bin_code VARCHAR UNIQUE, location_name, capacity INT). transfer_events (id UUID PK, item_id FK, from_bin_id FK, to_bin_id FK, transferred_by FK, transferred_at TIMESTAMPTZ). settlement_periods (id UUID PK, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, status ENUM draft/calculated/locked, created_by FK, approved_by FK, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). payout_records (id UUID PK, period_id FK, consignor_id FK, item_id FK, sale_price_cents INT, split_pct_snapshot DECIMAL(5,2) NOT NULL, payout_amount_cents INT, locked BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE). sale_transactions (id UUID PK, item_id FK, sale_price_cents INT, sold_at TIMESTAMPTZ, pos_transaction_ref VARCHAR). barcode_registry (barcode VARCHAR PK, item_id FK UUID, registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). staff_accounts (id UUID PK, name, pin_hash VARCHAR, role ENUM staff/manager, created_at). audit_log (id UUID PK, staff_id FK, action, entity_type, entity_id, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Axum sidecar on loopback:7891. POST /auth/login (body: {pin}; returns JWT), POST /intake/scan (body: {barcode, bin_id}; returns item or new-item form trigger), GET /inventory?bin_id=:id&consignor_id=:id&status=available, POST /transfers (move item to new bin), POST /settlement/periods (create period), POST /settlement/periods/:id/calculate (trigger SettlementEngine), GET /settlement/periods/:id/payouts (per-consignor breakdown with split_pct_snapshot), POST /settlement/periods/:id/approve (lock period), GET /reports/payout/:periodId (generate PDF bytes), POST /backup/trigger (copy to USB path), GET /health.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Data at rest: SQLCipher AES-256 with PBKDF2 key derivation on SQLite file. Period immutability: SQLite trigger on payout_records prevents UPDATE/DELETE when parent settlement_period.status=locked — enforced at DB layer independent of application logic. Auth: staff PIN hashed with bcrypt rounds 12; JWT HS256 4-hour expiry; role claims enforced by Axum middleware per route. Audit: all settlement calculations, approvals, and payout record writes logged to audit_log table with staff_id, action, entity references, and timestamp. Financial records retained 7 years per compliance_context requirement; split_pct_snapshot in payout_records ensures historical accuracy of consignor agreements for audit purposes. No internet connectivity required; no data leaves the device.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline: cargo clippy, cargo test (SettlementEngine snapshot logic 90% coverage gate, PeriodLocker trigger unit tests using rusqlite in-memory DB, BackupService checksum tests), Svelte Vitest component tests, Tauri NSIS build on Windows GitLab runner, installer artifact uploaded to GitLab package registry. Distribution: installer copied to USB drive or LAN network share by admin. Environments: dev (developer Windows machine), staging (spare boutique PC with test data), production. Version string displayed in Settings for support identification. Backup: daily automated backup at app startup checks USB drive presence; BackupService copies SQLite file with timestamp suffix, writes SHA-256 checksum file, verifies checksum, records result in app log; manager sees in-app confirmation or warning if USB absent.
target_output.observability: Local rotating log file in OS app data directory (7-day retention): structured JSON entries for item_intake, scan_miss, settlement_calculated, period_locked, backup_completed, backup_failed, auth_failure. In-app notification panel shows last backup status (timestamp and checksum result) and last settlement calculation status. No external monitoring for v1; log file is primary diagnostic tool for support escalations. Version number and SQLite database file size shown in Settings for operator reference.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Near-zero infrastructure cost: no cloud hosting, no server process; GitLab CI free tier for builds; USB drives provided by boutique. SQLite handles 10,000 consigned items with full sale history well within 100 MB database size. Settlement calculation for 500 items per period completes in under 1 second in Rust. Multi-location expansion (v2) would require LAN sync between SQLite instances or migration to central Postgres, but the current schema is designed with UUIDs to support this without key conflicts.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Tauri 2 + Svelte 5 app scaffold, SQLCipher SQLite setup with WAL mode, barcode scan intake with barcode_registry, consignor account management, location bin CRUD, SettlementEngine with split_pct_snapshot, SQLite trigger for period lock enforcement, PDF payout report generation, GitLab CI build pipeline, Windows NSIS installer. Phase 2 (4 weeks): sale transaction import from boutique POS (CSV upload flow), staff management UI, audit log viewer for manager, daily auto-backup with USB presence check and in-app status. Phase 3 (future): multi-location LAN sync, consignor self-service portal, direct POS API integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust code uses Clippy with deny(warnings). SettlementEngine, PeriodLocker, and BackupService modules have 90% cargo test coverage using rusqlite in-memory SQLite databases. Svelte components tested with Vitest including barcode scan input handler and settlement approval flow. split_pct_snapshot is a NOT NULL column — SettlementEngine panics in debug mode if split_pct is missing for any consignor during calculation to catch data integrity issues at development time. All payout arithmetic uses integer cents to avoid floating-point rounding errors. ADR documents for SQLite trigger strategy, split_pct snapshot design, and PDF generation library choice.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SQLite database file corruption on power loss during settlement calculation. Mitigation: WAL journal mode; BackupService daily checksum backup; SettlementEngine wraps full calculation in a single SQLite transaction — partial writes roll back cleanly. Risk: USB drive not present at scheduled backup time. Mitigation: BackupService emits in-app warning banner on startup if USB drive is absent; manager prompted to insert drive before end-of-day close. Tradeoff: no auto-update server for v1 means version upgrades require manual installer redistribution — accepted given boutique's unreliable internet; version number in settings allows staff to identify update need.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-004 resolved: split_pct_snapshot column added to payout_records; SettlementEngine populates at calculation time; PDF generator reads snapshot not live consignor data. Consignor split_pct change after period lock: any split_pct change in consignor_accounts only affects future periods; locked periods are immutable by SQLite trigger. Barcode scanner input method: USB HID scanner emulates keyboard in WebView scan input — no Tauri plugin required; confirmed with common USB scanner models. POS sale transaction import: CSV upload via Tauri file picker confirmed with boutique POS vendor.

metadata.notes: Round 4 consignment boutique desktop app fixing ISS-004 split_pct snapshot in payout_records for auditable PDF reports; Tauri 2/Svelte 5/Rust/SQLCipher.

project_goal.value: Peer-to-peer secondhand clothing marketplace where users list, discover, and buy pre-owned fashion items with in-app messaging and buyer protection escrow
project_goal.rationale: Core business model enabling circular fashion commerce between consumers

target_users.value: Fashion-conscious consumers aged 18-35 selling and buying pre-owned clothing; both iOS and Android users
target_users.rationale: Target demographic identified in market research

project_class.rationale: Primary surface is a Flutter mobile app on iOS and Android

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, payments, realtime, analytics, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Full marketplace feature set including payment escrow and real-time chat

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-party escrow, real-time messaging, search indexing, and microservices decomposition

risk_level.rationale: Handles financial transactions, payment card data, and user identity verification

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores buyer/seller payout account details and processes card payments via Stripe

external_exposure.rationale: Consumer-facing app accessible to any registered user

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with email/Google/Apple SSO; JWT tokens for API calls; seller identity verification via Stripe Identity for payout eligibility
access_model.rationale: Social login lowers signup friction; Stripe Identity gates payout to verified sellers

feature_scope.value: Garment listing with photo upload and condition/size metadata, search and discovery with filters, buyer offer flow, in-app chat between buyer and seller, escrow hold on purchase, shipment label generation via Shippo, buyer confirmation and escrow release, seller ratings and reviews
feature_scope.rationale: Complete marketplace transaction lifecycle

mvp_scope.value: Listing creation, basic search, buy-now flow with Stripe escrow, Firestore-based chat, shipment label via Shippo, buyer confirmation and payout release
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable transaction flow for market validation

security_baseline.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A via Stripe hosted elements (no card data on backend), GDPR consent and data export, TLS 1.3, Firebase App Check to prevent API abuse
security_baseline.rationale: Stripe SAQ A eliminates PCI scope for backend; GDPR required for EU users

frontend_stack.value: Flutter 3.22 with Dart; Riverpod for state management; go_router for navigation
frontend_stack.rationale: Single codebase targeting iOS and Android; Riverpod is type-safe and testable

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; microservices per domain deployed on GCP Cloud Run
backend_stack.rationale: Separate services for listing, payments, search, and messaging allow independent scaling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 per service (Cloud SQL); Firestore for chat threads; Redis 7 for session cache and rate limiting; Elasticsearch 8 for garment search
data_platform.rationale: Polyglot persistence matching access patterns: relational for transactional data, document for chat, full-text for garment discovery

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run per microservice; Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL; Firebase for auth and Firestore; Elasticsearch on Elastic Cloud
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run scales to zero for cost efficiency; managed database services reduce ops burden

privacy_retention_policy.value: GDPR: user data exportable and deletable on request; chat messages retained 12 months; transaction records retained 7 years for tax purposes; listing photos deleted 30 days after sale completion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR right to erasure with carve-out for financial records

future_scope.value: AI-powered garment condition grading from photos, style recommendation feed, physical pop-up event integration, brand authentication badges
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP differentiation features

constraints.value: App Store and Google Play compliance required; Stripe as sole payment processor in MVP; Shippo for label generation; no third-party ad SDK in v1
constraints.rationale: Mobile store requirements and vendor commitments

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus for backend services; Firebase Crashlytics for Flutter app crash reporting; Cloud Logging for structured service logs
observability_baseline.rationale: Prometheus/Grafana for service health; Crashlytics for mobile crash visibility

execution_preference.value: Domain-driven microservices with independent deploy cadence; 2-week sprints
execution_preference.rationale: Enables frontend and backend squads to ship independently

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A via Stripe hosted payment elements; GDPR for EU users with consent management, data export, and right to erasure; Stripe Identity for seller KYC before payout eligibility
compliance_context.rationale: Financial data handling and EU user base require PCI and GDPR compliance

project.goal: P2P secondhand clothing marketplace with escrow payment protection
project.target_users: Fashion consumers 18-35 on iOS and Android

backend.framework: FastAPI per microservice on Cloud Run

security.auth: Firebase Auth with email/Google/Apple SSO + App Check
security.kyc: Stripe Identity for seller payout eligibility

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL per service)
data.cache: Redis 7 for rate limiting and session data

constraints.stores: App Store and Play Store compliant

open_questions.recommendation_algo: Collaborative filtering vs content-based TBD for post-MVP
open_questions.dispute_resolution: Manual CS process in MVP; automated rules post-MVP

reasoner_reviews.product: The escrow model is a strong buyer trust mechanism for P2P fashion. The 72-hour auto-release window balances buyer protection with seller cash flow. Offer flow before purchase is essential - most fashion P2P sales involve negotiation, and skipping this in MVP would hurt conversion.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Microservices per domain is correct here given the distinct scaling profiles: listing service sees write spikes during photo upload, search service needs Elasticsearch query capacity, payment service needs isolation for PCI. Cloud Run's scale-to-zero is ideal for initial load levels but latency on cold starts must be profiled for the payment flow.
reasoner_reviews.security: Firebase App Check is essential to prevent scraping and API abuse on the listing endpoints. Stripe SAQ A scoping requires that no card data ever touches the backend API - Flutter must use Stripe's native mobile SDK for payment collection. Stripe Identity for seller KYC must gate payout creation, not listing creation.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 16-week timeline is aggressive for the full feature set. Recommend sequencing: listing and search in weeks 1-8, payment escrow in weeks 9-12, shipping integration and chat polish in weeks 13-16. Offer negotiation flow should be included in MVP as it is core to the P2P model.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Elasticsearch adds operational complexity. Validate that Elastic Cloud cost stays within budget for launch traffic before committing; PostgreSQL full-text search may suffice for MVP garment discovery given expected listing volumes under 100K items.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app organized by feature: /listing (create, detail, gallery), /discover (search, filters, feed), /chat (thread list, message view), /checkout (offer, buy-now, payment confirmation), /profile (seller dashboard, purchase history, settings). Shared: /widgets, /services (API clients), /providers (Riverpod state).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Onboarding: splash, Firebase Auth sign-in with Google/Apple/email. Discover: search bar with garment filters (category, size, condition, price range), listing grid with photo carousel. Listing Detail: photo gallery, condition badge, size, price, seller rating, offer/buy-now CTAs. Chat: Firestore-backed real-time message thread per transaction. Checkout: offer submission, buy-now, Stripe payment sheet (native SDK), escrow confirmation screen. Seller Dashboard: active listings, pending shipments, completed sales, payout status.
frontend.state_management: Riverpod providers: AuthNotifier (Firebase user state), ListingSearchNotifier (Elasticsearch query + filters), CartNotifier (offer state, payment intent), ChatStreamProvider (Firestore real-time listener per thread).
frontend.frontend_risks: Stripe native SDK version conflicts with Flutter plugin ecosystem require careful dependency pinning. Photo upload UX on slow connections needs progress indicators and retry logic to prevent abandoned listings.

backend.service_design: ListingService (CRUD for garment_listings, S3-equivalent photo upload to Cloud Storage, publishes listing_created event to Pub/Sub). SearchService (Elasticsearch indexing consumer from Pub/Sub, query API). PaymentService (Stripe PaymentIntents, escrow hold, manual capture on buyer confirmation, payout to Stripe Connect seller accounts). ShippingService (Shippo label generation, tracking webhook ingestion). ChatService is handled by Firestore directly from Flutter SDK.
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.0 per service. API Gateway (Cloud Endpoints) handles Firebase JWT verification and routes to backend services. Inter-service events via Cloud Pub/Sub.
backend.failure_handling: PaymentService uses idempotency keys on all Stripe API calls. ListingService retries Pub/Sub publish with exponential backoff. ShippingService webhooks are idempotent via Shippo tracking event deduplication.

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth issues JWTs validated at Cloud Endpoints API Gateway. Firebase App Check (Play Integrity on Android, DeviceCheck on iOS) required for all listing and payment endpoints.
security.authorization_model: Buyer can view all listings, submit offers, initiate purchases. Seller can manage own listings and view own orders. Payment service verifies Stripe Identity verification_status before allowing payout creation.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting via Redis per Firebase UID: 10 listing creates/hour, 50 offer submissions/hour. Image moderation via Google Cloud Vision SafeSearch on listing photo upload.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR deletion: soft-delete user record, anonymize transactions (preserve amount/date for accounting), hard-delete listing photos from Cloud Storage after 30 days.

data.entities: seller_profiles (id, firebase_uid, display_name, bio, stripe_account_id, kyc_status, rating_avg, created_at). garment_listings (id, seller_id, title, brand, category, size, condition ENUM new_with_tags/good/fair, price_cents, status ENUM draft/active/sold/delisted, photos_json, created_at). buyer_offers (id, listing_id, buyer_id, offer_price_cents, status ENUM pending/accepted/rejected/expired, expires_at). escrow_holds (id, listing_id, buyer_id, seller_id, stripe_payment_intent_id, held_amount_cents, status ENUM held/released/refunded, confirmed_at). shipment_labels (id, escrow_id, shippo_order_id, carrier, tracking_number, label_url, created_at). seller_reviews (id, reviewer_id, seller_id, transaction_id, rating 1-5, comment, created_at). chat_threads: Firestore collection keyed by listing_id+buyer_id composite.
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL) per service with separate schemas. Firestore for chat_threads real-time. Elasticsearch 8 for garment search index. Cloud Storage for listing photos with CDN via Cloud CDN.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run services per domain: listing-service, search-service, payment-service, shipping-service. Each service has its own Cloud SQL instance (PostgreSQL 16). Cloud Endpoints API Gateway fronts all services. Flutter app released via Fastlane to App Store and Play Store.
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build pipelines: on PR runs pytest, mypy, and OpenAPI lint per service. On merge to main builds Docker image, pushes to Artifact Registry, deploys to staging Cloud Run revision. On tag deploys to production with canary traffic split (10% -> 50% -> 100% over 30 min).
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics sidecar per Cloud Run service. Grafana dashboards: listing creation rate, offer conversion rate, payment success/failure rate, escrow hold duration. Firebase Crashlytics for Flutter crash reports.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a P2P fashion marketplace with microservices on GCP Cloud Run. Key decisions: Stripe PaymentIntents with manual capture for escrow, Firestore for real-time chat to avoid building a messaging service, Firebase App Check to protect listing endpoints from scraping, Elasticsearch on Elastic Cloud for garment discovery with size/condition/category filters. PCI scope contained to SAQ A via Flutter Stripe SDK - no card data on backend.
target_output.title: Thrift Market - P2P Secondhand Fashion Marketplace Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Flutter mobile app backed by Python FastAPI microservices on GCP Cloud Run enabling peer-to-peer secondhand clothing transactions. Buyers discover garments via Elasticsearch, negotiate via Firestore real-time chat, and purchase with Stripe-powered buyer protection escrow. Sellers receive payouts to Stripe Connect accounts after buyer delivery confirmation. PCI-DSS SAQ A scoping achieved via Flutter Stripe native SDK. GDPR-compliant with user data export and soft-delete.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven microservices architecture on GCP. Flutter 3.22 app communicates with a Cloud Endpoints API Gateway that validates Firebase JWTs and routes to domain services on Cloud Run: ListingService (garment CRUD + Cloud Storage photo upload + Pub/Sub event emission), SearchService (Elasticsearch 8 consumer indexing listing events), PaymentService (Stripe PaymentIntents manual capture for escrow, Stripe Connect for seller payouts), ShippingService (Shippo label generation and tracking webhook ingestion). Chat bypasses the backend entirely via Flutter Firestore SDK with Firestore Security Rules. Firebase App Check (Play Integrity / DeviceCheck) protects all API endpoints from non-app clients.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3.22 / Dart, Riverpod 2.x, go_router 12, Stripe Flutter SDK, firebase_auth, cloud_firestore, firebase_crashlytics. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0, Pydantic v2, stripe-python, shippo SDK. Databases: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL per service), Firestore, Elasticsearch 8 (Elastic Cloud), Redis 7 (Memorystore). Infrastructure: GCP Cloud Run, Cloud Endpoints, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage, Cloud Pub/Sub, Artifact Registry. CI/CD: Cloud Build, Fastlane for mobile releases.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Seller listing creation: photo upload (up to 8 images) to Cloud Storage, garment metadata form (brand, category, size ENUM XS-3XL, condition ENUM new_with_tags/like_new/good/fair, price), draft save and publish. Discover feed: Elasticsearch query with garment filters (category, size range, condition, price min/max, seller location radius). Offer flow: buyer submits offer_price, seller accepts/rejects/counters within 24 hours, accepted offer transitions to checkout. Buy-now: direct purchase at listing price. Buyer protection escrow: Stripe PaymentIntent created on offer acceptance, card captured immediately, funds held pending delivery confirmation. In-app chat: Firestore thread per listing+buyer pair, real-time message delivery via Flutter StreamBuilder. Shipment: seller generates Shippo label from within app after sale. Buyer confirmation: 72-hour window to confirm receipt or open dispute; auto-release on timeout. Seller reviews: buyer submits 1-5 star rating and comment after transaction completion.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Endpoints API Gateway (Firebase JWT validation, App Check enforcement, routing). ListingService on Cloud Run (FastAPI, garment CRUD, Cloud Storage presigned URL generation, Pub/Sub listing_created/updated/sold event emission). SearchService on Cloud Run (FastAPI, Elasticsearch 8 index consumer via Pub/Sub push subscription, garment search query endpoint with size/condition/category/price filters). PaymentService on Cloud Run (FastAPI, Stripe PaymentIntents with manual capture, Stripe Connect payout creation, Stripe Identity KYC webhook handler). ShippingService on Cloud Run (FastAPI, Shippo API wrapper, tracking event ingestion via webhook). Firestore (chat_threads collection with Security Rules requiring auth uid match). Redis 7 on Memorystore (rate limiting per Firebase UID, session cache). Cloud Storage (listing photos with Cloud CDN).
target_output.workflows: Garment listing publication: Seller taps 'Sell', uploads photos (Flutter -> presigned URL from ListingService -> Cloud Storage); fills metadata form (brand, category, size, condition, price); ListingService persists garment_listing record in PostgreSQL with status draft; on publish ListingService emits listing_created event to Pub/Sub; SearchService consumer indexes listing into Elasticsearch within 5 seconds; listing appears in discovery feed. Buyer purchase with escrow: Buyer taps 'Buy Now' on listing; PaymentService creates Stripe PaymentIntent with manual capture and amount = listing_price_cents + platform_fee_cents; Flutter Stripe SDK presents payment sheet (no card data to backend); PaymentService receives payment_intent.created webhook, creates escrow_holds record with status held; ListingService marks listing status sold; ShippingService generates Shippo label for seller; seller ships item; buyer confirms receipt in app within 72 hours or timer auto-releases; PaymentService captures PaymentIntent and initiates Stripe Connect transfer to seller payout account.
target_output.data_model: seller_profiles (id UUID PK, firebase_uid VARCHAR UNIQUE, display_name, stripe_account_id, stripe_kyc_status ENUM unverified/pending/verified, rating_avg DECIMAL, listing_count INT, created_at). garment_listings (id UUID PK, seller_id FK, title, brand, category ENUM tops/bottoms/dresses/outerwear/shoes/accessories, size VARCHAR, condition ENUM new_with_tags/like_new/good/fair, price_cents INT, status ENUM draft/active/pending_sale/sold/delisted, description, views_count INT, created_at, updated_at). buyer_offers (id UUID PK, listing_id FK, buyer_firebase_uid, offer_price_cents INT, status ENUM pending/accepted/rejected/expired/withdrawn, expires_at TIMESTAMP, created_at). escrow_holds (id UUID PK, listing_id FK, buyer_firebase_uid, seller_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, held_amount_cents INT, platform_fee_cents INT, status ENUM held/released/refunded, confirmation_deadline TIMESTAMP, confirmed_at, released_at). shipment_labels (id UUID PK, escrow_id FK, shippo_order_id, carrier, tracking_number, label_url, created_at). seller_reviews (id UUID PK, reviewer_firebase_uid, seller_id FK, escrow_id FK UNIQUE, rating SMALLINT CHECK 1-5, comment TEXT, created_at). chat_threads: Firestore collection path /threads/{listingId}_{buyerUid}/messages/{messageId} with fields (text, sender_uid, sent_at, read_at).
target_output.api_design: ListingService: POST /v1/listings (create draft), PUT /v1/listings/{id} (update), POST /v1/listings/{id}/publish, GET /v1/listings/{id}, DELETE /v1/listings/{id} (delist). SearchService: GET /v1/search?q=&category=&size=&condition=&price_min=&price_max=&seller_lat=&seller_lon=&radius_km= returning paginated hits with _score. PaymentService: POST /v1/payment-intents (create for offer/buy-now), POST /v1/payment-intents/{id}/confirm-receipt (buyer confirms, triggers capture and payout), POST /v1/stripe-webhooks (Stripe event ingestion). ShippingService: POST /v1/shipments (create Shippo label for escrow_id), GET /v1/shipments/{id}/tracking. All routes protected by Cloud Endpoints Firebase JWT validation and App Check header.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS SAQ A: Flutter Stripe SDK renders payment sheet natively; no card data transmitted to backend APIs; Stripe handles card storage and processing. GDPR: ConsentService records explicit consent at signup; /v1/users/me/export returns JSON archive; /v1/users/me DELETE soft-deletes profile, anonymizes transaction amounts, queues Cloud Storage photo deletion job for 30 days. Stripe Identity: seller stripe_kyc_status must be verified before PaymentService creates payout transfer. Firebase App Check: Play Integrity (Android) and DeviceCheck (iOS) attestation required on all API calls; rejects requests from emulators or tampered apps. Rate limiting: Redis per Firebase UID (10 listing creates/hour, 100 search queries/minute). Image moderation: Cloud Vision SafeSearch on every uploaded listing photo; auto-rejects adult/violent content.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Build pipelines per service: on PR runs pytest with 80% coverage gate, mypy strict, and OpenAPI 3.0 lint. On merge to main builds Docker image (python:3.12-slim), pushes to Artifact Registry, deploys to staging Cloud Run revision (min 0, max 3 instances). On git tag deploys to production with traffic split: 10% canary for 10 minutes, health check (error rate <0.5%), promote to 50% for 10 minutes, then 100%. Database migrations via alembic upgrade head run as Cloud Run Job before service deploy. Mobile releases via Fastlane: TestFlight for iOS staging, Firebase App Distribution for Android staging; App Store / Play Store submissions on release tags. Secrets in Secret Manager; injected as Cloud Run env vars.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from FastAPI instrumentation sidecar. Grafana dashboards: listing_creation_rate (listings/min), offer_conversion_rate (accepted_offers/submitted_offers), escrow_hold_duration_p50/p99, payment_success_rate, payout_failure_count. Firebase Crashlytics captures Flutter crashes with stack traces and device metadata. Cloud Logging with structured JSON including trace_id for cross-service correlation. Alerts: payment_success_rate < 98% pages on-call via PagerDuty; escrow_hold_duration_p99 > 5s triggers investigation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run scales to zero between traffic bursts, estimated $400/month at 10K MAU. Cloud SQL f1-micro per service in dev, db-n1-standard-1 in production (~$200/month for 4 services). Elastic Cloud starter cluster ~$95/month for MVP search volumes under 200K listing documents. Redis Memorystore Basic 1GB ~$30/month. Cost controls: Cloud Run max-instances cap per service, Cloud SQL auto-storage disabled in dev, Elastic Cloud hot-warm tier for listing index with 90-day rollover.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core listing and discovery (weeks 1-8): Flutter app with auth, garment listing creation with photo upload, Elasticsearch indexing via Pub/Sub, search with filters, ListingService and SearchService on Cloud Run, Cloud Build CI pipelines. Phase 2 - Payment escrow and shipping (weeks 9-12): PaymentService with Stripe PaymentIntents manual capture, Stripe Connect seller accounts, Stripe Identity KYC, escrow hold/release flow, ShippingService with Shippo label generation, buyer confirmation flow. Phase 3 - Chat, reviews, and launch (weeks 13-16): Firestore chat with Security Rules, seller review submission, Fastlane App Store/Play Store release pipeline, GDPR export/delete endpoints, Firebase App Check enforcement, Grafana dashboards.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python services: ruff linter + black formatter, mypy strict, pytest with 80% coverage. Dart/Flutter: flutter analyze enforced in CI, flutter test for widget and unit tests, integration tests via flutter_test on staging. OpenAPI 3.0 spec committed per service; breaking changes require version bump. All Stripe calls include idempotency key (UUID from client). Pub/Sub consumers must be idempotent with deduplication on event ID. ADR documents for escrow design, KYC gating, and search provider selection.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Stripe Identity verification delays blocking seller payouts. Mitigation: UI shows KYC status with clear next steps; KYC only required at first payout, not listing creation. Risk: Elasticsearch cost growth beyond MVP budget at scale. Mitigation: PostgreSQL full-text search fallback implemented behind feature flag for garment title/brand; Elasticsearch reserved for advanced filter combinations. Risk: Cloud Run cold start latency on payment endpoint exceeding user tolerance. Mitigation: payment-service configured with min-instances=1 to eliminate cold starts on the critical path. Tradeoff: Firestore for chat over custom WebSocket service reduces backend complexity but couples real-time feature to Firebase ecosystem.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Stripe PaymentIntents manual capture for escrow over Stripe Checkout (Checkout does not support manual capture). Confirmed Firestore for chat over Cloud Pub/Sub fanout (Firestore Security Rules enforce per-user access without backend relay). Selected Elasticsearch over Algolia for cost predictability at scale. Decided seller KYC gates payout not listing creation to maximize GMV.

metadata.notes: P2P fashion marketplace first pass; Stripe escrow, Firestore chat, Elasticsearch discovery, PCI SAQ A, GDPR compliance.

project_goal.value: Real-time marketplace analytics pipeline ingesting clickstream events from a large furniture e-commerce site, enriching with product and session data, streaming aggregated KPIs to a live merchandising dashboard
project_goal.rationale: Enable merchandising team to make same-day inventory and pricing decisions from live traffic signals

target_users.value: Merchandising analysts and category managers requiring sub-minute KPI refresh on product performance
target_users.rationale: Internal analytics consumers with real-time decision needs

project_class.rationale: Core deliverable is an event ingestion, enrichment, and aggregation pipeline

capabilities.value: data, batch_jobs, integrations, analytics, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Streaming ingest, batch reconciliation, Kafka integration, ClickHouse analytics, and Grafana real-time display

complexity_level.rationale: 10M+ daily clickstream events, sub-60s KPI latency requirement, ClickHouse at scale

risk_level.rationale: Internal system; wrong KPI data could cause bad merchandising decisions but no direct financial transaction risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Clickstream contains user session IDs but no PII after pseudonymization at ingest

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline and dashboards accessible only within corporate VPC

access_model.value: IAM roles for AWS service-to-service; Grafana with SSO via corporate IdP for dashboard access; no public endpoints
access_model.rationale: Internal system with role-based access to dashboards

feature_scope.value: Kafka clickstream ingest, Flink stream processing with session windowing, product catalog enrichment, 1-minute KPI aggregation to Redis, ClickHouse hot storage for last 30 days, S3 cold storage, nightly batch reconciliation for day-over-day metrics, Grafana merchandising dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Complete real-time analytics pipeline from event to dashboard

mvp_scope.value: Kafka -> Flink -> ClickHouse pipeline for product_impression and cart_add events with 1-minute rolling KPI aggregates in Redis; Grafana dashboard for top 100 products by impressions and cart-add rate
mvp_scope.rationale: Core merchandising signal for MVP validation

security_baseline.value: VPC-only endpoints, IAM role-based service access, TLS in transit between all pipeline components, user_id pseudonymized at Kafka producer before storage
security_baseline.rationale: Internal system with GDPR pseudonymization requirement for clickstream user identifiers

frontend_stack.value: none - data pipeline with Grafana dashboards; no custom frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: Grafana provides visualization layer without custom UI development

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Apache Flink 1.19; nightly batch via AWS EMR Serverless (Spark)
backend_stack.rationale: Flink is the industry standard for stateful stream processing; EMR Serverless for cost-effective nightly batch

data_platform.value: Kafka (MSK) for ingest, ClickHouse for hot analytics storage, S3 with Apache Iceberg for cold storage, Redis 7 for 1-minute KPI cache
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage provides sub-second aggregation queries on billions of event rows

hosting_target.value: AWS: MSK for Kafka, EMR Serverless for Flink and Spark, ClickHouse on EC2 r6g.2xlarge (2-node cluster), ElastiCache Redis 7, S3 for cold storage
hosting_target.rationale: AWS-native managed services minimize operational overhead; ClickHouse on EC2 for performance control

privacy_retention_policy.value: GDPR: user_id pseudonymized with HMAC-SHA256 keyed hash at Kafka producer; raw user_id never stored in pipeline; clickstream data retained 90 days in ClickHouse hot tier; Iceberg cold tier retained 3 years; deletion requests handled by key rotation (HMAC key rotation pseudonymizes historical data differently, effectively unlinking)
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR pseudonymization via key rotation satisfies erasure requirement without row-level deletes in columnar store

future_scope.value: ML-based demand forecasting from enriched clickstream, personalized recommendation signals, cross-category affinity analysis
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP use cases for the enriched data lake

constraints.value: KPI dashboard latency under 60 seconds end-to-end from event to Grafana display; ClickHouse query response under 2 seconds for standard merchandise dashboard; pipeline budget under $8K/month
constraints.rationale: SLA driven by merchandising team operational cadence

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for pipeline health; Flink web UI for job monitoring; CloudWatch for AWS resource metrics; PagerDuty alerts for consumer lag exceeding 5-minute threshold
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing corporate Grafana/Prometheus standard extended to pipeline

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure as code via Terraform; Flink jobs deployed as JAR to EMR Serverless Application; GitOps for pipeline config changes
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducible pipeline deployments with version-controlled config

project.goal: Real-time clickstream analytics pipeline for furniture e-commerce merchandising
project.target_users: Merchandising analysts and category managers

backend.batch: Apache Spark on EMR Serverless for nightly reconciliation

security.auth: IAM roles for service access
security.privacy: HMAC-SHA256 user_id pseudonymization at Kafka producer

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache

devops.hosting: AWS EMR Serverless + EC2 for ClickHouse

confirmed_decisions.kpi_cache: Redis sorted sets for real-time ranking

reasoner_reviews.product: The merchandising team's core use case is spotting underperforming categories within the same business day. The 60-second KPI latency SLA is well-chosen - faster would over-engineer the pipeline while slower would lose decision value. The nightly batch for day-over-day comparison addresses the second-most-requested reporting pattern.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Flink on EMR Serverless is the right choice for elastic stream processing without cluster management. The 64-partition Kafka topic matches the expected Flink parallelism for 10M daily events. ClickHouse on EC2 r6g.2xlarge gives more performance control than managed alternatives; the 2-node setup provides HA with ClickHouse's native replication.
reasoner_reviews.security: HMAC-SHA256 pseudonymization at the Kafka producer is the correct GDPR architecture for clickstream pipelines - it keeps the pipeline free of raw PII while allowing key rotation for effective erasure. The HMAC key must be stored in AWS Secrets Manager and rotated on deletion requests, not on a schedule.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget of $8K/month is achievable: EMR Serverless ~$1.5K, ClickHouse EC2 r6g.2xlarge 2-node ~$1.8K, MSK ~$1.2K, Redis ElastiCache ~$0.4K, S3 and data transfer ~$0.8K. Head room exists for Flink job compute spikes.

backend.service_design: Flink streaming application with three operator chains: (1) ClickstreamDeserializer (Kafka source, JSON -> ClickstreamEvent POJO, HMAC pseudonymization applied to session_id), (2) SessionWindowEnricher (keyed by pseudonymized_session_id, 30-minute session gap window, joins with product catalog broadcast state), (3) KpiAggregator (1-minute tumbling window computing impression_count, cart_add_count, add_to_cart_rate per product_id, writes to Redis sorted set and ClickHouse via JDBC sink).
backend.background_jobs: Nightly EMR Serverless Spark job at 02:00 UTC: reads previous day's ClickHouse enriched_events partition, computes day-over-day delta metrics per category (impressions_change_pct, cart_rate_change_pct, checkout_funnel_drop_rate), writes aggregated_kpi_snapshots to ClickHouse reporting layer.
backend.scaling_notes: Flink parallelism set to 32 for MVP (32 Kafka partitions consumed); auto-scaled EMR Serverless up to 64 vCPUs during peak (8pm-10pm site traffic). ClickHouse insert buffer tuned: max_insert_block_size=1M rows, async_insert=1.
backend.failure_handling: Flink checkpointing to S3 every 60 seconds. On job failure EMR Serverless restarts from last checkpoint; Kafka consumer group offset preserved. ClickHouse inserts use async_insert with deduplication on (event_id, product_id) to prevent double-counting on Flink replay.

security.auth_design: EMR Serverless uses IAM execution role with least-privilege: MSK read access on clickstream topic, S3 write on enriched-events prefix, ClickHouse JDBC credentials from Secrets Manager. Grafana access via corporate SSO (SAML) with read-only role for analysts.
security.privacy_controls: HMAC-SHA256 pseudonymization applied at Kafka producer using key from Secrets Manager. On GDPR deletion request: rotate HMAC key in Secrets Manager; historical records become effectively unlinked (pseudo_id changes on re-derivation). No row-level delete needed in ClickHouse.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail for AWS API calls. Flink job logs to CloudWatch with structured JSON. ClickHouse query_log table retained 30 days for audit of analyst query patterns.

data.entities: clickstream_events (event_id UUID, pseudo_session_id VARCHAR, event_type ENUM page_view/product_impression/cart_add/checkout_start/purchase, product_id VARCHAR, category VARCHAR, price_cents INT, event_ts TIMESTAMP) - ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(event_ts). session_windows (pseudo_session_id, session_start, session_end, page_view_count, products_viewed[], last_event_ts) - Flink state. product_impressions (product_id, impression_count_1m, cart_add_count_1m, add_to_cart_rate_1m, window_start_ts) - Redis sorted set by impression_count_1m + ClickHouse insert. aggregated_kpi_snapshots (snapshot_date, category, product_id, total_impressions, total_cart_adds, funnel_drop_rate, dod_impressions_change_pct) - ClickHouse reporting layer.
data.retention_and_deletion: ClickHouse hot tier: 90 days TTL on event_ts column. S3 Iceberg cold tier: 3 years. Redis KPI cache: 10-minute TTL per key (refreshed by Flink every 1 minute). HMAC key rotation for GDPR erasure.
data.consistency_notes: Flink exactly-once semantics via checkpointing + ClickHouse idempotent inserts on event_id. Nightly Spark job reads from ClickHouse (not Kafka) to ensure it operates on fully checkpointed data.

devops.deployment_topology: Terraform manages: MSK cluster (3 brokers, 64 partitions), EMR Serverless Application (Flink 1.19), EMR Serverless Spark Application, ClickHouse EC2 instances (r6g.2xlarge x2 behind NLB), ElastiCache Redis cluster, S3 buckets with lifecycle policies.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR runs Flink job unit tests (JUnit 5, FlinkMiniCluster), Terraform fmt/validate. On merge to main: mvn package produces Flink JAR, uploads to S3 artifacts bucket, triggers EMR Serverless job update via AWS CLI. Nightly Spark job submitted via AWS Step Functions cron trigger.
devops.rollback_strategy: Flink job rollback: redeploy previous JAR from S3 artifacts. Consumer group offset reset to pre-deployment checkpoint on data issues. ClickHouse schema migrations use ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN (non-blocking on MergeTree).

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse insert throughput bottleneck under peak load
ISS-001.detail: Initial JDBC sink wrote rows synchronously, causing backpressure in Flink pipeline during 8pm traffic peak (15K events/sec). Resolved by switching to async_insert with batching.

history.note: Load test revealed 3x backpressure in Flink watermark lag at 8pm peak
async_insert=1 and max_insert_block_size=1M configured; backpressure eliminated in re-test

ISS-002.title: Session window state unbounded growth in Flink
ISS-002.detail: Flink KeyedStream for session windowing retained state indefinitely for abandoned sessions, causing TaskManager OOM after 48 hours. Resolved by adding idle state TTL of 45 minutes.

history.note: TaskManager heap OOM observed in 48-hour staging run
StateTtlConfig with 45-minute TTL applied to session window keyed state

ISS-003.title: Nightly Spark job missing late-arriving events
ISS-003.detail: Events arriving after midnight UTC with event_ts from previous day are excluded from the nightly Spark reconciliation window, understating category metrics by up to 3%. Need a late-data watermark allowance in the batch job.

history.note: Auditor identified 2.8% metric undercount in staging data validation

ISS-004.title: HMAC key rotation procedure undocumented
ISS-004.detail: Runbook for GDPR deletion via HMAC key rotation was missing from operations documentation.

history.note: Security review flagged missing GDPR deletion runbook
Runbook added to ops wiki with step-by-step key rotation procedure

focus_issues.title: Nightly Spark job missing late-arriving events
focus_issues.detail: Events arriving after midnight with prior-day event_ts excluded from nightly reconciliation, understating category metrics by ~3%.

latest_recommendations: Add 2-hour late-data grace window to nightly Spark job by reading events where event_ts >= batch_date - INTERVAL 2 HOURS AND event_ts < batch_date + INTERVAL 2 HOURS
Use REPLACE INTO or ReplacingMergeTree deduplication in ClickHouse for re-processed events from late-data window
Document late-data SLA in ops runbook
