previous_plan.title: Home Goods Recommendation Vector Search Migration Prototype
previous_plan.executive_summary: Prototype comparing a fine-tuned all-MiniLM-L6-v2 ONNX embedding model + Qdrant HNSW vector store against legacy on-premise collaborative filter for home goods product recommendations. Shadow A/B routing, NDCG evaluation, latency benchmarking.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: EmbeddingService and RecommendationService on Cloud Run; Qdrant persistent Cloud Run container; shadow router duplicates legacy API read traffic. ISS-001 resolved: readiness check prevents cold-start request failures.
previous_plan.data_model: product_embeddings in Qdrant collection, recommendation_requests and latency_benchmarks in PostgreSQL, relevance_evaluation_sets for NDCG computation.
previous_plan.workflows: Embedding pipeline: product catalog from BigQuery -> EmbeddingService ONNX inference -> Qdrant upsert. Shadow routing: legacy API duplicates queries to RecommendationService. Weekly NDCG eval script.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses two methodology issues that could invalidate the migration decision. ISS-002 requires documenting the contrastive pair generation strategy - specifically the in-batch negative sampling with hard negative mining from BM25 candidates. ISS-003 requires a benchmark warm-up phase (20 requests) before p50/p95/p99 measurement with cold and warm latency reported separately in latency_benchmarks. Both fixes are in the evaluation tooling, not the inference architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Added TRAINING_DATA.md documentation to the fine-tuning repository documenting the contrastive pair generation strategy: positive pairs are (anchor_product_id, co-viewed_product_id) from BigQuery sessions where both products were viewed within the same 30-minute window. In-batch negatives: all other products in the training batch serve as implicit negatives (MultipleNegativesRankingLoss). Hard negative mining: for each anchor, retrieve top-10 BM25 candidates from product title+description index and include 3 random samples as hard negatives (not in the positive set). Added pair_generation.py script with inline docstrings, reproducible random seed (42), and a validation assertion that positive pair density is >0.8% of catalog cross-product space to detect data sparsity issues.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ISS-002 resolved; fine-tuning pipeline is now reproducible and the training data quality can be independently validated for the migration decision report.

fix_report.action_taken: Updated benchmark harness (benchmark.py) to send 20 warm-up requests to both EmbeddingService and RecommendationService before starting the timed measurement window. Warm-up requests are explicitly excluded from all latency percentile calculations. latency_benchmarks PostgreSQL table extended with warm_p50_ms, warm_p95_ms, warm_p99_ms, cold_p99_ms, warm_up_request_count columns. Benchmark report generator outputs a comparison table with separate warm and cold rows. Added assertion: benchmark run is invalid (raises BenchmarkValidationError) if warm_p99_ms > 50ms, indicating Qdrant index is not fully loaded.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ISS-003 resolved; latency comparison with legacy engine now uses warm steady-state measurements for both systems, producing a fair and defensible benchmark for the migration decision.

target_output.title: Home Goods Recommendation Vector Search Migration Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype on GCP Cloud Run comparing a fine-tuned all-MiniLM-L6-v2 ONNX embedding model with Qdrant HNSW vector similarity search against a legacy on-premise collaborative filter for home goods product recommendations. Shadow A/B routing sends read queries to both engines simultaneously without production impact. Latency benchmarks use a 20-request warm-up phase for fair comparison. Reproducible contrastive fine-tuning pipeline with documented in-batch negative and hard negative mining strategy. Weekly NDCG@10 evaluation against a held-out human-labeled relevance set produces the migration decision evidence package.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two stateless Cloud Run services handle inference: EmbeddingService (FastAPI, loads fine-tuned all-MiniLM-L6-v2 ONNX model from GCS at startup, 8-12ms p99 CPU inference) and RecommendationService (FastAPI, queries Qdrant top-k with cosine similarity). A third Cloud Run service runs Qdrant with min-instances=1 for warm index; GCS-backed CSI volume persists the HNSW collection across restarts; Cloud Run readiness check polls collection status before routing traffic. Shadow router middleware in the legacy API gateway duplicates product detail page recommendation queries to RecommendationService without user-facing impact. Experiment results (latency, NDCG, precision) stored in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL. Weekly evaluation Jupyter notebooks committed to Git with co-versioned benchmark results.
target_output.technology_stack: Inference: Python 3.12, FastAPI, sentence-transformers 2.7, ONNX Runtime 1.17 (CPU), optimum CLI for ONNX export. Vector store: Qdrant 1.9 on Cloud Run (HNSW m=16, ef_construction=100, ef=50 at query time, cosine metric). Fine-tuning: sentence-transformers MultipleNegativesRankingLoss, BM25 hard negative mining via rank_bm25. Data: BigQuery for session log ingestion, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-f1-micro) for experiment metadata. Storage: GCS for model artifacts, evaluation datasets, and experiment reports. Infrastructure: GCP Cloud Run, GitHub Actions CI, Prometheus + Grafana for service health.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Embedding pipeline: product catalog batch job reads 50K home goods items (title, description, category, price_tier) from BigQuery, calls EmbeddingService /embed, upserts 384-dim float32 vectors to Qdrant products_v1 collection with metadata payload. Recommendation inference: RecommendationService /recommend accepts query_product_id + top_k, fetches stored embedding from Qdrant by product_id, performs cosine similarity search, returns ranked product_id list with scores. Shadow A/B routing: legacy API gateway middleware sends parallel read request to RecommendationService; result logged to recommendation_requests table; user sees legacy result only. Latency benchmark harness: benchmark.py sends 20 warm-up requests, then 1000 timed queries, records warm p50/p95/p99 and cold p99 per engine in latency_benchmarks. Weekly NDCG evaluation: evaluation.py queries RecommendationService for each entry in relevance_evaluation_sets, computes NDCG@10 and precision@5, writes to evaluation_runs. Comparison report: Jupyter notebook joining latency_benchmarks, evaluation_runs, and ab_test_assignments for migration decision summary.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run EmbeddingService (FastAPI, ONNX Runtime, GCS model download at startup, /embed POST endpoint, min-instances=1 during benchmarks, 2vCPU 1GB). Cloud Run RecommendationService (FastAPI, Qdrant Python client, /recommend GET endpoint, 1vCPU 512MB). Cloud Run Qdrant service (Qdrant 1.9, 2vCPU 4GB, min-instances=1, GCS-backed CSI persistent volume, readiness check on /collections/products_v1 status). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-f1-micro, stores recommendation_requests, latency_benchmarks, relevance_evaluation_sets, evaluation_runs, ab_test_assignments). GCS buckets: model-artifacts/all-minilm-finetuned/ (ONNX model weights), evaluation-datasets/ (relevance annotation CSV), experiment-results/ (JSON benchmark exports). Shadow router middleware (Python class injected into legacy FastAPI gateway, sends non-blocking background request to RecommendationService using httpx.AsyncClient). BM25 hard negative mining script (rank_bm25, generates pair_generation_output.parquet committed to GCS). Prometheus + Grafana (Cloud Run metrics: request latency histograms per service, Qdrant query_duration_seconds, embedding inference duration).
target_output.workflows: Product catalog embedding and Qdrant indexing: BigQuery export job reads product catalog (50K rows); catalog_embedder.py batches 256 products per call to EmbeddingService /embed; upserts vectors to Qdrant products_v1 collection with metadata (sku, category, price_tier); logs product_embeddings record to PostgreSQL; full run completes in ~35 minutes at 8-12ms per embedding. Benchmark run with warm-up (ISS-003 fix): benchmark.py sets min-instances=1 on EmbeddingService and RecommendationService via GCP API; sends 20 warm-up requests discarded from metrics; sends 1000 timed /recommend requests for random query products; records warm_p50_ms, warm_p95_ms, warm_p99_ms, cold_p99_ms (first request after pod scale event) to latency_benchmarks; simultaneously runs same query set against legacy engine via HTTP; writes comparison JSON to GCS experiment-results/. Reproducible fine-tuning pipeline (ISS-002 fix): pair_generation.py reads 3-month session logs from BigQuery; extracts co-view positive pairs within 30-minute session windows; generates BM25 hard negatives using rank_bm25 on product title+description corpus; validates positive pair density >0.8%; outputs pair_generation_output.parquet with random seed 42; fine_tune.py trains all-MiniLM-L6-v2 with MultipleNegativesRankingLoss for 3 epochs on 4xA100 via Vertex AI training job; exports to ONNX via optimum; uploads to GCS model-artifacts/.
target_output.data_model: product_embeddings: Qdrant collection 'products_v1', HNSW index (m=16, ef_construction=100), cosine distance, 384 dimensions; payload fields (sku STRING, title STRING, category STRING, price_tier ENUM budget/mid/premium, indexed_at DATETIME). recommendation_requests (id UUID PK, query_product_id VARCHAR, top_k INT, latency_ms FLOAT, result_product_ids TEXT[], score_vector FLOAT[], engine ENUM qdrant/legacy, is_shadow BOOLEAN, experiment_run_id UUID, created_at TIMESTAMP). relevance_evaluation_sets (id UUID PK, query_product_id VARCHAR, relevant_product_ids TEXT[], annotation_method ENUM human_label/click_through, annotator VARCHAR, created_at). ab_test_assignments (pseudo_session_id VARCHAR, engine ENUM qdrant/legacy, assigned_at TIMESTAMP). latency_benchmarks (run_id UUID PK, engine ENUM qdrant/legacy, model_version VARCHAR, warm_p50_ms FLOAT, warm_p95_ms FLOAT, warm_p99_ms FLOAT, cold_p99_ms FLOAT, warm_up_request_count INT, timed_request_count INT, queries_per_second FLOAT, run_at TIMESTAMP). evaluation_runs (run_id UUID PK, model_version VARCHAR, ndcg_at_10 FLOAT, precision_at_5 FLOAT, query_count INT, evaluated_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: EmbeddingService: POST /embed { products: [{product_id, title, description}], model_version: str } -> { embeddings: [[float32 x 384]], latency_ms: float }. POST /embed/batch (bulk, up to 256 products per call). GET /health (returns model_loaded: bool, model_version, onnx_session_ready). RecommendationService: GET /recommend?product_id=&top_k=10&include_scores=true -> { recommendations: [{product_id, score}], latency_ms, engine: 'qdrant' }. GET /health (returns qdrant_collection_ready: bool, vector_count). Qdrant direct: accessible only by RecommendationService via internal Cloud Run URL. PostgreSQL accessed directly by Python scripts (no REST wrapper needed for prototype).
target_output.security_and_compliance: VPC isolation: all Cloud Run services use VPC connector restricting ingress to internal GCP project traffic only. Service account tokens: shadow router uses GCP metadata server workload identity to obtain tokens for RecommendationService calls. GCS access: model-artifacts bucket uses IAM binding to EmbeddingService service account (roles/storage.objectViewer only). BigQuery access: session log export uses read-only service account (roles/bigquery.dataViewer on specific dataset). No customer PII: user_id replaced with pseudo_session_id (HMAC-SHA256 with project-scoped key) before BigQuery export; pseudo_session_id stored in ab_test_assignments only for experiment correlation. VPC Service Controls: applied to GCS and BigQuery restricting access to project service accounts.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: on PR runs pytest for EmbeddingService, RecommendationService, evaluation scripts, and benchmark harness; includes BenchmarkValidationError test asserting warm-up exclusion logic. On merge to main: Docker build for each service, push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy. Model fine-tuning: triggered via GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch with inputs (epochs, learning_rate, negative_mining_strategy); runs Vertex AI custom training job; uploads ONNX to GCS; creates model_version record in PostgreSQL. Qdrant reindex: triggered manually after new model version; catalog_embedder.py run creates new Qdrant collection version (products_v{n+1}) while old collection serves traffic; atomic collection alias swap on completion. Experiment results committed to Git: benchmark JSON + evaluation Jupyter notebooks versioned alongside the code that produced them.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from FastAPI instrumentator: embedding_inference_duration_seconds histogram (expected p99 8-12ms), recommendation_latency_seconds histogram (Qdrant query + score fetch), shadow_request_errors_total counter. Grafana dashboard: embedding latency p50/p95/p99 vs 20ms budget, Qdrant query latency vs 10ms budget, shadow error rate, evaluation NDCG@10 trend across model versions. Cloud Run built-in metrics: request count, latency, instance count (monitors cold start frequency). Weekly evaluation Jupyter notebooks exported to HTML and committed to experiment-results/ Git branch with tagged model version and benchmark run_id for traceability.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Monthly estimate during 8-week prototype: Cloud Run EmbeddingService 2vCPU min-1 ~$40/month, RecommendationService 1vCPU min-0 ~$15/month active, Qdrant 2vCPU min-1 ~$50/month, Cloud SQL db-f1-micro ~$7/month, GCS model/data storage ~$10/month, BigQuery query costs ~$5/month. Total ~$127/month active, ~$70/month idle between experiments. Well within $1.2K/month budget with headroom for Vertex AI fine-tuning job (~$80 per training run on 4xA100 for 3 epochs).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Infrastructure and baseline (weeks 1-2): Cloud Run service scaffolding, Qdrant setup with GCS persistence and readiness check, PostgreSQL schema, GitHub Actions CI, baseline latency benchmark vs legacy engine with warm-up phase (ISS-003 fix). Phase 2 - Fine-tuning and embedding (weeks 3-5): BigQuery session log export with pseudonymization, pair_generation.py with documented BM25 hard negative mining (ISS-002 fix), Vertex AI fine-tuning job, ONNX export, catalog embedding run (50K products), Qdrant reindex. Phase 3 - Evaluation and report (weeks 6-8): shadow routing activation, NDCG@10 and precision@5 evaluation against human-labeled relevance set, latency benchmark comparison (warm p99 vs legacy), ab_test_assignments analysis, Jupyter notebook migration decision report with recommendation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with ruff linter and mypy strict. pytest for all service and evaluation script tests; benchmark.py must have unit test asserting warm_up_request_count is excluded from latency_percentiles. All experiment parameters (top_k, HNSW ef, model_version, benchmark_query_count, warm_up_count) externalized to config.yaml; never hardcoded. pair_generation.py must accept --seed argument defaulting to 42 for reproducibility; outputs parquet with pair generation metadata JSON sidecar. All benchmark runs and evaluation runs assigned a UUID run_id; results stored in PostgreSQL with git commit SHA for traceability. Jupyter notebooks cleared of output before commit; results exported to HTML sidecar committed alongside.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Fine-tuned model underperforms generic all-MiniLM-L6-v2 on NDCG due to insufficient contrastive training data. Mitigation: pair density validation in pair_generation.py; fallback evaluation with generic model as sanity check. Risk: Qdrant GCS-backed volume CSI driver instability on Cloud Run causing index corruption. Mitigation: weekly Qdrant backup script copies collection snapshot to GCS archive; reindex from product catalog as fallback (35-minute recovery). Tradeoff: Cloud Run Qdrant over managed vector DB (Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud) - chosen for cost control and self-hosting alignment with production migration target (GKE Qdrant cluster); accepted operational risk of CSI persistence vs managed service reliability. Tradeoff: ONNX CPU inference over GPU - Cloud Run GPU is in preview; CPU with ONNX meets the 20ms embedding budget at 50K catalog scale; GPU needed only if catalog exceeds 500K products.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed NDCG@10 as primary relevance metric over MRR because home goods search has multiple relevant substitutes per query, not a single correct answer. Confirmed in-batch negatives with BM25 hard mining over random-only negatives to avoid false negatives from popular products appearing as implicit negatives across multiple training pairs. Decided Qdrant cosine metric over dot product to normalize for varying embedding magnitudes from fine-tuning.

metadata.notes: Research prototype round 2; ISS-002 contrastive pair generation documented; ISS-003 warm-up benchmark harness added for fair latency comparison.

project.goal: TypeScript SDK for Shopify Admin GraphQL API with typed builders, pagination, and retry
project.target_users: Shopify app developers on Node 20+, Bun 1.x

backend.framework: tsup bundle, vitest tests, changesets versioning

security.auth: OAuth2 access token passthrough only

data.db: none
data.cache: in-memory rate-limit window state per client instance

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with matrix testing on Node 20, Node 22, Bun 1.x

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK scope is well-defined for the graphql-first Shopify ecosystem. Typed query builders reduce the risk of malformed GraphQL documents and give IDE autocompletion — a genuine DX improvement over raw fetch calls. The async iterator pattern for pagination is the right abstraction level.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Dual ESM/CJS output via tsup is the correct approach given the mixed Node and Bun consumer landscape. Rate-limit state must be scoped per ShopifyClient instance to avoid cross-shop contamination in multi-tenant app servers. The retry module should be its own internal module to allow independent testing.
reasoner_reviews.security: Token passthrough is the right boundary — the SDK must never cache or log access tokens. Query builder parameter validation should guard against GraphQL injection via template literal misuse. The observable event system should never emit token values in event payloads.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero production dependency constraint beyond graphql-tag is aggressive but achievable given the narrow scope. Tree-shakeable ESM is mandatory for Remix and Next.js consumers who bundle at the page level. Node 20 minimum is appropriate given LTS support timelines.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan underspecified the rate-limit window reset logic — Shopify's leaky bucket model requires tracking available capacity and bucket size, not just counting 429 responses. The async iterator must correctly handle partial page fetches that return fewer items than the requested page size.

backend.service_design: ShopifyClient class is the public entry point accepting ShopifyClientConfig with accessToken, shop domain, and apiVersion. Internal modules: GraphQLExecutor (raw fetch + response parsing), RetryOrchestrator (backoff schedule, jitter), PaginationIterator (cursor tracking, page assembly), RateLimitTracker (per-instance leaky bucket capacity state), EventBus (typed EventEmitter wrapper).
backend.api_patterns: Typed query builder functions: products(args: ProductQueryArgs): GraphQLOperation, orders(args: OrderQueryArgs): GraphQLOperation. Each returns an operation object executable via client.execute(op) for single fetches or client.paginate(op) returning AsyncIterator<ProductPage>. No dynamic string concatenation — all queries use gql tag literals.
backend.failure_handling: RetryOrchestrator implements exponential backoff starting at 1s, doubling to max 32s with plus-or-minus 25% jitter. Retries on 429 (respects Retry-After header when present) and 500/502/503. Non-retryable errors (400, 401, 403) throw immediately. Max 5 retry attempts configurable via client options.
backend.backend_risks: Risk: Shopify API version deprecation breaking typed builders. Mitigation: apiVersion is a required config field and type definitions are generated per API version from the Shopify introspection schema. Risk: consumers on Bun may encounter streaming response differences. Mitigation: CI matrix includes Bun 1.x integration tests against Shopify partner sandbox.

security.auth_design: Access token injected into X-Shopify-Access-Token header at request time from caller-supplied config. Token never stored in module scope or emitted in events. Debug logging mode explicitly redacts authorization headers.
security.secrets_management: No secrets managed by SDK. ShopifyClientConfig is caller-owned; SDK documentation explicitly warns against hardcoding tokens and recommends environment variable injection.
security.security_risks: Risk: query builder accepts raw filter strings that could be GraphQL-injected. Mitigation: filter argument types are union literals or structured objects, never free-form strings in generated queries.

data.entities: graphql_operations (typed query/mutation definitions per API entity), query_builders (product, order, customer, metafield typed builder functions), pagination_cursors (in-memory cursor state per active AsyncIterator), rate_limit_state (per-ShopifyClient instance: currentlyAvailable int, maximumAvailable int, restoreRate number, lastUpdatedAt Date), webhook_schemas (Zod schemas for order/create, product/update, refund/create webhook payloads), api_client_config (accessToken, shop, apiVersion, retryOptions, timeoutMs)
data.schema_notes: All types generated from Shopify Admin GraphQL 2024-01 introspection schema using graphql-codegen at build time. Generated types committed to repo and re-generated on API version bump.
data.data_risks: Risk: stale generated types if API version is bumped without re-running codegen. Mitigation: CI enforces codegen output is up-to-date via git diff --exit-code on generated files.

devops.deployment_topology: No hosted infrastructure. GitHub Actions pipeline: typecheck (tsc --noEmit), lint (ESLint), test (vitest with Node 20, Node 22, Bun 1.x matrix), build (tsup ESM + CJS), publish (npm publish --tag latest on semver tag).
devops.ci_cd_design: Changesets for version management: PRs include changeset files, release PR is auto-created by changeset bot, merge triggers npm publish. Provenance attestation via npm provenance flag for supply chain transparency.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: accidental major version publish. Mitigation: publish workflow requires manual approval step for major versions.

ISS-001.title: Rate-limit state contamination in multi-tenant app servers
ISS-001.detail: Previous plan scoped rate-limit state globally per module, causing capacity tracking to bleed across shops in apps serving multiple merchants simultaneously.

history.note: GlobalRateLimitStore singleton identified as root cause

ISS-002.title: Async iterator does not handle partial final page
ISS-002.detail: When Shopify returns a page with fewer items than requested page size and hasNextPage=false, the iterator was previously yielding an empty final page instead of stopping at the last non-empty page.

history.note: Off-by-one in cursor advance logic

ISS-003.title: Retry-After header not respected on 429 responses
ISS-003.detail: Retry logic was using fixed backoff schedule even when Shopify sends a Retry-After header with the exact wait duration, causing unnecessary delay or premature retry.

history.note: RetryOrchestrator did not parse Retry-After header

focus_issues.title: Async iterator does not handle partial final page
focus_issues.detail: When Shopify returns a page with fewer items than requested page size and hasNextPage=false, the iterator was previously yielding an empty final page instead of stopping at the last non-empty page.

focus_issues.title: Retry-After header not respected on 429 responses
focus_issues.detail: Retry logic was using fixed backoff schedule even when Shopify sends a Retry-After header with the exact wait duration, causing unnecessary delay or premature retry.

latest_recommendations: Fix pagination iterator to stop on non-empty partial page rather than continuing to fetch
Parse Retry-After header in RetryOrchestrator and use it as the wait duration when present
Add integration tests against Shopify partner sandbox for both issues
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Shopify Admin GraphQL SDK v1 Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two medium issues unresolved affecting core pagination and retry reliability

previous_audits.summary: Solid SDK architecture with correct TypeScript-first design and dual ESM/CJS output. Rate-limit scoping fix improves multi-tenant safety. Two correctness issues in pagination and retry remain unresolved.
strengths: Per-instance rate-limit state correctly prevents cross-shop contamination
Typed query builders with gql tag literals prevent dynamic query injection
Zero production dependency constraint met
concerns: Async iterator yields empty final page when Shopify returns partial last page
Retry-After header ignored causing suboptimal retry timing
blocking_issues: Pagination iterator partial page bug causes data duplication or missed records in SDK consumers
recommendations: In PaginationIterator, after receiving a page, check if edges.length > 0 before yielding; stop when hasNextPage=false regardless of edge count
In RetryOrchestrator, parse response.headers.get('Retry-After') and use Math.max(parsedRetryAfter * 1000, baseBackoff) as the wait duration

issue_updates.title: Async iterator partial page bug
issue_updates.detail: PaginationIterator.next() yields empty arrays on final page when item count < pageSize

issue_updates.title: Retry-After header not parsed
issue_updates.detail: RetryOrchestrator uses fixed exponential schedule, ignoring Shopify-provided Retry-After values

previous_plan.title: Shopify Admin GraphQL SDK v1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A zero-dependency TypeScript SDK for the Shopify Admin GraphQL API providing typed query builders for Products, Orders, and Customers with cursor-based async iterator pagination and exponential backoff retry. Distributed as a dual ESM/CJS npm package compatible with Node 20+, Bun, and Deno.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Modular TypeScript library with ShopifyClient as public entry point delegating to internal GraphQLExecutor, RetryOrchestrator, PaginationIterator, and a global RateLimitStore (since replaced with per-instance state). Published via tsup to npm with full TypeScript declarations.
previous_plan.data_model: graphql_operations typed per entity, query_builders for product/order/customer, pagination_cursors in global store (ISS-001 root cause), rate_limit_state global singleton (resolved), webhook_schemas as Zod types.
previous_plan.workflows: Single-page fetch and paginated async iteration workflows defined but pagination stop condition had off-by-one bug.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the Shopify Admin GraphQL SDK to fix two correctness issues from round 1: PaginationIterator must stop yielding when it receives a partial final page (hasNextPage=false), and RetryOrchestrator must parse and respect the Retry-After response header before falling back to computed backoff. Both fixes are self-contained to their respective internal modules and require targeted integration tests against the Shopify partner sandbox. Per-instance rate-limit state from round 1 is retained.

fix_report.action_taken: Updated PaginationIterator.next() to check edges.length > 0 before yielding and to call return() when hasNextPage=false, ensuring the async iterator terminates cleanly on a partial final page without emitting an empty page to the caller.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SDK consumers using for-await-of loops receive exactly the records returned by Shopify with no trailing empty pages or premature stops.

fix_report.action_taken: Updated RetryOrchestrator to extract the Retry-After header from 429 responses and compute waitMs = Math.max(parseInt(retryAfterHeader) * 1000, minimumBackoffMs) before sleeping, falling back to exponential backoff with jitter only when the header is absent.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SDK retries align with Shopify declared availability window, reducing unnecessary wait time when Retry-After is short and preventing premature retry when the server requests a longer wait.

target_output.title: Shopify Admin GraphQL SDK — Architecture Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A zero-production-dependency TypeScript SDK for the Shopify Admin GraphQL API delivering typed query builders for Products, Orders, Customers, and Metafields with a fixed cursor-based async iterator and Retry-After-aware exponential backoff. Distributed as a dual ESM/CJS npm package via npm registry, compatible with Node 20+, Bun 1.x, and Deno. This revision corrects pagination termination logic and retry header handling identified in round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Package-first architecture with ShopifyClient as the sole public class, instantiated per shop with an OAuth2 access token, target shop domain, and API version string. Internal module graph: GraphQLExecutor handles raw fetch to the Shopify Admin GraphQL endpoint with header injection and response parsing; RetryOrchestrator wraps each execution with Retry-After-aware backoff and jitter; PaginationIterator consumes GraphQLExecutor to build AsyncIterator<T[]> with correct partial-page termination; RateLimitTracker maintains per-instance leaky bucket state; EventBus emits typed events for rate_limit_hit, retry_attempt, and request_complete. All types are generated from Shopify Admin GraphQL 2024-01 introspection schema via graphql-codegen and committed to the repo.
target_output.technology_stack: TypeScript 5.5 with strict mode. tsup for dual ESM/CJS bundle with .d.ts declarations. vitest for unit and integration tests with Node 20, Node 22, and Bun 1.x matrix. graphql-tag as the only production dependency. changesets for semantic versioning and automated npm publish. ESLint with @typescript-eslint. GitHub Actions for CI. Zod for webhook payload schema validation (devDependency only, not bundled in core package).
target_output.functional_feature_map: ShopifyClient instantiation with ShopifyClientConfig (accessToken, shop, apiVersion, retryOptions). Typed query builders: products(args) for listings with variant expansion, orders(args) for order line items and fulfillments, customers(args) for profile and address data, metafields(args, ownerType) for namespace-scoped custom data. client.execute(op) for single GraphQL fetches returning typed results. client.paginate(op) returning AsyncIterator<T[]> with correct partial-page stop. client.on('retry_attempt', handler) event subscription for logging. Webhook schema validators for order/create, product/update, refund/create payloads via Zod parse.
target_output.system_components: ShopifyClient (public class, config validation, module orchestration). GraphQLExecutor (fetch to https://shop/admin/api/version/graphql.json, request body serialization, response error parsing from GraphQL errors array). RetryOrchestrator (retry loop with configurable maxAttempts, reads Retry-After header on 429, falls back to exponential backoff 1s/2s/4s/8s/16s/32s with plus-or-minus 25% jitter, emits retry_attempt event). PaginationIterator (cursor tracking via pageInfo.endCursor, yields only when edges.length > 0, terminates cleanly when hasNextPage=false). RateLimitTracker (per-instance leaky bucket: tracks throttleStatus.currentlyAvailable and maximumAvailable from Shopify extensions field). EventBus (typed EventEmitter wrapping retry_attempt, rate_limit_hit, request_complete payloads — no token values in any payload).
target_output.workflows: Product Pagination: Developer calls client.paginate(products({first:50, query:'status:active'})). PaginationIterator sends query with after:null, receives first page edges and pageInfo. If edges.length > 0, yields the page. If hasNextPage=true, advances cursor to endCursor and fetches next page. If hasNextPage=false after yielding the final non-empty page, iterator calls return() — no empty page is emitted. Rate-Limited Retry: GraphQLExecutor receives 429 from Shopify. RetryOrchestrator reads Retry-After header; if present (e.g. value '2'), waits 2000ms; if absent, computes backoff = min(baseDelay * 2^attempt, 32000) * jitter. Emits retry_attempt event with attempt number and waitMs. After wait, re-executes the same GraphQL operation. On success, emits request_complete. After maxAttempts (default 5), throws ShopifyRateLimitError.
target_output.data_model: graphql_operations (TypeScript interface per entity: ProductNode with id, title, handle, variants, status; OrderNode with id, name, lineItems, financialStatus, fulfillmentStatus; CustomerNode with id, email, addresses, ordersCount; MetafieldNode with id, namespace, key, value, type). query_builders (ProductQueryArgs with query string union, first/after cursor, sortKey enum; OrderQueryArgs with financial_status filter, date range). pagination_cursors (per-AsyncIterator state: currentCursor string or null, hasNextPage boolean, pageSize number). rate_limit_state (per-ShopifyClient instance: currentlyAvailable number, maximumAvailable number, restoreRate number, lastUpdatedAt Date). webhook_schemas (Zod schemas: OrderCreateWebhook with id, line_items array, customer, total_price; ProductUpdateWebhook with id, variants array, status). api_client_config (accessToken string, shop string, apiVersion literal union, retryOptions RetryConfig, timeoutMs number).
target_output.api_design: Public API surface (package exports): ShopifyClient class with constructor(config: ShopifyClientConfig), execute<T>(op: GraphQLOperation<T>): Promise<T>, paginate<T>(op: GraphQLOperation<T>): AsyncIterableIterator<T[]>, on(event, handler): this, off(event, handler): this. Query builders: products(args: ProductQueryArgs): GraphQLOperation<ProductConnection>, orders(args: OrderQueryArgs): GraphQLOperation<OrderConnection>, customers(args: CustomerQueryArgs): GraphQLOperation<CustomerConnection>, metafields(args, ownerType): GraphQLOperation<MetafieldConnection>. Webhook validators: validateOrderCreate(payload: unknown): OrderCreateWebhook (throws ZodError on invalid shape). Error types: ShopifyGraphQLError, ShopifyRateLimitError, ShopifyNetworkError.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Access token injected as X-Shopify-Access-Token header at request execution time from caller-supplied config field; token never stored in module scope, closure, or EventBus payloads. Debug mode flag redacts all authorization headers from logged request objects. Query builder argument types are TypeScript union literals and structured interfaces — no free-form string interpolation into GraphQL query bodies, preventing injection via builder misuse. Webhook validation uses Zod strict parsing to reject unexpected fields. npm provenance attestation on publish via GitHub Actions OIDC for supply chain transparency.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline triggered on push to main and on semver tags. Jobs: (1) typecheck — tsc --noEmit with strict tsconfig; (2) lint — ESLint with @typescript-eslint/recommended; (3) test — vitest run with matrix strategy node-version [20, 22] and bun-version [1.x]; (4) build — tsup src/index.ts generating dist/index.mjs (ESM), dist/index.cjs (CJS), dist/index.d.ts; (5) publish — on tag push, runs npm publish --provenance --access public. Changesets bot opens a release PR when changeset files accumulate; merge triggers publish job. Major version tags require manual approval gate in GitHub Actions environment. Package.json exports map: import points to dist/index.mjs, require points to dist/index.cjs, types points to dist/index.d.ts.
target_output.observability: EventBus emits three observable event types: retry_attempt ({attemptNumber, waitMs, statusCode, operationName}), rate_limit_hit ({currentlyAvailable, maximumAvailable, shop}), request_complete ({operationName, durationMs, rateLimitConsumed}). Consumers subscribe via client.on('retry_attempt', handler) for integration into their existing logging stack. SDK emits no telemetry itself — all observability is opt-in via event subscriptions. vitest coverage report tracked in CI; target 90% line coverage on internal modules.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero ongoing infrastructure cost — npm registry hosting is free for public packages. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI for open-source. Per-instance RateLimitTracker scales horizontally with the caller's app server instances; each ShopifyClient instance independently tracks its own leaky bucket state. High-throughput apps serving thousands of concurrent shop sessions should pool ShopifyClient instances per shop using a Map<shopDomain, ShopifyClient> pattern documented in the SDK README.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 v1.0.0 (complete in this revision): ShopifyClient with Products and Orders query builders, pagination iterator with partial-page fix (ISS-002 resolved), Retry-After-aware retry (ISS-003 resolved), EventBus observability, dual ESM/CJS npm publish with provenance. Phase 2 v1.1.0 (4 weeks): Customers and Metafields query builders, webhook schema validators for order/create and product/update, integration test suite against Shopify partner sandbox on Node 20 and Bun 1.x. Phase 3 v1.2.0 (4 weeks): Bulk operation helpers for large dataset exports via Shopify bulk query API, REST Admin API client as separate entry point, React hooks package as @shopify-sdk/react-hooks for Polaris app developers.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enforced via tsconfig with noUncheckedIndexedAccess. vitest for all tests; unit tests per internal module in src/__tests__/, integration tests in tests/integration/ requiring SHOPIFY_PARTNER_SANDBOX_TOKEN env var. 90% line coverage gate in CI. changesets required for all PRs modifying public API surface. Conventional commits (feat:, fix:, docs:) enforced via commitlint. No default exports — all public symbols are named exports from src/index.ts. graphql-tag literals only in query builder implementations.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Shopify Admin GraphQL API version deprecation breaks generated types. Mitigation: apiVersion is a required constructor parameter; codegen re-runs on version bump with CI diff check enforcing committed output is current. Risk: consumers sharing a single ShopifyClient instance across shops see incorrect rate-limit tracking. Mitigation: documented that ShopifyClient must be instantiated per shop with Map-based pooling pattern in README. Tradeoff: graphql-tag as sole production dependency adds ~7KB to consumer bundles but is already present in most Shopify app stacks. Tradeoff: Zod for webhook validation is a devDependency only — consumers who want runtime webhook parsing must install Zod separately, keeping core bundle size zero-overhead for non-webhook use cases.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed tsup over rollup for simpler dual ESM/CJS config. Chose vitest over jest for native ESM support without transform config. Decided Zod webhook schemas are devDependency only to preserve zero-production-dep constraint on core. Partial-page fix confirmed: iterator stops when hasNextPage=false after yielding last non-empty page, not when edges.length drops below pageSize.

metadata.notes: Shopify Admin GraphQL SDK revision fixing pagination partial-page termination and Retry-After header handling.

project_goal.value: Seller performance analytics web app for a handmade crafts marketplace using ML-driven demand forecasting to suggest optimal pricing, restock timing, and promotional windows for individual shop owners
project_goal.rationale: Core product goal: empower craft sellers with ML-backed business intelligence

target_users.value: Individual handmade craft shop owners on the marketplace platform seeking data-driven guidance for pricing and restocking decisions
target_users.rationale: Primary end users are sellers, not buyers or platform admins

project_class.rationale: Dashboard web application with server-side ML pipeline, no mobile-first requirement

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full analytics stack with ML forecasting pipeline and nightly batch jobs

complexity_level.rationale: Per-product ML model training, ClickHouse analytics, and multi-source data pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Seller sales data is commercially sensitive but no financial PCI data processed

data_sensitivity.rationale: Seller sales metrics are internal marketplace data, not customer PII

external_exposure.rationale: Dashboard accessible only to authenticated shop owners via Cognito session

access_model.value: Cognito JWT-based session; each seller scoped to their own shop_id; no cross-seller data access
access_model.rationale: Data isolation per shop owner is a hard requirement

feature_scope.value: Demand forecast dashboard per product, pricing suggestion cards with confidence intervals, restock alert feed, seasonal pattern visualizations, promo window calendar, seller feedback on suggestions (accept/dismiss/snooze)
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for seller analytics MVP and beyond

mvp_scope.value: Demand forecast per product with pricing suggestions and restock alerts; nightly batch model training pipeline; seller feedback capture
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for Q3 seller beta program

security_baseline.value: Cognito JWT auth, shop_id row-level security on all queries, TLS 1.3 in transit, no cross-seller data leakage
security_baseline.rationale: Seller data isolation is non-negotiable

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Recharts for data visualization
frontend_stack.rationale: SSR capabilities and React ecosystem for charting components

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; scikit-learn for demand forecasting models
backend_stack.rationale: Python ML ecosystem alignment with FastAPI for API layer

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for transactional data; ClickHouse for analytics time-series; Redis 7 for caching forecast results
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse handles high-volume sales history queries at seller dashboard load time

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1; S3 for model artifacts; ECS scheduled tasks for nightly batch jobs
hosting_target.rationale: Fargate containers for API and batch workers with S3 model storage

privacy_retention_policy.value: Seller sales history retained for 36 months for model training; forecast outputs retained 6 months; seller feedback logs retained 12 months
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Model training requires multi-year history; forecast outputs have shorter business value

future_scope.value: Cross-seller benchmark comparisons (anonymized), marketplace-wide trend alerts, mobile app for seller push notifications
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 expansion items requiring anonymized aggregation capabilities

constraints.value: Model training must complete within 4-hour nightly window; dashboard p95 load time under 2s; AWS us-east-1 only
constraints.rationale: Nightly window driven by marketplace data export schedule

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on FastAPI service; CloudWatch for ECS batch job metrics; custom metrics for model training duration and forecast confidence distribution
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard plus ML-specific metrics

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints; separate ML pipeline and API teams; model releases gated by accuracy regression tests
execution_preference.rationale: ML pipeline cadence decoupled from API feature cadence

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o used to generate natural-language explanations of forecast recommendations displayed below each suggestion card; explanations are pre-generated during nightly batch and cached in Redis
llm_integration.rationale: Natural language explanation increases seller trust in ML recommendations

project.goal: ML-driven seller analytics with demand forecasting and pricing recommendations
project.target_users: Handmade crafts marketplace shop owners

frontend.charts: Recharts for forecast timelines and seasonal patterns

backend.ml: scikit-learn Prophet-inspired demand models per product

security.auth: Cognito user pools
security.row_security: shop_id scoping on all DB queries

data.cache: Redis 7 for forecast output cache

devops.batch: ECS Scheduled Tasks for nightly forecasting

reasoner_reviews.product: The demand forecasting angle is a strong differentiator for craft marketplace sellers who typically lack data tooling. Surfacing confidence intervals alongside pricing suggestions reduces over-reliance on low-confidence forecasts. The accept/dismiss/snooze feedback loop is essential for model improvement and creates a virtuous retraining cycle.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Per-product model training is the right granularity for a crafts marketplace where each shop's product mix has unique seasonal patterns. ClickHouse partitioned by shop_id and product_id will handle the trailing 24-month sales history scans without impacting PostgreSQL transactional performance. Pre-generating LLM explanations during nightly batch prevents cold-path GPT-4o latency from blocking dashboard loads.
reasoner_reviews.security: Row-level security via shop_id on all ClickHouse and PostgreSQL queries must be enforced in the FastAPI service layer — the API must never accept shop_id from the request body, always deriving it from the Cognito JWT claims to prevent horizontal data exposure between sellers.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour nightly batch window is achievable for 200 pilot sellers but will require parallelization via ECS task concurrency as the seller base grows. Redis caching of forecast outputs and LLM explanations is critical for dashboard load times within the 2s p95 target.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The round 1 plan did not specify how model accuracy regression is detected before deploying new forecast models to production. Without a holdout evaluation step, a degraded model could silently serve worse recommendations to sellers. The batch pipeline must include a validation gate.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with /dashboard/[shopId] dynamic segment. Server Components fetch forecast summaries server-side; client-side Recharts components hydrate with cached data. Route-level loading.tsx for skeleton states during forecast data fetches.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard home: product grid with demand trend sparklines and color-coded restock urgency badges. Product detail: 90-day forecast chart with confidence interval bands, pricing suggestion card with accept/dismiss/snooze actions, seasonal pattern heatmap. Promo calendar: month view with suggested promotional windows highlighted. Alert feed: time-sorted restock alerts with product image and days-until-stockout countdown.
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server state with 5-minute stale time on forecast data. Optimistic updates for seller feedback submissions (accept/dismiss/snooze reflect immediately in UI before API confirmation). No global client state store needed.
frontend.performance_notes: Recharts components loaded lazily per route. Forecast chart data pre-fetched server-side in Next.js page component. Redis-cached forecast JSON served via FastAPI in under 50ms for 95th percentile dashboard loads.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with routers: /forecasts (per-product forecast retrieval), /suggestions (pricing and restock suggestions), /feedback (seller accept/dismiss/snooze), /alerts (restock alert feed). ML pipeline as separate ECS Scheduled Task invoking train_models.py and generate_forecasts.py scripts.
backend.business_modules: ForecastService retrieves pre-computed ClickHouse forecast rows and Redis-cached LLM explanations. SuggestionEngine applies business rules to forecast outputs (pricing suggestions derived from demand percentile thresholds, restock alerts triggered when days_of_stock < demand_days_forecast). FeedbackRepository stores seller responses to PostgreSQL suggestion_feedback table for retraining pipeline.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly ECS Scheduled Task at 02:00 UTC: (1) export trailing 24-month sales history per shop from marketplace data warehouse to S3; (2) for each shop, train or update per-product demand models with scikit-learn, evaluate holdout MAE; (3) if MAE regression > 10% versus current production model, skip deployment and alert Datadog; (4) generate 90-day forecasts and write to ClickHouse; (5) call OpenAI GPT-4o for natural-language explanations per suggestion, cache in Redis with 24-hour TTL.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS batch task concurrency set to 20 shops in parallel. At 200 pilot sellers the 4-hour window is met. At 2000 sellers, concurrency must increase to 200 — validate Fargate vCPU limits and OpenAI rate limits before scaling.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pools with seller-to-shop_id mapping stored in PostgreSQL seller_accounts table. FastAPI extracts shop_id from verified JWT sub claim, never from request parameters.
security.authorization_model: All ClickHouse queries include WHERE shop_id = :shop_id derived from JWT. All PostgreSQL queries scoped by shop_id foreign key. ForecastService rejects requests where JWT shop_id does not match path parameter shopId.
security.privacy_controls: Seller sales data never commingled in shared data structures. LLM prompt templates use aggregate statistics only (sales counts, price percentiles) — no individual customer names or order IDs sent to OpenAI API.
security.security_risks: Risk: LLM prompt injection via crafted product title strings. Mitigation: product titles sanitized and length-capped before inclusion in GPT-4o prompts. Seller-controlled strings are quoted and position-limited in prompt templates.

data.entities: shop_profiles (shop_id UUID PK, seller_id FK, shop_name, created_at, marketplace_joined_at), product_listings (product_id UUID PK, shop_id FK, title, category, base_price, status ENUM active/inactive/draft, created_at), historical_sales (sale_id, product_id FK, shop_id FK, quantity_sold, sale_price, sold_at DATE — ClickHouse table partitioned by toYYYYMM(sold_at)), demand_forecast_runs (run_id UUID PK, shop_id FK, product_id FK, run_date DATE, model_version, mae_score, forecast_horizon_days, status ENUM completed/failed/skipped), pricing_suggestions (id UUID PK, product_id FK, shop_id FK, suggested_price DECIMAL, confidence FLOAT, rationale_cached TEXT, valid_until TIMESTAMP, generated_at), restock_alerts (id UUID PK, product_id FK, shop_id FK, current_stock INT, days_of_stock_remaining FLOAT, urgency ENUM critical/high/medium, triggered_at), seasonal_patterns (id UUID PK, product_id FK, shop_id FK, month_index SMALLINT, relative_demand_index FLOAT, computed_at), promo_windows (id UUID PK, product_id FK, shop_id FK, window_start DATE, window_end DATE, expected_uplift_pct FLOAT, basis_text TEXT), suggestion_feedback (id UUID PK, suggestion_id FK, shop_id FK, action ENUM accepted/dismissed/snoozed, snoozed_until TIMESTAMP, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16: transactional tables (shop_profiles, product_listings, demand_forecast_runs, pricing_suggestions, restock_alerts, suggestion_feedback). ClickHouse: historical_sales partitioned by month, indexed on (shop_id, product_id, sold_at). S3: serialized scikit-learn model artifacts per shop/product keyed as models/{shop_id}/{product_id}/model_{version}.pkl. Redis: forecast JSON blobs keyed as forecast:{shop_id}:{product_id} with 24-hour TTL; LLM explanations keyed as explanation:{suggestion_id}.
data.retention_and_deletion: historical_sales in ClickHouse: TTL 36 months via ClickHouse TTL expression. pricing_suggestions: soft-delete after valid_until passes. suggestion_feedback: retained 12 months for model retraining then anonymized.
data.analytics_events: seller_viewed_forecast (shop_id, product_id, viewed_at), seller_acted_on_suggestion (shop_id, suggestion_id, action, acted_at), model_training_completed (shop_id, product_count, duration_seconds, avg_mae, run_date)

devops.deployment_topology: Three ECS services: api-service (FastAPI, 2-4 tasks auto-scaling on CPU 60%), frontend-service (Next.js SSR, 2-4 tasks), batch-worker (ECS Scheduled Task, not a persistent service, triggered at 02:00 UTC). ALB routing /api/* to api-service, /* to frontend-service. ClickHouse deployed on EC2 r6i.large with EBS gp3 volume.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff + ESLint), test (pytest for FastAPI + vitest for Next.js, 80% coverage gates), build Docker images, push ECR, deploy ECS rolling update. Model training CI: separate workflow triggered on ML code changes, runs accuracy regression test on held-out 2024 sales data before merging to main.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on FastAPI (ddtrace). CloudWatch metrics for ECS batch task duration, task failures. Custom Datadog metrics: forecast_model_mae_avg, llm_explanation_latency_p95, restock_alert_generation_count. PagerDuty alert if batch job fails or p95 dashboard latency exceeds 3s.

ISS-001.title: No model accuracy regression gate before production deployment
ISS-001.detail: The nightly batch pipeline deploys updated forecast models without evaluating accuracy on a holdout set. A degraded model could silently serve worse pricing recommendations to sellers for 24 hours before detection.

history.note: Batch pipeline script skipped validation step in round 1 design

ISS-002.title: shop_id not derived from JWT in all API paths
ISS-002.detail: Several forecast retrieval endpoints accepted shop_id as a query parameter rather than deriving it exclusively from the Cognito JWT claims, creating a horizontal privilege escalation risk between sellers.

history.note: ForecastService path parameter was not cross-checked with JWT-derived shop_id

ISS-003.title: LLM prompt injection risk via product title field
ISS-003.detail: Product titles are seller-controlled strings included verbatim in GPT-4o prompt templates during nightly batch, enabling prompt injection that could alter explanation output or extract context from the prompt.

history.note: No sanitization or length cap applied to product_title before prompt assembly

focus_issues.title: No model accuracy regression gate before production deployment
focus_issues.detail: The nightly batch pipeline deploys updated forecast models without evaluating accuracy on a holdout set.

focus_issues.title: shop_id not derived from JWT in all API paths
focus_issues.detail: Several forecast retrieval endpoints accepted shop_id as a query parameter rather than deriving it exclusively from Cognito JWT claims.

latest_recommendations: Add holdout MAE evaluation step to batch pipeline with 10% regression threshold before model deployment
Remove shop_id from all API request parameters; derive exclusively from JWT sub claim in FastAPI dependency
Sanitize product titles in LLM prompt templates: strip special characters, cap at 60 characters, wrap in quoted context

strengths: LLM explanations pre-cached during batch prevents cold-path latency
ClickHouse partitioned by shop_id and product_id is the right analytics store choice
Per-product scikit-learn models appropriate for heterogeneous craft categories
concerns: Batch pipeline deploys new models without MAE holdout evaluation
shop_id accepted from request body in some endpoints, not solely from JWT
Product titles not sanitized before GPT-4o prompt assembly

Model deployment without accuracy gate risks degraded recommendations

Create FastAPI dependency get_current_shop_id() that extracts shop_id from Cognito JWT claims and use it exclusively across all forecast, suggestion, and alert endpoints

issue_updates.title: No model accuracy regression gate
issue_updates.detail: Batch pipeline train_models.py writes new model artifacts to S3 and immediately updates the active model pointer without any holdout evaluation

issue_updates.title: shop_id accepted as request parameter

previous_plan.title: Crafts Marketplace Seller Analytics — Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: ML-driven seller analytics for handmade crafts marketplace with per-product demand forecasting, pricing suggestions, and restock alerts. Next.js 14 frontend, FastAPI ML backend, ClickHouse for analytics, nightly ECS batch job for model training.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three-tier: Next.js SSR dashboard on ECS, FastAPI on ECS reading from ClickHouse and PostgreSQL, nightly ECS Scheduled Task for model training. LLM explanations via OpenAI GPT-4o called during batch and cached in Redis.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT auth, shop_id scoping on queries, TLS 1.3. shop_id still accepted as request param in forecast endpoints (ISS-002 root cause).
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI, ECS Fargate rolling deploy, batch job at 02:00 UTC without model validation gate (ISS-001 root cause).

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the crafts marketplace seller analytics plan to address two high-severity issues: adding a model accuracy regression gate to the nightly batch pipeline (ISS-001) and eliminating shop_id as a request parameter across all API endpoints, enforcing exclusive JWT-derived scoping (ISS-002). Also adding product title sanitization for LLM prompts (ISS-003). The ClickHouse/PostgreSQL split and LLM caching strategy from round 1 are sound and retained.

fix_report.action_taken: Added model_validation_gate function to train_models.py batch script: after training the new model, computes MAE on the most recent 30-day holdout window, compares to current production model MAE stored in demand_forecast_runs table, and aborts S3 artifact promotion if regression exceeds 10%. Failure triggers a Datadog alert and leaves the existing production model active.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Degraded forecast models are never deployed to sellers; worst-case scenario is a stale but previously-validated model serving for an additional 24-hour cycle.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed shop_id from all FastAPI endpoint path parameters and query strings. Created a get_current_shop_id() FastAPI dependency that extracts the shop_id from the verified Cognito JWT sub claim via the seller_accounts table lookup. All forecast, suggestion, alert, and feedback routers now use Depends(get_current_shop_id) exclusively.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No API path can serve data for a shop other than the authenticated seller's own shop, eliminating horizontal privilege escalation.

target_output.title: Crafts Marketplace Seller Analytics — Architecture Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: ML-driven seller performance analytics for a handmade crafts marketplace delivering per-product demand forecasts, pricing suggestions with confidence intervals, and restock alerts to shop owners. Built on Next.js 14, FastAPI with scikit-learn, ClickHouse for sales analytics, and GPT-4o for natural-language forecast explanations cached during nightly batch. This revision hardens data isolation via JWT-exclusive shop_id scoping and adds a model accuracy regression gate before production model deployment.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture on AWS us-east-1: Next.js 14 App Router on ECS Fargate serves the seller dashboard with server-side rendering of forecast summaries and client-side Recharts visualizations. FastAPI service on ECS handles API requests, derives shop_id exclusively from Cognito JWT claims, and reads pre-computed forecasts from Redis cache or ClickHouse. An ECS Scheduled Task at 02:00 UTC runs the ML batch pipeline: it trains per-product scikit-learn demand models, validates accuracy on a 30-day holdout before promoting to S3, generates 90-day forecasts written to ClickHouse, and calls GPT-4o to generate natural-language explanation text cached in Redis. PostgreSQL 16 stores transactional records; ClickHouse stores the monthly-partitioned historical_sales table. All shop data access gated by shop_id from JWT.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Recharts, React Query. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2, scikit-learn for demand forecasting, OpenAI Python SDK for GPT-4o explanations. Databases: PostgreSQL 16 (transactional), ClickHouse on EC2 r6i.large (analytics). Cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Auth: AWS Cognito user pools. Hosting: AWS ECS Fargate for API and Next.js services; ECS Scheduled Tasks for batch. Model storage: S3 with versioned artifact paths. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Datadog APM and custom metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Forecast Dashboard: product grid with 7-day demand trend sparklines and color-coded urgency badges (green/yellow/red based on days_of_stock_remaining). Product Detail View: 90-day demand forecast line chart with confidence interval bands, current versus suggested price comparison card, seller accept/dismiss/snooze action buttons, GPT-4o natural language explanation panel. Seasonal Pattern View: monthly heatmap of relative_demand_index per product showing peak craft-buying seasons (November/December elevated for most categories). Restock Alert Feed: time-sorted list of restock_alerts with product photo, current stock level, and days_of_stock_remaining formatted as urgency pills. Promo Window Calendar: month grid with suggested promotional windows highlighted, expected_uplift_pct displayed on hover.
target_output.system_components: ALB routing /api/* to FastAPI ECS service and /* to Next.js ECS service. FastAPI service with four routers: /forecasts (GET per product, shop_id from JWT), /suggestions (GET pricing suggestions with cached LLM explanations), /alerts (GET restock alerts sorted by urgency), /feedback (POST seller actions on suggestions). get_current_shop_id() FastAPI dependency enforcing JWT-exclusive shop scoping on all routers. Next.js App Router with server components for initial forecast summary fetch and client components for interactive charts. ECS Scheduled Task invoking batch pipeline: ModelTrainer (per-product scikit-learn fit + MAE holdout gate), ForecastGenerator (90-day forward predictions written to ClickHouse), ExplanationGenerator (GPT-4o calls with sanitized product context, Redis cache writes). ClickHouse cluster on EC2 for historical_sales. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for transactional data. ElastiCache Redis 7 for forecast and explanation caching.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Model Training with Validation Gate: ECS batch task starts at 02:00 UTC. For each shop, for each active product: load trailing 24-month sales from ClickHouse, split last 30 days as holdout, train scikit-learn model on prior 21 months, evaluate MAE on holdout, retrieve current production model MAE from demand_forecast_runs. If new MAE is more than 10% worse, log skipped deployment to demand_forecast_runs with status=skipped and trigger Datadog alert. Otherwise promote new model artifact to S3 at models/{shop_id}/{product_id}/model_current.pkl, generate 90-day forecasts, write to ClickHouse, call GPT-4o with sanitized product context to generate explanation text, cache in Redis with 24-hour TTL. Seller Feedback Loop: Seller views pricing suggestion on dashboard, clicks accept. Next.js sends POST /feedback with suggestion_id and action=accepted. FastAPI's get_current_shop_id() dependency verifies JWT, rejects if shop_id mismatch. FeedbackRepository writes to suggestion_feedback table. On next model training cycle, FeedbackAggregator reads accepted/dismissed signals and adjusts training sample weights for that product.
target_output.data_model: shop_profiles (shop_id UUID PK, seller_id FK, shop_name VARCHAR, marketplace_joined_at TIMESTAMP, created_at TIMESTAMP). product_listings (product_id UUID PK, shop_id FK, title VARCHAR(120), category VARCHAR(60), base_price DECIMAL(10,2), status ENUM active/inactive/draft, created_at). historical_sales (sale_id UUID, product_id UUID, shop_id UUID, quantity_sold SMALLINT, sale_price DECIMAL(10,2), sold_at DATE — ClickHouse ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY (shop_id, product_id, sold_at) PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(sold_at) TTL sold_at + INTERVAL 36 MONTH). demand_forecast_runs (run_id UUID PK, shop_id FK, product_id FK, run_date DATE, model_version VARCHAR, mae_score FLOAT, holdout_mae_baseline FLOAT, status ENUM completed/failed/skipped, created_at). pricing_suggestions (id UUID PK, product_id FK, shop_id FK, suggested_price DECIMAL(10,2), confidence FLOAT CHECK(0<=confidence<=1), explanation_cached TEXT, valid_until TIMESTAMP, generated_at). restock_alerts (id UUID PK, product_id FK, shop_id FK, current_stock INT, days_of_stock_remaining FLOAT, urgency ENUM critical/high/medium, triggered_at). seasonal_patterns (product_id FK, shop_id FK, month_index SMALLINT, relative_demand_index FLOAT, computed_at — PRIMARY KEY product_id, shop_id, month_index). promo_windows (id UUID PK, product_id FK, shop_id FK, window_start DATE, window_end DATE, expected_uplift_pct FLOAT, basis_text VARCHAR(200), generated_at). suggestion_feedback (id UUID PK, suggestion_id FK, shop_id FK, action ENUM accepted/dismissed/snoozed, snoozed_until TIMESTAMP, created_at).
target_output.api_design: All endpoints use get_current_shop_id() FastAPI dependency — no shop_id in path or query params. GET /api/v1/forecasts returns list of per-product ForecastSummary objects (product_id, product_title, demand_trend_7d, days_of_stock_remaining, urgency) for authenticated seller's shop. GET /api/v1/forecasts/{product_id} returns ForecastDetail (90-day forecast series with confidence bands, seasonal_patterns array, pricing_suggestion with explanation). GET /api/v1/alerts returns restock_alerts sorted by urgency and triggered_at. POST /api/v1/feedback body {suggestion_id: UUID, action: accepted|dismissed|snoozed, snoozed_until?: ISO8601} returns 204. GET /api/v1/promo-windows returns promo_windows for the next 60 days across all shop products.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito user pools with JWT validation via python-jose on every FastAPI request. get_current_shop_id() dependency resolves seller_id from JWT sub claim, looks up shop_id in seller_accounts table — shop_id never accepted from client payload or query string. ClickHouse queries include hardcoded WHERE shop_id = :shop_id parameter binding. PostgreSQL row-level security policy on product_listings and pricing_suggestions enforcing shop_id = current_setting('app.current_shop_id'). LLM prompt template: product titles stripped of non-alphanumeric characters, capped at 60 characters, wrapped in angle-bracket delimiters to prevent injection: '<product: {sanitized_title}>'. OpenAI API key stored in AWS Secrets Manager, rotated every 90 days. TLS 1.3 on all ALB listeners.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI pipeline: ruff lint and mypy type check for Python, ESLint for Next.js, pytest (80% coverage gate on FastAPI), vitest (Next.js components), Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deploy via aws-actions/amazon-ecs-deploy-task-definition. Separate ML CI workflow: on changes to ml/ directory, runs accuracy regression test against 2024 held-out sales data before merge allowed. Environments: dev (single-AZ Fargate, ClickHouse on t3.medium), staging (multi-AZ, mirrors prod topology), production (multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c, ECS auto-scaling 2-4 tasks on CPU 60%, RDS Multi-AZ PostgreSQL, ClickHouse on r6i.large EBS gp3 500GB). ECS Scheduled Task batch job at 02:00 UTC in production only; dev and staging run on-demand via CLI trigger. S3 model artifacts versioned with lifecycle rule archiving versions older than 90 days to Glacier.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces on all FastAPI request handlers with shop_id as a custom tag for per-seller latency analysis. Custom metrics: forecast_model_mae_avg (mean MAE across all products trained in last batch run), model_deployment_skipped_count (count of products where regression gate blocked deployment), llm_explanation_latency_p95 (GPT-4o call duration during batch), restock_alert_count_by_urgency, suggestion_acceptance_rate (accepted / total suggestions per 24h). CloudWatch alarm on ECS batch task failure status triggers PagerDuty page. Dashboard p95 latency tracked via Datadog SLO at 2s target.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $800/month at 200 sellers: ECS Fargate for API and Next.js ($150), ClickHouse on r6i.large reserved ($250), RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.t3.medium ($180), ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.micro ($50), OpenAI GPT-4o batch calls ~$0.02/suggestion at 500 nightly suggestions ($10/month), S3 and data transfer ($30). At 2000 sellers, batch concurrency increases and OpenAI costs scale linearly; ClickHouse may require vertical scaling to r6i.xlarge at around $1800/month total.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Authentication with Cognito, product forecast dashboard with demand trend sparklines, pricing suggestions with GPT-4o explanations, restock alerts, nightly batch pipeline with model validation gate (ISS-001 fix), JWT-exclusive shop_id scoping (ISS-002 fix), LLM prompt sanitization (ISS-003 fix), beta launch with 200 pilot sellers. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Seasonal pattern heatmap visualization, promo window calendar, seller feedback loop wired into model retraining sample weights, suggestion acceptance rate analytics for internal product team. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Anonymized cross-seller category benchmark comparisons, marketplace-wide trend alerts for top-performing craft categories, mobile push notification service for restock alerts via SNS.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python strict type annotations enforced with mypy in CI. FastAPI dependencies for shop_id scoping must be used on every router — enforced via integration test that calls all endpoints with a mismatched JWT shop_id and asserts 403. scikit-learn model artifacts serialized with joblib; model version strings include UTC timestamp to enable rollback audit. LLM prompt templates versioned in ml/prompts/ directory; changes require ML team review. React Query stale times: 5 minutes for forecast summaries, 30 minutes for seasonal patterns, 24 hours for promo windows. No hardcoded shop_id in any test fixture — all tests use factory-generated UUIDs.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse single-node is a reliability risk for the analytics query path. Mitigation: ClickHouse on EBS gp3 with daily S3 snapshots; failover plan is restoring from S3 within 2 hours. Risk: OpenAI GPT-4o API downtime blocks nightly explanation generation. Mitigation: batch pipeline logs warning and writes NULL to explanation_cached; dashboard displays a generic static fallback text if explanation is null. Tradeoff: per-product scikit-learn models versus a single marketplace-wide model — per-product models capture craft category seasonality more accurately but require longer training time at scale. Tradeoff: ClickHouse on EC2 over managed ClickHouse Cloud for cost control at pilot scale, accepting single-node reliability risk.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected ClickHouse over Redshift for sub-second seller dashboard query response on columnar aggregations. Confirmed GPT-4o over GPT-3.5-turbo for explanation quality — cost acceptable at pilot scale of 200 sellers. Decided model training runs nightly rather than incrementally to simplify pipeline state management. Product title sanitization uses angle-bracket wrapping plus 60-character truncation as the injection mitigation strategy.

metadata.notes: Crafts marketplace ML analytics revision adding model validation gate and JWT-exclusive shop scoping.

project_goal.value: Returns management platform for mid-market apparel brands that automates refund decisions based on return reason and item condition, streamlines reverse logistics, and routes items to resale, refurbishment, or disposal channels to reduce manual handling costs
project_goal.rationale: Core value proposition: reduce reverse logistics cost through automation

target_users.value: Brand operations teams (returns managers, warehouse staff, finance ops) and brand admins configuring automation rules
target_users.rationale: Internal brand users, not consumers — consumer-facing return portal is a separate system

project_class.rationale: React frontend dashboard plus Go API backend with warehouse integration webhooks

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, payments, analytics, admin_panel, integrations, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full platform: refund decisioning, payments processing, warehouse integrations, analytics, admin rule configuration

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-channel disposition routing, refund automation engine, warehouse webhook integrations, cost savings analytics

risk_level.rationale: Processes refund payments and handles brand financial data but within managed B2B context

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes refund amounts, recovered item values, and brand cost savings financials

external_exposure.rationale: Brand staff access only; no public-facing UI

access_model.value: Okta OIDC with RBAC: returns_agent (view/create returns), warehouse_staff (update condition assessments), finance_ops (view reports, trigger refunds), brand_admin (configure decision trees, manage channels)
access_model.rationale: Role-based access matches operational responsibilities

feature_scope.value: Return request intake with reason and item condition capture, automated refund decision engine with brand-configured rules, prepaid shipping label generation, warehouse condition assessment workflow, disposition routing to resale/refurbishment/disposal channels, cost savings reporting per brand and channel, admin panel for decision tree configuration
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end reverse logistics workflow automation

mvp_scope.value: Return intake, automated refund/exchange/store credit decisions, shipping label generation, warehouse arrival and condition grading, basic disposition routing, brand-level cost savings dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable to onboard first 3 brand clients

security_baseline.value: Okta OIDC JWT auth, role-based access control, TLS 1.3 in transit, financial data encrypted at rest, audit log for all refund decisions and condition assessments
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data security baseline

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript and Vite
frontend_stack.rationale: React 18 with fast Vite HMR dev experience and TypeScript safety

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Chi router
backend_stack.rationale: Go for high-throughput webhook processing and low-latency refund decisioning

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for all transactional data; Redis 7 for decision cache and job queues
data_platform.rationale: Relational data model fits complex return lifecycle state machine; Redis for async job queueing

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1; RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container and database services with multi-AZ reliability

privacy_retention_policy.value: Return records retained 7 years for financial audit compliance; cost savings reports retained 5 years; shipping label data retained 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention requirements for brand clients

future_scope.value: Consumer-facing self-service return portal, carrier API direct integrations, AI-powered condition assessment from warehouse photos, cross-brand channel analytics
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 expansion into consumer-facing and AI capabilities

constraints.value: Must support multi-brand tenancy with strict data isolation; shipping label generation must complete within 5 seconds; budget under $2500/month per brand client at MVP scale
constraints.rationale: Multi-tenancy and latency SLA driven by brand contract requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on Go service, structured JSON logging, custom metrics for return processing volume, refund decision latency, disposition routing outcomes
observability_baseline.rationale: Operational visibility into return processing throughput and financial decision accuracy

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with CircleCI for CI/CD; feature branches with PR review; staged rollout per brand client
execution_preference.rationale: Staged rollout reduces risk when onboarding new brand clients to automation

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ A for refund payment flows (Stripe tokenization); SOX-adjacent financial data retention for brand client audit requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Refund payments require PCI compliance; financial record retention obligations

project.goal: Automate apparel brand return decisioning and reverse logistics disposition routing
project.target_users: Brand operations staff: returns agents, warehouse staff, finance ops, brand admins

frontend.state: Zustand for return workflow state, React Query for server data

backend.integrations: Stripe for refunds, EasyPost for shipping labels, warehouse webhook receiver

security.auth: Okta OIDC JWT

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ
data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache
data.queue: Redis-backed job queue for async label generation

reasoner_reviews.product: The automated decision engine is the core value driver — brands pay to eliminate manual returns agent review for straightforward cases (like-new item + wrong size reason = immediate exchange). The decision tree config admin panel must be intuitive enough for non-technical brand admins to adjust rules without engineering support.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Chi is well-suited to the high-frequency webhook ingestion from warehouse systems and EasyPost carrier callbacks. The return request lifecycle maps naturally to a PostgreSQL state machine with explicit status transitions. Redis job queue for asynchronous label generation decouples the synchronous refund decision from the slower EasyPost API call.
reasoner_reviews.security: Multi-tenant brand_id isolation is the most critical architectural control — every query must be scoped by brand_id derived from the Okta JWT org claim, never from request payload. The audit_events table for refund decisions must be append-only and never modified post-write to satisfy financial audit requirements.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5-second label generation SLA requires EasyPost API calls to be async but the UI needs a real-time status update — WebSocket or SSE for label_ready notification is appropriate. $2500/month per brand at MVP scale is achievable with shared Fargate cluster and per-brand database schemas.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan did not specify how the decision tree configuration is versioned — when a brand admin modifies automation rules, existing in-flight return requests must continue processing under the rule version that was active when they were submitted. Decision tree versioning is a correctness requirement, not just a nice-to-have.
