frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with React Router v6 and route-based code splitting. Top-level layout with brand sidebar navigation, role-aware menu items. Separate route bundles for returns workflow, warehouse operations, finance dashboard, and admin configuration panel.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Returns Queue: table of pending return_requests filterable by reason, status, urgency, SKU. Return Detail: item photo, return reason, condition assessment form for warehouse staff, decision badge (auto-approved/manual review/rejected), refund amount, disposition assignment. Disposition Board: Kanban-style view of items moving through resale/refurbishment/disposal channels with recovered value per lane. Finance Report: monthly bar charts of refund costs, recovered value, cost savings percentage. Admin Panel: decision tree builder with condition-reason matrix and outcome assignment dropdowns.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for multi-step condition assessment workflow state (warehouse staff flows across 4 screens). React Query for return list and detail fetches with 30-second polling for status updates. SSE subscription on /returns/{id}/events for real-time shipping label ready notification.
frontend.frontend_risks: Risk: decision tree builder complexity requires careful form state management for nested rule conditions. Mitigation: JSON schema-driven form builder with Zod validation at each step.

backend.service_design: Go Chi application with domain packages: returns (request intake, lifecycle state machine), decisions (engine evaluating return against decision tree version), disposition (channel routing logic), labels (EasyPost async job handler), webhooks (warehouse system receiver), reports (brand cost savings aggregations).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful API: POST /returns (create return request), GET /returns (paginated queue with filters), PATCH /returns/{id}/assessment (warehouse condition update), GET /returns/{id}/label (poll or SSE for label URL), POST /returns/{id}/disposition (manual override). Admin: POST /brands/{id}/decision-trees (publish new version), GET /brands/{id}/decision-trees (list versions).
backend.background_jobs: Redis-backed job queue with dedicated worker goroutines: LabelGenerationJob (calls EasyPost, writes logistics_labels record, publishes SSE event), RefundSettlementJob (calls Stripe refund API for approved returns, updates refund_decisions status), NightlyRecoveredValueJob (aggregates disposition channel outcomes into cost_savings_reports).
backend.failure_handling: EasyPost label generation: retry 3 times with 2s backoff, then mark label_status=failed and alert ops. Stripe refund: idempotency key per refund_decision_id, retry on 5xx, permanent failure triggers finance_ops notification. Warehouse webhooks: idempotency on event_id, duplicate events silently discarded.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC JWT validation on every request via Go middleware. brand_id extracted from JWT org claim, never from request body. Role extracted from JWT groups claim.
security.authorization_model: Chi middleware enforces: returns_agent can read/create returns for own brand only; warehouse_staff can update condition assessments; finance_ops can view reports and trigger settlement; brand_admin can modify decision trees. Middleware rejects any request where JWT brand_id does not match URL brand_id parameter.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_events table: insert-only trigger, no UPDATE or DELETE permitted. Every refund decision, condition assessment change, and decision tree publish event written with actor_id, brand_id, action, payload_hash, and created_at. Finance reports joined against audit_events for immutability verification.
security.security_risks: Risk: Stripe webhook spoofing could trigger fraudulent refund settlements. Mitigation: Stripe webhook signature verification via HMAC-SHA256 on raw body before any processing.

data.entities: return_requests (id UUID PK, brand_id UUID FK, order_id, sku, return_reason ENUM wrong_size/wrong_item/defective/changed_mind/not_as_described, status ENUM submitted/in_review/assessed/decision_made/label_issued/received/disposed, submitted_at, decision_tree_version_id FK), return_line_items (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, sku, quantity, unit_price DECIMAL, condition_assessment_id FK), condition_assessments (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, graded_by_staff_id FK, grade ENUM like_new/good/fair/poor/unsaleable, notes TEXT, photo_s3_keys TEXT[], graded_at), refund_decisions (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, brand_id FK, decision ENUM full_refund/exchange/store_credit/reject, refund_amount DECIMAL, stripe_refund_id, decision_source ENUM automated/manual_override, decision_tree_version_id FK, decided_at), disposal_channels (id UUID PK, brand_id FK, channel_type ENUM resale/refurbishment/liquidation/disposal, partner_name, api_webhook_url, is_active BOOL), logistics_labels (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, carrier, tracking_number, label_url_s3, label_status ENUM pending/issued/failed, easypost_shipment_id, created_at), cost_savings_reports (id UUID PK, brand_id FK, period_month DATE, total_returns INT, automated_decision_rate FLOAT, total_refund_cost DECIMAL, total_recovered_value DECIMAL, net_cost_savings DECIMAL, generated_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ: all transactional tables with brand_id FK for tenant isolation, RLS policy enforcing brand_id = current_setting('app.brand_id'). Redis ElastiCache: job queue (LIST data structure per job type), label_ready events (Pub/Sub channel per return_id). S3: condition assessment photos keyed as brands/{brand_id}/assessments/{return_id}/{photo_id}.jpg.
data.migration_notes: Per-brand database schema approach considered but rejected for operational complexity; single schema with RLS provides sufficient isolation with simpler migration tooling (golang-migrate).

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster with two services: api-service (Go Chi, 2-8 tasks auto-scaling on CPU 65%) and worker-service (job queue consumers, 1-4 tasks). ALB in front of api-service. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in us-east-1a/1c. ElastiCache Redis single-node with Multi-AZ replica. S3 bucket with lifecycle rules for photo archival.
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipeline: go vet + golangci-lint, go test (85% coverage gate), Docker build + ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with health check gate. Staged rollout: new brand client environments deployed to staging first, brand-specific smoke test suite run before production promotion.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS deployment keeps previous task definition revision available for 15-minute rollback window. Database migrations use golang-migrate with down migrations for every change. Redis job queue items reprocessed by fallback worker on service restart.

ISS-001.title: Decision tree not versioned at return submission time
ISS-001.detail: When a brand admin modifies automation rules mid-week, existing in-flight return requests are evaluated against the new rule version rather than the version active at submission time, causing inconsistent decisions for the same return batch.

history.note: return_requests table had no decision_tree_version_id FK in round 1 design

ISS-002.title: Stripe webhook not signature-verified before processing
ISS-002.detail: The Stripe webhook receiver in round 1 parsed event payload without verifying the Stripe-Signature header HMAC, allowing spoofed refund settlement events from external actors.

history.note: Webhook handler missing stripe.ConstructEvent() verification step

ISS-003.title: No SSE or polling mechanism for label generation status
ISS-003.detail: Label generation is async but the round 1 frontend had no real-time mechanism to notify the UI when the label was ready, leaving users with a stale pending state until manual refresh.

history.note: Frontend used polling with 30s interval — too slow for a 5s SLA target

focus_issues.title: Decision tree not versioned at return submission time
focus_issues.detail: In-flight returns evaluated against updated rules instead of rules active at submission time.

focus_issues.title: Stripe webhook not signature-verified before processing
focus_issues.detail: Webhook handler missing HMAC verification allowing spoofed settlement events.

latest_recommendations: Add decision_tree_version_id FK to return_requests table, set at submission time; decision engine always resolves the tree version pinned at submission
Add Stripe-Signature HMAC verification using stripe.ConstructEvent() before processing any settlement webhook
Implement SSE channel per return_id for label_ready notification instead of 30s polling
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Apparel Returns Management Platform — Round 1 Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two high severity issues unresolved — decision consistency and payment security are blocking production launch

previous_audits.summary: Solid Go + PostgreSQL architecture for reverse logistics automation with well-designed disposition routing. Two critical correctness and security gaps block production launch: decision tree versioning and Stripe webhook verification.
strengths: Go Chi with Redis job queue is appropriate for async label generation under 5s SLA
RLS-based multi-tenant isolation is correctly designed
Append-only audit_events table satisfies financial audit requirements
concerns: In-flight returns re-evaluated under updated decision tree versions causing decision inconsistency
Stripe webhook lacks HMAC verification
SSE not implemented — 30s polling is too slow for label ready notification
blocking_issues: Stripe webhook spoofing vulnerability must be fixed before handling real refund settlements
Decision tree mutability during batch processing creates financial dispute risk
recommendations: Pin decision_tree_version_id at return submission time and resolve the specific pinned version in the decision engine
Add stripe.ConstructEvent(body, sig, webhookSecret) call at the top of the Stripe webhook handler, returning 400 on verification failure

issue_updates.title: Decision tree versioning missing
issue_updates.detail: return_requests.decision_tree_version_id column absent in round 1 schema; DecisionEngine reads current active tree version at decision time

issue_updates.title: Stripe webhook HMAC verification absent
issue_updates.detail: StripeWebhookHandler processes raw JSON payload without calling stripe.ConstructEvent for signature validation

previous_plan.title: Apparel Returns Management Platform — Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 with Chi backend and React 18 frontend for apparel brand returns automation. Decision engine evaluates return reasons against brand-configured rules, generates EasyPost shipping labels asynchronously, routes items to disposition channels, and triggers Stripe refunds.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate: Go Chi api-service and worker-service. RDS PostgreSQL with brand_id RLS. Redis for job queue. Decision engine reads current active decision tree version at decision time (ISS-001 root cause). Stripe webhook handler processes raw payload without HMAC verification (ISS-002 root cause).
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC JWT auth, brand_id from JWT org claim, TLS 1.3. Stripe webhook missing signature verification.
previous_plan.data_model: return_requests without decision_tree_version_id FK. refund_decisions, condition_assessments, disposition_channels, logistics_labels, cost_savings_reports tables present.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the apparel returns management platform to fix two high-severity issues: pinning decision_tree_version_id at return submission time so in-flight returns always evaluate against the rule version active at submission (ISS-001), and adding Stripe-Signature HMAC verification via stripe.ConstructEvent() before processing any settlement webhook (ISS-002). Also implementing SSE for label-ready notification to replace the too-slow 30s polling (ISS-003). The Go Chi + Redis job queue + PostgreSQL RLS foundation from round 1 is sound and retained.

fix_report.action_taken: Added decision_tree_version_id UUID FK column to return_requests table, populated from the brand's currently published decision tree at the moment POST /returns is called. DecisionEngine now accepts a version_id parameter and retrieves the specific decision_tree_versions row rather than querying for the latest active version. Brand admin publish action creates a new version record; existing in-flight returns retain their pinned version_id.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Modifying automation rules mid-week does not alter decisions for returns already submitted in that period; each return batch is evaluated consistently against its pinned rule version.

fix_report.action_taken: Added stripe.ConstructEvent(rawBody, sigHeader, webhookSecret) at the top of the StripeWebhookHandler before any JSON parsing. Handler returns HTTP 400 if signature verification fails. Stripe webhook secret stored in AWS Secrets Manager, injected at container start. Raw body buffered before parsing to preserve the exact bytes needed for HMAC verification.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Spoofed Stripe events are rejected before any settlement processing occurs; only events with valid HMAC signatures from Stripe's webhook delivery system trigger refund state transitions.

target_output.title: Apparel Returns Management Platform — Architecture Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A fullstack returns automation platform for mid-market apparel brands built with React 18, Go 1.22 with Chi, and PostgreSQL 16 on AWS ECS Fargate. Automates refund decisions via brand-configured decision trees pinned at submission time, routes returned items to resale, refurbishment, or disposal channels, generates EasyPost shipping labels asynchronously with SSE notification, and processes Stripe refunds with full HMAC webhook verification. This revision resolves decision tree versioning and payment security issues from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-service ECS Fargate deployment: Go Chi api-service handles synchronous return intake, decision engine evaluation, admin operations, and serves SSE event streams per return_id; worker-service consumes Redis job queue for async EasyPost label generation and Stripe refund settlement. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with row-level security enforcing brand_id isolation. ElastiCache Redis for job queue and Pub/Sub label-ready notifications. React 18 SPA on CloudFront backed by S3 for static assets. Stripe webhook endpoint verifies HMAC signatures before processing settlement events. Okta OIDC JWT carries brand_id from org claim, enforced by Go middleware on all routes. Decision trees are versioned; return_requests pin decision_tree_version_id at submission time, isolating in-flight returns from subsequent rule changes.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query, native EventSource API for SSE. Backend: Go 1.22, Chi router, go-stripe SDK (HMAC webhook verification), EasyPost Go SDK, golang-migrate for schema migrations. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with pgcrypto for financial field encryption. Cache/Queue: Redis 7 ElastiCache. Auth: Okta OIDC. Hosting: AWS ECS Fargate, ALB, CloudFront + S3 for frontend. CI/CD: CircleCI. Monitoring: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Return Request Intake: returns_agent submits return with reason code, SKU, and quantity; system pins current decision_tree_version_id, runs DecisionEngine, creates refund_decision record and queues LabelGenerationJob. Condition Assessment Workflow: warehouse_staff opens return on mobile-optimized detail screen, selects condition grade (like_new/good/fair/poor/unsaleable), uploads photos to S3, triggers disposition routing update. Disposition Routing: DecisionEngine maps condition grade to highest-value channel from brand's disposal_channels configuration — like_new returns route to resale partner, fair to refurbishment, unsaleable to liquidation. Shipping Label Generation: EasyPost async job generates prepaid label, posts to S3, publishes SSE event; frontend EventSource listener updates label URL in real time. Finance Settlement: Stripe RefundSettlementJob calls Stripe refund API with idempotency key, updates refund_decisions.stripe_refund_id. Cost Savings Dashboard: finance_ops views monthly bar chart of net_cost_savings per channel with automated decision rate gauge. Admin Decision Tree Builder: brand_admin creates new tree version with condition-reason matrix, publishes as new version_id — active returns are unaffected.
target_output.system_components: ALB routing /api/* to api-service ECS tasks and static assets via CloudFront. Go Chi api-service with middleware stack: OktaJWTMiddleware (validates JWT, injects brand_id and role), BrandIsolationMiddleware (sets PostgreSQL session variable app.brand_id for RLS). Domain handlers: ReturnHandler (POST /returns pins decision_tree_version_id), DecisionEngine (resolves pinned tree version, evaluates return_reason x condition_grade matrix), DispositionRouter (maps assessment grade to highest-value channel). SSE handler at GET /returns/{id}/events serving label_ready and status_changed events via Redis Pub/Sub subscription. Stripe webhook handler: raw body buffered, stripe.ConstructEvent() verified, routes to RefundSettlementService. Worker-service goroutines consuming Redis LPUSH queues: LabelWorker (EasyPost SDK calls, S3 label storage, Redis PUBLISH label_ready), RefundWorker (Stripe refund API with idempotency), NightlyReportWorker (cost savings aggregation via PostgreSQL CTE). React 18 SPA with EventSource client for label notifications.
target_output.workflows: Return Submission and Decision: returns_agent submits POST /returns. BrandIsolationMiddleware injects brand_id from JWT. ReturnHandler queries decision_tree_versions for brand's latest published version, writes return_requests with pinned decision_tree_version_id. DecisionEngine loads that specific version's condition-reason matrix, applies rules, writes refund_decisions with decision source=automated, enqueues LabelGenerationJob. If rules indicate manual_review, creates return with status=in_review. Label Generation with SSE: LabelWorker dequeues job, calls EasyPost create_shipment, receives label URL, uploads label PDF to S3, writes logistics_labels record, publishes PUBLISH returns:{return_id}:label_ready to Redis. SSE handler subscribed to that Redis channel pushes data: {label_url, tracking_number} event to frontend EventSource listener. UI label status updates from pending to issued inline. Stripe Refund Settlement: StripeWebhookHandler receives POST /webhooks/stripe. Reads raw body buffer, extracts Stripe-Signature header, calls stripe.ConstructEvent(rawBody, sig, secret). On HMAC failure returns 400. On success, dispatches charge.refunded event to RefundSettlementService which updates refund_decisions.status=settled and stripe_refund_id.
target_output.data_model: return_requests (id UUID PK, brand_id UUID FK, order_id VARCHAR, sku VARCHAR, return_reason ENUM wrong_size/wrong_item/defective/changed_mind/not_as_described, status ENUM submitted/in_review/assessed/decision_made/label_issued/received/disposed, decision_tree_version_id UUID FK NOT NULL, submitted_at TIMESTAMP, updated_at TIMESTAMP). return_line_items (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, sku, quantity SMALLINT, unit_price DECIMAL(10,2), condition_assessment_id FK NULLABLE). condition_assessments (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, graded_by_staff_id FK, grade ENUM like_new/good/fair/poor/unsaleable, notes TEXT, photo_s3_keys TEXT[], graded_at TIMESTAMP). refund_decisions (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, brand_id FK, decision ENUM full_refund/exchange/store_credit/reject, refund_amount DECIMAL(10,2), stripe_refund_id VARCHAR NULLABLE, decision_source ENUM automated/manual_override, decision_tree_version_id UUID FK, decided_at TIMESTAMP). decision_tree_versions (id UUID PK, brand_id FK, version_number INT, rules_json JSONB NOT NULL, published_by FK, published_at TIMESTAMP, is_active BOOL). disposal_channels (id UUID PK, brand_id FK, channel_type ENUM resale/refurbishment/liquidation/disposal, partner_name VARCHAR, api_webhook_url, is_active BOOL). logistics_labels (id UUID PK, return_request_id FK, carrier VARCHAR, tracking_number VARCHAR, label_url_s3 VARCHAR, label_status ENUM pending/issued/failed, easypost_shipment_id VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP). cost_savings_reports (id UUID PK, brand_id FK, period_month DATE, total_returns INT, automated_decision_rate FLOAT, total_refund_cost DECIMAL(12,2), total_recovered_value DECIMAL(12,2), net_cost_savings DECIMAL(12,2), generated_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/returns — create return request, pins current decision_tree_version_id, runs decision engine, returns return_id and initial decision. GET /api/v1/returns — paginated queue with filter params status, reason, sku, submitted_after. GET /api/v1/returns/{id} — full return detail with decision, label status, condition assessment. GET /api/v1/returns/{id}/events — SSE stream for label_ready and status_changed events. PATCH /api/v1/returns/{id}/assessment — warehouse staff condition grade submission. GET /api/v1/returns/{id}/label — label URL and tracking number (polling fallback). GET /api/v1/reports/cost-savings?period_month=2026-02 — finance_ops cost savings report. POST /api/v1/brands/{id}/decision-trees — brand_admin publishes new decision tree version. POST /webhooks/stripe — raw body HMAC-verified Stripe event handler.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC JWT validated on every request by Go middleware; brand_id extracted from JWT org claim and used to set PostgreSQL session variable app.brand_id enabling RLS policy on all tables. Role extracted from JWT groups claim; endpoint authorization enforced per role in Chi route middleware. Stripe webhook: raw body buffered on POST /webhooks/stripe before any parsing; stripe.ConstructEvent(rawBody, stripeSignatureHeader, webhookSecret) called — HTTP 400 returned on verification failure. Stripe webhook secret in AWS Secrets Manager, rotated on compromise. Financial fields (refund_amount, unit_price, net_cost_savings) stored with pgcrypto column-level encryption. audit_events table with INSERT-only trigger recording every refund_decision, condition_assessment, and decision_tree_version publish event with actor_id, action, payload_hash, brand_id, created_at — no UPDATE or DELETE permitted. PCI-DSS SAQ A compliance maintained: no card data stored or transmitted through platform; Stripe handles all card tokenization.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: CircleCI pipeline: go vet, golangci-lint, go test with 85% coverage gate on domain packages, Docker multi-stage build (distroless base image), ECR push, ECS deploy via aws-ecs-deploy orb with health check gate. Environments: dev (single-AZ, t3.medium RDS, Redis cache.t3.micro), staging (multi-AZ mirror, load test with 1000 concurrent returns), production (multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c, ECS api-service auto-scales 2-8 tasks on CPU 65%, worker-service auto-scales 1-4 tasks on queue depth via CloudWatch custom metric). Staged brand onboarding: new brand client provisioned in staging first, acceptance tests run against brand's own rule configuration, then promoted to production. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager: Okta client secret, Stripe webhook secret, Stripe API key, EasyPost API key — injected at ECS task definition launch.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces on all Go Chi handlers with brand_id and decision_source as custom span tags. Custom Datadog metrics: returns_processed_per_minute (per brand), automated_decision_rate (automated / total per brand per hour), label_generation_latency_p95 (EasyPost API call duration), refund_settlement_latency_p95, disposition_routing_outcome_count (by channel_type and brand). Datadog monitor alerts: return processing error rate > 2% triggers PagerDuty page; label_generation_latency_p95 > 4s triggers warning alert (SLA buffer before 5s breach); Stripe webhook verification failure count > 10/min triggers security alert.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1800/month for 3 brand clients at MVP scale: ECS Fargate api-service and worker-service ($400), RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.t3.medium ($280), ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.small ($90), ALB ($20), CloudFront+S3 ($50), Datadog ($300), Stripe fees on refund volume (variable). At 20 brand clients, ECS auto-scaling handles throughput increase; RDS may require upgrade to db.r6g.large at approximately $3500/month total for 20 brands.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Return intake and automated decision engine with decision_tree_version pinning (ISS-001), Stripe refund settlement with HMAC webhook verification (ISS-002), EasyPost label generation with SSE notification (ISS-003), condition assessment workflow for warehouse staff, basic disposition routing, brand admin decision tree builder, cost savings dashboard, Okta OIDC auth with RBAC, launch with 3 brand clients. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Consumer-facing self-service return portal (separate Next.js app), advanced analytics with channel-level recovered value breakdown, disposition channel webhook integrations for resale and refurbishment partners. Phase 3 (8 weeks): AI-powered condition grading from warehouse photos (computer vision model), cross-brand anonymized channel performance benchmarks, carrier rate-shopping integration for label cost optimization.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go domain packages organized under internal/ with explicit interface boundaries between returns, decisions, disposition, labels, and reports. All PostgreSQL queries use pgx with named parameter bindings — no string-interpolated SQL. golang-migrate for all schema changes with down migrations required. brand_id must never appear as a URL parameter that overrides JWT-derived brand_id — enforced by integration tests calling all endpoints with mismatched JWT brand_id asserting 403. Stripe webhook handler tested with real stripe.ConstructEvent() using test webhook secrets — never mocked in integration tests. CircleCI test job runs PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7 in service containers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis single-node Pub/Sub for SSE label notifications loses in-flight events on Redis restart. Mitigation: frontend SSE client reconnects and falls back to GET /returns/{id}/label polling on disconnect. Risk: decision_tree_versions.rules_json schema evolves and breaks the DecisionEngine parser for older pinned versions. Mitigation: rules_json has a schema_version field; DecisionEngine dispatches to version-specific rule evaluators. Tradeoff: single PostgreSQL schema with RLS versus per-brand schemas — RLS chosen for simpler migration tooling at MVP brand count, with migration plan to per-brand schemas at 50+ clients. Tradeoff: EasyPost over direct carrier API — EasyPost adds cost per label but eliminates multi-carrier integration complexity for MVP.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Okta over AWS Cognito for enterprise brand client SSO compatibility. Selected EasyPost over Shippo for broader carrier coverage. Decided decision tree stored as JSONB in PostgreSQL rather than a separate rules engine service for MVP simplicity. SSE chosen over WebSocket for label-ready notification — unidirectional push is sufficient and simpler to implement behind ALB.

metadata.notes: Apparel returns platform revision adding decision tree version pinning and Stripe webhook HMAC verification.

project_goal.value: Modernized vendor portal replacing a 2012 JSP/Struts admin panel, giving food suppliers real-time inventory management, promotion scheduling with category manager approval, and order fulfillment tracking for a large grocery delivery marketplace
project_goal.rationale: Legacy panel is a source of supplier friction and operational bottleneck for inventory accuracy

target_users.value: Food suppliers (inventory managers, trade marketing teams) and internal category managers who approve promotional campaigns
target_users.rationale: Two distinct user personas with different access levels — suppliers external, category managers internal

project_class.rationale: Browser-based supplier portal with server-rendered pages and realtime inventory updates

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, admin_panel, integrations, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Real-time inventory events, event bus integration, analytics dashboard, promotion approval workflow

complexity_level.rationale: Legacy migration, event bus integration, multi-role approval workflow, real-time inventory signals

risk_level.rationale: Partner-facing with food safety data and internal event bus access, but no financial payment processing

data_sensitivity.rationale: Supplier inventory and pricing data is commercially sensitive internal marketplace data

external_exposure.rationale: External food suppliers access the portal; internal category managers have elevated permissions

access_model.value: Keycloak OIDC with roles: supplier_user (own SKUs only), supplier_admin (all supplier account data), category_manager (all suppliers read + promotion approval), platform_admin (full access)
access_model.rationale: Role hierarchy matches supplier account isolation and internal approval workflow needs

feature_scope.value: Real-time inventory management with low-stock alerts, promotion scheduling with future effective dates, category manager approval workflow with audit trail, order fulfillment tracking and status updates, vendor performance scorecard, food safety compliance certification upload and expiry tracking, product catalogue management
feature_scope.rationale: Full supplier portal feature set replacing legacy JSP/Struts functionality plus modern capabilities

mvp_scope.value: Inventory replenishment submission with warehouse capacity validation, promotion scheduling with category manager approval, order fulfillment event feed, supplier dashboard with key performance metrics
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable to retire the 2012 panel for the top 50 suppliers

security_baseline.value: Keycloak OIDC JWT auth, supplier_account_id isolation on all queries, TLS 1.3, audit log for all inventory and price change events, OWASP top 10 controls
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing portal security baseline with audit trail for regulatory compliance

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 15 with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS
frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for fast initial supplier dashboard load; App Router for nested route layouts per role

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3 and Spring Data JPA
backend_stack.rationale: Existing engineering org Java expertise; Spring Boot for production-grade enterprise integration patterns

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for transactional data; Redis 7 for session cache and real-time inventory event buffer
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for complex supplier data relationships; Redis for sub-second inventory level reads

hosting_target.value: GCP GKE Autopilot in europe-west1; Cloud SQL PostgreSQL; Memorystore Redis
hosting_target.rationale: Marketplace already on GCP; GKE Autopilot reduces cluster management overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Supplier inventory and order data retained 5 years for audit; promotion change history retained 3 years; food safety certifications retained 7 years per FDA/EU FIC requirements
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Food safety regulatory record retention obligations vary by certification type

future_scope.value: AI-powered demand forecasting for suppliers, direct EDI integration for large retailers, mobile app for warehouse floor staff
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 expansion items building on portal foundation

constraints.value: Must maintain zero supplier data visibility across supplier accounts; inventory submissions must publish to internal event bus within 500ms; legacy JSP/Struts system decommissioned after 6-month parallel run
constraints.rationale: Event bus SLA driven by downstream marketplace order routing; parallel run required for supplier migration safety

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for metrics; structured JSON logging to GCP Cloud Logging; custom metrics for inventory submission latency, event bus publish success rate, promotion approval cycle time
observability_baseline.rationale: GCP-native logging with Grafana for operational dashboards

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI with feature flags for per-supplier rollout during parallel run phase
execution_preference.rationale: Feature flags enable gradual migration from legacy panel without hard cutover

project.goal: Replace legacy JSP/Struts vendor portal with modern Next.js + Spring Boot system
project.target_users: Food suppliers and internal category managers

frontend.realtime: Server-Sent Events for inventory alert feed
frontend.state: React Query for server state, lightweight Zustand for multi-step promotion wizard

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3 with Spring Data JPA, Spring Security for Keycloak integration
backend.event_bus: Pub/Sub client for marketplace internal event bus

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC JWT

data.db: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16
data.cache: Memorystore Redis 7

reasoner_reviews.product: The inventory replenishment and promotion scheduling features are the highest-friction points in the current JSP/Struts system — suppliers report that promotions require email back-and-forth with category managers because the old system has no approval workflow. The category manager approval with audit trail is a genuine product improvement that will also satisfy the marketplace's internal audit requirements for price changes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring Boot 3 on GKE Autopilot is well-suited to the enterprise integration pattern requirements — the marketplace event bus integration via Pub/Sub benefits from Spring's @PubSubListener abstraction. Next.js 15 App Router with per-role nested layouts is the right choice for the distinct supplier-versus-category-manager UI contexts. Real-time inventory alert delivery via SSE is simpler than WebSocket for this unidirectional push use case.
reasoner_reviews.security: Supplier account isolation is the most critical control — supplier_account_id must be enforced via Spring Security at the service layer, never trusting supplier_account_id from the request payload. Category managers can view all suppliers' data, so the RBAC model must explicitly distinguish read-all from modify-own permissions. The promotion audit trail must be stored in an append-only table to satisfy marketplace compliance requirements.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 500ms event bus publish SLA requires asynchronous Pub/Sub publishing with a synchronous ACK on the inventory submission API response — the API returns 200 after Pub/Sub publish confirmation, not after downstream consumer processing. The 6-month parallel run requires a feature flag system to route individual suppliers between old and new systems.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan did not specify how warehouse capacity constraints are validated during inventory submission. The requirement states submissions must validate against warehouse capacity — this requires integration with the marketplace's warehouse capacity service, not just client-side validation. The previous plan treated this as a UI concern only.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 15 App Router with route groups: (supplier) for supplier_user and supplier_admin routes, (internal) for category_manager and platform_admin routes. Each group has its own layout.tsx with role-specific navigation. Server Components fetch initial dashboard data; Client Components handle interactive inventory table editing and promotion wizard multi-step form.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Supplier Dashboard: inventory summary cards per product category with low-stock count badge, recent order fulfillment events feed, vendor performance scorecard (fill rate, on-time delivery, return rate). Inventory Management: paginated product_catalogue table with inline quantity edit for replenishment submissions, warehouse capacity indicator bar per SKU. Promotion Scheduler: multi-step wizard — product selection, discount type and percentage, effective date range, supporting materials upload. Shows pending approval badge after submission. Category Manager Approval Queue: list of pending promotion_schedules with supplier name, product range, discount depth, effective dates, approve/reject with required comment. Audit Trail: timeline view of all price and promotion changes per supplier.
frontend.state_management: React Query for inventory levels (30s stale time with SSE invalidation on low_stock_alert event), promotion list, and fulfillment feed. Zustand for promotion wizard multi-step state. SSE subscription to /api/v1/suppliers/{id}/alerts for real-time low-stock and capacity breach notifications.
frontend.frontend_risks: Risk: SSE connection drops on GKE pod restart require client-side reconnect logic with Last-Event-ID header. Mitigation: EventSource reconnect is automatic; backend tracks last emitted event_id per supplier for replay on reconnect.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot 3 application with service layer packages: inventory (replenishment submissions, capacity validation, Pub/Sub publish), promotions (scheduling, approval workflow, activation), catalogue (product management, food safety certifications), fulfillment (order event ingestion from Pub/Sub, status updates), analytics (vendor performance score computation), audit (append-only event recording).
backend.api_patterns: REST API with SpringDoc OpenAPI 3. supplier_account_id injected by Spring Security from JWT claims, never accepted from request body. Key endpoints use @PreAuthorize annotations for role checks. Pub/Sub publishing via Spring Cloud GCP @PubSubPublisher for inventory-updated events.
backend.business_modules: InventoryService validates replenishment quantity against warehouse capacity via synchronous REST call to internal WarehouseCapacityService, then writes inventory_replenishments record and publishes to Pub/Sub inventory-updated topic. PromotionApprovalService manages state machine: DRAFT -> PENDING_APPROVAL -> APPROVED/REJECTED -> ACTIVE/CANCELLED. FulfillmentEventConsumer subscribes to Pub/Sub order-events topic, maps events to fulfillment_events records per supplier.
backend.scaling_notes: GKE Autopilot handles burst scaling on inventory submission spikes (end-of-day replenishment batches from suppliers). Pub/Sub publish call is async with synchronous ACK; Spring @Async used for non-blocking publish confirmation tracking.

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC realm per environment. Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server validates JWT, extracts supplier_account_id from custom claim. category_manager and platform_admin roles do not have supplier_account_id claim — service layer handles this by skipping the supplier filter for those roles.
security.authorization_model: Spring Security method-level @PreAuthorize: supplier_user and supplier_admin endpoints enforce hasRole('SUPPLIER') and service layer applies WHERE supplier_account_id = :supplierFromJwt. category_manager endpoints use hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') with read access to all supplier data. Promotion approval requires hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') — supplier roles cannot self-approve.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_events table with PostgreSQL trigger preventing UPDATE/DELETE. All inventory submissions, price changes, promotion state transitions, and certification uploads written with actor_id, supplier_account_id, action, before_state JSONB, after_state JSONB, created_at. Spring @Aspect AuditAspect intercepts all service write methods.
security.security_risks: Risk: misconfigured Keycloak realm allows supplier roles to acquire category_manager privileges. Mitigation: Keycloak role assignment is restricted to platform_admin; Spring Security role checks are the enforcement boundary.

data.entities: supplier_accounts (id UUID PK, company_name, keycloak_group_id, tier ENUM bronze/silver/gold, created_at, is_active BOOL). product_catalogue (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, sku VARCHAR, name VARCHAR, category VARCHAR, barcode VARCHAR, current_price DECIMAL(10,2), is_active BOOL). inventory_levels (product_id FK, supplier_account_id FK, quantity_on_hand INT, quantity_incoming INT, reorder_threshold INT, last_updated TIMESTAMP — PRIMARY KEY product_id). inventory_replenishments (id UUID PK, product_id FK, supplier_account_id FK, quantity_submitted INT, warehouse_capacity_at_submission INT, status ENUM submitted/capacity_rejected/confirmed, pub_sub_message_id VARCHAR, submitted_at). promotion_schedules (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, product_ids UUID[], discount_type ENUM percentage/fixed_price/bogo, discount_value DECIMAL, effective_start DATE, effective_end DATE, status ENUM draft/pending_approval/approved/rejected/active/cancelled, submitted_at, reviewed_by FK NULLABLE, review_comment TEXT). purchase_orders (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, marketplace_order_id, total_units INT, status ENUM created/confirmed/shipped/delivered/cancelled, created_at). fulfillment_events (id UUID PK, purchase_order_id FK, supplier_account_id FK, event_type ENUM shipped/delayed/partial_delivered/delivered, pub_sub_message_id, event_at TIMESTAMP). compliance_certifications (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, cert_type VARCHAR, issuing_body VARCHAR, cert_number VARCHAR, issued_date DATE, expiry_date DATE, document_s3_key VARCHAR, status ENUM active/expiring_soon/expired). vendor_performance_scores (supplier_account_id FK, period_month DATE, fill_rate FLOAT, on_time_delivery_rate FLOAT, return_rate FLOAT, promotion_compliance_rate FLOAT, composite_score FLOAT, computed_at — PRIMARY KEY supplier_account_id, period_month)
data.retention_and_deletion: inventory_replenishments and fulfillment_events archived to Cloud Storage after 5 years. compliance_certifications retained 7 years per food safety regulations. promotion_schedules retained 3 years with full audit trail.
data.consistency_notes: inventory_levels updated via PostgreSQL advisory lock on product_id during concurrent replenishment submissions to prevent double-counting. Pub/Sub message deduplication via message_id stored in inventory_replenishments.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster europe-west1 with three Deployments: portal-api (Spring Boot, 2-10 pods HPA on CPU 70%), portal-frontend (Next.js, 2-6 pods), pubsub-consumer (fulfillment event consumer, 1-3 pods). GCP Load Balancer + Cloud Armor WAF in front of portal-api. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with read replica for analytics queries. Memorystore Redis for sessions and SSE event buffer.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: spotbugs + checkstyle for Java, ESLint for Next.js, JUnit 5 tests (85% coverage gate), Docker build + Artifact Registry push, GKE rolling deploy via kubectl. Feature flag service (LaunchDarkly) for per-supplier migration toggle during parallel run. Smoke test suite run against staging after each deploy.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics exposed on /actuator/prometheus by Spring Boot. Grafana dashboards: inventory_submission_latency_p95, pub_sub_publish_success_rate, promotion_approval_cycle_time_avg, sso_login_failure_rate. GCP Cloud Logging for structured JSON logs with supplier_account_id as indexed field. Alerting on pub_sub_publish_latency > 400ms (warn) and > 500ms (critical PagerDuty).

ISS-001.title: Warehouse capacity validation is client-side only
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 design validated inventory replenishment quantities only in the Next.js frontend using static capacity thresholds. The backend submitted quantities to Pub/Sub without calling the internal WarehouseCapacityService, allowing over-submissions that downstream warehouse systems had to reject after the fact.

history.note: InventoryService had no WarehouseCapacityService integration; capacity check was a frontend-only guard

ISS-002.title: Promotion self-approval gap — supplier could approve own promotions
ISS-002.detail: The promotion state machine in round 1 allowed a user with both supplier_admin and platform_admin roles to advance a promotion from PENDING_APPROVAL to APPROVED using the approval endpoint, bypassing the requirement that only category_manager role can approve.

history.note: PromotionApprovalService checked hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') OR hasRole('PLATFORM_ADMIN') — platform_admin should not approve

ISS-003.title: SSE reconnect does not replay missed low-stock alerts
ISS-003.detail: On SSE disconnect and reconnect, the client does not send Last-Event-ID, and the server does not replay events from the reconnect point, causing suppliers to miss low-stock alerts that fired during the disconnected window.

history.note: SSE handler emitted events without id field; client EventSource did not include Last-Event-ID on reconnect

focus_issues.title: Warehouse capacity validation is client-side only
focus_issues.detail: Backend submits to Pub/Sub without calling WarehouseCapacityService, enabling over-submissions.

focus_issues.title: Promotion self-approval gap
focus_issues.detail: platform_admin role incorrectly included in promotion approval authorization check.

latest_recommendations: Add synchronous call to WarehouseCapacityService in InventoryService before Pub/Sub publish; return 422 with remaining capacity if submission exceeds available warehouse space
Restrict promotion approval endpoint to hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') only — remove PLATFORM_ADMIN from approval authorization
Add numeric id field to all SSE events; backend stores last N alerts per supplier in Redis; replay from Last-Event-ID on reconnect
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Grocery Delivery Vendor Portal — Round 1 Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two high severity issues unresolved — capacity validation bypass and promotion approval gap compromise operational integrity

previous_audits.summary: Good foundation with Spring Boot Pub/Sub integration and Next.js 15 App Router role segmentation. Two high-severity operational and authorization gaps block production readiness for the top-50 supplier launch.
strengths: GKE Autopilot with HPA correctly handles end-of-day inventory submission spikes
Keycloak OIDC with supplier_account_id JWT claim provides solid isolation foundation
Append-only audit_events with AuditAspect is well-designed for compliance
concerns: Backend inventory service submits to Pub/Sub without server-side capacity validation
PLATFORM_ADMIN role included in promotion approval check creates self-approval risk
SSE events missing id field prevents Last-Event-ID replay on reconnect
blocking_issues: Warehouse over-submission causes downstream warehouse system rejections and supply chain disruption
Promotion self-approval violates marketplace pricing governance policy
recommendations: In InventoryService.submitReplenishment(), call WarehouseCapacityService.checkCapacity(productId, quantity) synchronously before writing to DB or publishing to Pub/Sub; return HTTP 422 with available_capacity field if capacity check fails
Update PromotionApprovalService @PreAuthorize to hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') — remove OR condition on PLATFORM_ADMIN

issue_updates.title: Server-side capacity validation missing
issue_updates.detail: InventoryService.submitReplenishment() publishes to Pub/Sub inventory-updated topic without calling any warehouse capacity check

issue_updates.title: Promotion approval grants platform_admin self-approval
issue_updates.detail: @PreAuthorize('hasRole(CATEGORY_MANAGER) or hasRole(PLATFORM_ADMIN)') on approvePromotion endpoint allows supplier_admin + platform_admin dual-role users to self-approve

previous_plan.title: Grocery Delivery Vendor Portal — Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Next.js 15 frontend and Spring Boot 3 backend on GKE Autopilot replacing legacy JSP/Struts vendor panel. Inventory management with Pub/Sub integration, promotion scheduling with category manager approval, fulfillment event feed.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: GKE Autopilot: portal-api (Spring Boot), portal-frontend (Next.js), pubsub-consumer. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL, Memorystore Redis. Keycloak OIDC. SSE for real-time alerts without id fields (ISS-003 root cause). InventoryService submits to Pub/Sub without capacity check (ISS-001 root cause).
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak OIDC, supplier_account_id from JWT, TLS 1.3. PromotionApprovalService @PreAuthorize included PLATFORM_ADMIN erroneously (ISS-002 root cause).
previous_plan.system_components: Standard three-service GKE deployment without WarehouseCapacityService integration in InventoryService.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the grocery delivery vendor portal to address two high-severity operational gaps: adding synchronous WarehouseCapacityService validation in InventoryService before any Pub/Sub publish (ISS-001), and restricting promotion approval to hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') only, removing the erroneous PLATFORM_ADMIN inclusion (ISS-002). Also fixing SSE event id fields and Redis-backed replay on reconnect (ISS-003). The Spring Boot Pub/Sub integration, Keycloak OIDC supplier isolation, and GKE Autopilot topology from round 1 are retained.

fix_report.action_taken: Added synchronous call to internal WarehouseCapacityService REST endpoint in InventoryService.submitReplenishment() before writing to inventory_replenishments table or publishing to Pub/Sub. If available warehouse capacity is less than submitted quantity, method returns HTTP 422 with body {error: 'capacity_exceeded', available_capacity: N, submitted: M}. WarehouseCapacityService URL injected via Spring @Value from GKE ConfigMap.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Over-submissions are rejected at the vendor portal API before reaching Pub/Sub, eliminating downstream warehouse system rejections from over-committed quantities.

fix_report.action_taken: Changed @PreAuthorize annotation on PromotionApprovalService.approvePromotion() from hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') or hasRole('PLATFORM_ADMIN') to hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') exclusively. PLATFORM_ADMIN role retains read access to promotion data but cannot advance promotion state to APPROVED. Added integration test verifying that a user with PLATFORM_ADMIN + SUPPLIER_ADMIN dual roles receives 403 on the approval endpoint.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Only designated category managers can approve promotions; brand admins with elevated platform access cannot self-approve price changes, preserving marketplace pricing governance integrity.

target_output.title: Grocery Delivery Vendor Portal — Architecture Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A modernized vendor portal for a large grocery delivery marketplace built with Next.js 15 and Spring Boot 3 on GCP GKE Autopilot, replacing a 2012 JSP/Struts admin panel. Provides food suppliers with real-time inventory management, promotion scheduling with mandatory category manager approval, order fulfillment tracking, and vendor performance scorecards. This revision adds server-side warehouse capacity validation before inventory Pub/Sub publish and restricts promotion approval to category_manager role only, resolving the two high-severity operational and authorization gaps from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-service GKE Autopilot deployment on europe-west1: portal-api (Spring Boot 3) handles REST API requests, publishes inventory-updated events to GCP Pub/Sub, and serves SSE streams for real-time supplier alerts; portal-frontend (Next.js 15) renders role-segmented supplier and category manager dashboards with App Router nested layouts; pubsub-consumer processes inbound order-events from the marketplace event bus and writes fulfillment_events. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with read replica handles all transactional data; Memorystore Redis stores Spring Sessions and SSE alert replay buffers per supplier. InventoryService synchronously calls the internal WarehouseCapacityService before submitting any replenishment to Pub/Sub. Keycloak OIDC JWT carries supplier_account_id and role; Spring Security enforces supplier isolation via service-layer checks and @PreAuthorize annotations. Promotion approval is gated exclusively behind hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') with full audit trail in append-only audit_events.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 15, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, native EventSource API with Last-Event-ID replay. Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3, Spring Data JPA (Hibernate 6), Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server for Keycloak JWT, Spring Cloud GCP for Pub/Sub, SpringDoc OpenAPI 3. Database: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with read replica. Cache/Sessions: Memorystore Redis 7 via Spring Session Data Redis. Auth: Keycloak OIDC. Hosting: GKE Autopilot europe-west1, GCP Load Balancer + Cloud Armor. CI/CD: GitLab CI. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana, GCP Cloud Logging. Feature flags: LaunchDarkly for parallel run migration.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Inventory Replenishment: supplier submits quantity per SKU; Spring Boot calls WarehouseCapacityService synchronously, returns 422 with remaining capacity on failure, publishes inventory-updated to Pub/Sub on success. Low-stock SSE Alerts: Redis-buffered low-stock events pushed to supplier via SSE with numeric event ids; Last-Event-ID replay on reconnect. Promotion Scheduling Wizard: supplier creates promotion draft with product selection, discount structure, and date range; submits for approval triggering notification to category managers. Category Manager Approval: approval queue with supplier details, discount depth, date range; approve with optional comment or reject with required rationale; only hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') may advance state. Order Fulfillment Feed: real-time fulfillment_events from marketplace Pub/Sub displayed as timeline per purchase_order. Vendor Performance Scorecard: monthly fill_rate, on-time delivery, return_rate, and composite_score charts. Food Safety Certification Tracker: upload panel with expiry date tracking and 60-day advance renewal alerts.
target_output.system_components: GCP Load Balancer with Cloud Armor WAF rules (OWASP top 10 ruleset) terminating TLS 1.3 in front of GKE cluster. portal-api Spring Boot pods: JwtAuthFilter (validates Keycloak JWT, injects supplier_account_id and role into SecurityContext), InventoryService (validates via WarehouseCapacityService REST call before Pub/Sub publish), PromotionApprovalService (@PreAuthorize hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') exclusively on approval methods), SSEController (GET /api/v1/suppliers/{id}/alerts returning Server-Sent Events with id field, replay from Redis buffer keyed as sse:alerts:{supplier_id} on Last-Event-ID). pubsub-consumer pods: FulfillmentEventConsumer (@PubSubListener on order-events subscription, writes fulfillment_events with supplier_account_id from event payload, idempotent on pub_sub_message_id). portal-frontend Next.js pods: route groups (supplier) and (internal) with role-enforced layouts. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with read replica serving analytics queries. Memorystore Redis for Spring Session and SSE replay buffer (capped at 100 events per supplier, TTL 1 hour). WarehouseCapacityService: internal GKE service with REST endpoint GET /capacity/{product_id} returning available_units and max_units.
target_output.workflows: Inventory Replenishment Submission: supplier_user submits POST /api/v1/inventory/replenishments with product_id and quantity. JwtAuthFilter injects supplier_account_id from JWT. InventoryService retrieves product, verifies product.supplier_account_id matches JWT supplier_account_id (403 if mismatch). Calls WarehouseCapacityService GET /capacity/{product_id} — if quantity > available_capacity, returns HTTP 422 {error: 'capacity_exceeded', available_capacity: N}. On capacity OK, writes inventory_replenishments record with status=submitted and warehouse_capacity_at_submission, publishes inventory-updated Pub/Sub message (Spring Cloud GCP async publish with ACK confirmation), returns 201. Promotion Approval Workflow: supplier_admin submits multi-step wizard, creates promotion_schedules with status=draft, then submits (status=pending_approval). Keycloak triggers email notification to category managers. Category manager opens approval queue, reviews promotion detail, calls PATCH /api/v1/promotions/{id}/approve with optional comment. PromotionApprovalService verifies hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') — if calling user has PLATFORM_ADMIN but not CATEGORY_MANAGER, returns 403. On authorized approve, updates status=approved, writes audit_events record with before/after state, schedules activation job for effective_start date.
target_output.data_model: supplier_accounts (id UUID PK, company_name VARCHAR, keycloak_group_id VARCHAR, tier ENUM bronze/silver/gold, created_at, is_active BOOL). product_catalogue (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, sku VARCHAR UNIQUE, name VARCHAR, category VARCHAR, barcode VARCHAR, current_price DECIMAL(10,2), is_active BOOL). inventory_levels (product_id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, quantity_on_hand INT, quantity_incoming INT, reorder_threshold INT, last_updated TIMESTAMP). inventory_replenishments (id UUID PK, product_id FK, supplier_account_id FK, quantity_submitted INT, warehouse_capacity_at_submission INT, status ENUM submitted/capacity_rejected/confirmed, pub_sub_message_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, submitted_at). promotion_schedules (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, product_ids UUID[], discount_type ENUM percentage/fixed_price/bogo, discount_value DECIMAL(5,2), effective_start DATE, effective_end DATE, status ENUM draft/pending_approval/approved/rejected/active/cancelled, submitted_at, reviewed_by UUID FK NULLABLE, review_comment TEXT NULLABLE). purchase_orders (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, marketplace_order_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, total_units INT, status ENUM created/confirmed/shipped/delivered/cancelled, created_at). fulfillment_events (id UUID PK, purchase_order_id FK, supplier_account_id FK, event_type ENUM shipped/delayed/partial_delivered/delivered, pub_sub_message_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, event_at TIMESTAMP). compliance_certifications (id UUID PK, supplier_account_id FK, cert_type VARCHAR, issuing_body VARCHAR, cert_number VARCHAR, issued_date DATE, expiry_date DATE, document_gcs_key VARCHAR, status ENUM active/expiring_soon/expired). vendor_performance_scores (supplier_account_id FK, period_month DATE, fill_rate FLOAT, on_time_delivery_rate FLOAT, return_rate FLOAT, promotion_compliance_rate FLOAT, composite_score FLOAT, computed_at — PRIMARY KEY supplier_account_id, period_month). audit_events (id UUID PK, actor_id FK, supplier_account_id UUID, action VARCHAR, before_state JSONB, after_state JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMP — no UPDATE/DELETE via trigger).
target_output.api_design: All supplier-scoped endpoints derive supplier_account_id from JWT — never from request body. POST /api/v1/inventory/replenishments — submit replenishment, calls WarehouseCapacityService synchronously, returns 422 on capacity exceeded with available_capacity. GET /api/v1/inventory — paginated inventory_levels for authenticated supplier. GET /api/v1/suppliers/{id}/alerts — SSE stream with id field for Last-Event-ID replay. POST /api/v1/promotions — create promotion draft. PATCH /api/v1/promotions/{id}/submit — advance to pending_approval. PATCH /api/v1/promotions/{id}/approve — @PreAuthorize hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') only, updates status to approved. PATCH /api/v1/promotions/{id}/reject — @PreAuthorize hasRole('CATEGORY_MANAGER') only, requires review_comment. GET /api/v1/promotions?status=pending_approval — category manager approval queue (returns all suppliers' promotions for CATEGORY_MANAGER role). GET /api/v1/purchase-orders — fulfillment event feed for authenticated supplier. GET /api/v1/analytics/vendor-performance — monthly scorecard for authenticated supplier.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak OIDC realm with supplier_account_id as a custom JWT claim; Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server validates token and extracts SecurityContext. InventoryService and all supplier-scoped services extract supplier_account_id from SecurityContextHolder — never from request parameters. @PreAuthorize('hasRole(CATEGORY_MANAGER)') exclusively on promotion approval and rejection endpoints — no PLATFORM_ADMIN bypass. Cloud Armor WAF with OWASP CRS rules blocks SQL injection and XSS at load balancer level. audit_events table protected by PostgreSQL rule preventing UPDATE/DELETE at database level. Spring @Aspect AuditAspect intercepts all @Audited annotated service methods, writes before/after state to audit_events. Food safety certifications stored in GCS with signed URL access — documents never returned inline in API responses. TLS 1.3 enforced at GCP Load Balancer. Keycloak role assignment restricted to platform_admin role via Keycloak admin console RBAC policy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline: spotbugs, checkstyle, JUnit 5 (85% coverage gate on service layer), ESLint for Next.js, Docker multi-stage builds to Artifact Registry, GKE rolling deploy via kubectl apply with readinessProbe on /actuator/health. GKE Autopilot HPA: portal-api scales 2-10 pods on CPU 70%, portal-frontend 2-6 pods on request rate, pubsub-consumer 1-3 pods on Pub/Sub subscription message count custom metric. Feature flag: LaunchDarkly toggles per supplier account between legacy JSP portal URL and new portal URL during 6-month parallel run — supplier-by-supplier migration with rollback toggle. Staging environment mirrors production topology; smoke test suite run on staging post-deploy before LaunchDarkly percentage rollout increase. Cloud SQL daily automated backups with 7-day retention; PITR enabled for 3-day window.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics via Spring Boot Actuator /actuator/prometheus: http_request_duration_seconds (labeled by endpoint and supplier_tier), inventory_submission_total, pub_sub_publish_latency_seconds, warehouse_capacity_service_call_duration_seconds. Grafana dashboards: inventory submission rate and capacity rejection rate, promotion approval cycle time distribution, Pub/Sub publish success rate and latency p95, SSE active connection count per supplier. GCP Cloud Logging with structured JSON; supplier_account_id as indexed LogField for per-supplier incident investigation. Alerting: Pub/Sub publish latency > 400ms triggers warn alert; > 500ms triggers PagerDuty page (breaches 500ms SLA). Warehouse capacity service call failure rate > 5% triggers critical alert.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1400/month at 50 suppliers: GKE Autopilot node pool ($600), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL with read replica ($350), Memorystore Redis ($120), GCP Load Balancer ($50), Cloud Logging and Pub/Sub ($80), LaunchDarkly Starter ($80), Keycloak on GKE ($0 self-hosted). At 500 suppliers with higher inventory submission frequency, Cloud SQL may require upgrade to db-custom-4-16384 and pubsub-consumer scales to 10 pods at approximately $3200/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Inventory replenishment with server-side WarehouseCapacityService validation (ISS-001 fix), promotion scheduling with category_manager-only approval (ISS-002 fix), SSE low-stock alerts with Last-Event-ID replay (ISS-003 fix), order fulfillment event feed, vendor performance scorecard, Keycloak OIDC auth with RBAC, LaunchDarkly feature flag for parallel run, launch to top 50 suppliers. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Food safety certification upload and expiry tracking, product catalogue management with bulk import CSV, advanced promotion analytics for category managers, audit trail export for compliance reporting. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Supplier self-service onboarding workflow, EDI integration for large retailer bulk order submission, mobile-responsive redesign for warehouse floor staff access.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java strict constructor injection (no field injection) for all Spring beans — enforced via ArchUnit test. All JPA queries use named parameters, no native query string concatenation. supplier_account_id in service methods always sourced from Spring SecurityContextHolder — unit tests inject a mock SecurityContext, never hardcode test supplier IDs. Integration tests run against PostgreSQL 16 and Redis via Testcontainers. @PreAuthorize annotations tested via @WithMockUser in Spring Security test framework — approval endpoint must return 403 for PLATFORM_ADMIN role. GitLab CI fails if spotbugs or checkstyle violations exist.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WarehouseCapacityService is a synchronous dependency — if it is unavailable, inventory submissions fail. Mitigation: circuit breaker via Resilience4j with fallback returning a 503 with retry_after hint; capacity service is an internal GKE service with 99.9% uptime SLA. Risk: LaunchDarkly dependency during parallel run — if LaunchDarkly API is unavailable, flag evaluation falls back to cached value (default=legacy portal URL). Tradeoff: server-side SSE over WebSocket — SSE is unidirectional but sufficient for inventory alerts and simpler to proxy through GCP Load Balancer without upgrade headers. Tradeoff: Cloud SQL single-region over AlloyDB — Cloud SQL is 40% cheaper at current scale; AlloyDB migration path documented for > 500 suppliers.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed GCP Pub/Sub over Kafka for marketplace event bus integration — existing marketplace infrastructure uses Pub/Sub. Chose Keycloak self-hosted on GKE over Google Identity Platform for enterprise SSO compatibility with supplier SAML IdPs. Selected Last-Event-ID replay over WebSocket for low-stock alerts — unidirectional push is sufficient. Warehouse capacity validation confirmed as synchronous blocking call per product manager requirement that suppliers see immediate rejection with available capacity.

metadata.notes: Grocery delivery vendor portal revision adding server-side warehouse capacity validation and restricting promotion approval to category_manager role.

project_goal.value: Research prototype exploring a unified storefront API gateway that aggregates product, inventory, and pricing data from disparate internal microservices for a multi-brand e-commerce conglomerate, testing GraphQL federation as a simplification strategy to reduce direct microservice coupling
project_goal.rationale: Engineering leadership wants evidence that federation reduces round-trips and simplifies storefront consumer code before committing to a full migration

target_users.value: Internal platform engineers and frontend teams who currently call 5-8 microservices directly to assemble a product page; the prototype will benchmark whether federation reduces integration complexity
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are internal engineers evaluating federation viability, not end users

project_class.rationale: Exploratory prototype for architectural decision validation, not a production system

capabilities.value: backend, data, public_api, integrations
capabilities.rationale: API gateway with subgraph integrations; no frontend or auth complexity in prototype scope

complexity_level.rationale: Apollo Federation 2 supergraph composition, multi-subgraph resolver tracing, and synthetic load benchmarking require deep GraphQL expertise

risk_level.rationale: Internal-only prototype with no real customer data; uses synthetic product data seeded from internal fixtures

data_sensitivity.rationale: Only internal fixture data in prototype; no real customer, order, or payment data processed

external_exposure.rationale: Prototype accessible only on internal VPN; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Internal service tokens via shared Authorization header; no user-level auth in prototype scope
access_model.rationale: Prototype focuses on federation architecture, not auth patterns

feature_scope.value: Federated product subgraph (brand, title, variants, images), inventory subgraph (stock levels, warehouse locations, availability), pricing subgraph (base price, promotional price, brand-tier discounts); Apollo Router supergraph composition at build time; resolver latency tracing; N+1 query detection; synthetic load benchmarking versus direct microservice baseline
feature_scope.rationale: Covers the three highest-traffic data domains for a product detail page load

mvp_scope.value: Product and inventory subgraphs federated via Apollo Router; synthetic load test comparing federation versus direct REST calls for a product page query; resolver trace dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum scope to produce the latency and round-trip data engineering leadership needs for the migration decision

security_baseline.value: Internal service token validation on Apollo Router; no external traffic; TLS on intra-cluster subgraph calls
security_baseline.rationale: Prototype internal security baseline sufficient for VPN-only access

frontend_stack.value: none — prototype is API gateway only; no UI beyond Apollo Sandbox for query exploration
frontend_stack.rationale: research_prototype focused on API layer performance measurement

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Apollo Federation 2, Apollo Router (Rust-based), Redis 7 for field-level resolver caching
backend_stack.rationale: Apollo Federation 2 is the leading GraphQL federation implementation; Rust-based Apollo Router for production-grade request routing

data_platform.value: None — pass-through gateway; subgraphs call downstream REST microservices and cache selected fields in Redis
data_platform.rationale: Prototype does not own data; it federates from existing microservices

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate single-environment; no multi-AZ needed for prototype
hosting_target.rationale: Single environment sufficient for benchmark measurements

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data in prototype; synthetic fixture data only; resolver traces retained for 30 days for analysis
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Prototype scope excludes real data processing

future_scope.value: If prototype validates federation, production rollout would add pricing subgraph, auth token forwarding, schema registry with managed federation, and gradual migration of storefront consumers off direct REST calls
future_scope.rationale: Prototype decision gate before committing engineering capacity to production migration

constraints.value: Prototype must use real internal microservice APIs (not mocks) for benchmark validity; total prototype delivery in 4 weeks; resolver traces must be exportable for latency analysis
constraints.rationale: Real API calls are required for benchmark data to be credible in migration decision

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for ECS task metrics; Apollo Router built-in telemetry exporting resolver traces to OTLP collector; custom benchmark output as JSON reports in S3
observability_baseline.rationale: OTLP resolver traces are the primary output artifact of the prototype

execution_preference.value: Spike-style development: build subgraphs in week 1-2, compose supergraph and run benchmarks in week 3, analyze and document findings in week 4
execution_preference.rationale: 4-week spike cadence drives toward a clear architectural decision output

project.goal: Validate GraphQL federation as a unification strategy for multi-brand storefront microservices
project.target_users: Internal platform engineers making migration decision

backend.framework: Apollo Server 4 for subgraphs, Apollo Router (Rust) as supergraph gateway
backend.caching: Redis 7 for repeated field resolver results
backend.api: GraphQL via Apollo Federation 2 subgraph specs

security.auth: Internal service token header validation on Apollo Router

data.db: none — pass-through to downstream REST APIs
data.cache: Redis 7 for resolver-level caching of high-frequency fields
data.tracing: OpenTelemetry trace export via Apollo Router OTLP exporter

constraints.real_apis: must call real internal microservice REST APIs

open_questions.n_plus_one_mitigation: evaluate DataLoader vs router-level batching directive
open_questions.subgraph_error_isolation: determine if partial data responses are acceptable for prototype benchmarks

reasoner_reviews.product: The prototype scope is correctly scoped to the product detail page use case — that is the highest-read-traffic query path and the one where N+1 queries and round-trip overhead are most impactful. Measuring both latency distribution and round-trip count under synthetic load will give engineering leadership the data they need for the migration decision. The 4-week timeline is tight but achievable for a three-subgraph prototype.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Apollo Federation 2 with the Rust-based Apollo Router is the right implementation choice — the Router's built-in subgraph request planning and field-level caching configuration are purpose-built for this scenario. Composing the supergraph at build time via rover dev (or rover supergraph compose) gives immediate visibility into schema conflicts between product, inventory, and pricing subgraphs. The N+1 problem for inventory lookups across product variants must be addressed with DataLoader or Apollo Rover batch directives to make the latency comparison fair.
reasoner_reviews.security: Internal service token forwarding from Apollo Router to subgraphs using header propagation config is the appropriate minimal auth pattern for a VPN-only prototype. Subgraphs should validate the token header is present and reject requests without it — even for an internal prototype this prevents accidental direct subgraph calls bypassing the router.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 4-week timeline is achievable but requires starting with the product subgraph wiring immediately. Using real internal microservice APIs adds network latency variance to benchmarks — the benchmark report must account for this by measuring the direct REST baseline under identical conditions from the same ECS task. The OTLP trace export must be configured from day 1 to capture resolver latency data throughout the benchmark runs.

backend.service_design: Three Apollo Server 4 subgraph services: ProductSubgraph (entities: Product, ProductVariant, Brand), InventorySubgraph (entity extensions: Product.inventoryLevel, ProductVariant.stockStatus), PricingSubgraph (entity extensions: Product.basePrice, ProductVariant.promotionalPrice). Apollo Router as the supergraph gateway composing the three subgraph schemas via supergraph.graphql built by rover supergraph compose.
backend.api_patterns: Federation entity resolution: ProductSubgraph owns the Product type with @key(fields: 'sku'). InventorySubgraph extends Product with @key(fields: 'sku') @external and resolves inventoryLevel via REST call to InventoryMicroservice. PricingSubgraph extends ProductVariant with @key(fields: 'sku variantId') and resolves promotionalPrice via PricingMicroservice. Apollo Router handles query planning and subgraph fan-out.
backend.failure_handling: Apollo Router @connect directive marks subgraph fields as nullable with partial data response on subgraph timeout. If InventorySubgraph is unavailable, product queries return product data with null inventoryLevel rather than failing the full request. Subgraph timeout set to 500ms; Router overall timeout 2000ms.
backend.backend_risks: Risk: schema composition conflicts between subgraphs if downstream microservice field names do not align with federation type names. Mitigation: run rover subgraph check on each subgraph schema against the schema registry before compose. Risk: N+1 queries on ProductVariant.inventoryLevel resolver without DataLoader causing 50x REST calls per product list query. Mitigation: DataLoader batching in InventorySubgraph groups variant inventory lookups into a single batch REST call.

security.auth_design: Apollo Router configured with header propagation: x-internal-service-token header forwarded to all subgraphs. Router-level inline plugin validates token presence and value against a hardcoded allowlist (acceptable for internal prototype). Subgraphs validate x-internal-service-token header and return 401 on missing or invalid token.
security.secrets_management: Service token stored in AWS Secrets Manager, injected as ECS task definition environment variable INTERNAL_SERVICE_TOKEN. Hardcoded token allowlist in Router plugin config is acceptable for 4-week prototype scope.
security.security_risks: Risk: direct subgraph endpoint exposure if ECS security group is misconfigured. Mitigation: ECS security group allows inbound only from Apollo Router task security group; subgraph tasks have no public IP.

data.entities: subgraph_schemas (Product type: sku, brandId, title, description, imageUrls, variants — owned by ProductSubgraph), federated_products (Product entity resolved by ProductSubgraph REST calls to ProductMicroservice GET /products/{sku}), inventory_entities (ProductVariant.stockStatus ENUM in_stock/low_stock/out_of_stock, Product.inventoryLevel {warehouseId, quantityAvailable} — resolved by InventorySubgraph from InventoryMicroservice GET /inventory/batch), pricing_variants (ProductVariant.basePrice DECIMAL, ProductVariant.promotionalPrice DECIMAL, Product.tierDiscount FLOAT by brand tier — resolved by PricingSubgraph from PricingMicroservice GET /pricing/variants), supergraph_operations (composed supergraph SDL stored in supergraph.graphql, rover supergraph compose output), resolver_traces (OpenTelemetry spans per resolver invocation: subgraph, field path, duration_ms, cache_hit BOOL, downstream_service — exported via OTLP to CloudWatch)
data.schema_notes: Apollo Federation 2 @key directives use 'sku' for Product and 'sku variantId' compound key for ProductVariant. @shareable applied to Product.title to allow both ProductSubgraph and a future SEOSubgraph to return it. rover subgraph publish used to register each subgraph schema to Apollo managed federation registry (free tier for prototype).
data.data_risks: Risk: ProductMicroservice returns inconsistent sku field naming (snake_case vs camelCase) compared to GraphQL schema conventions. Mitigation: subgraph resolver maps snake_case REST response fields to camelCase GraphQL types explicitly.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate single environment: apollo-router task (1 instance, 1 vCPU, 2GB — Rust binary, minimal resources), product-subgraph task (1 instance, 0.5 vCPU), inventory-subgraph task (1 instance, 0.5 vCPU), pricing-subgraph task (1 instance, 0.5 vCPU), redis task (1 instance, cache.t3.micro via ElastiCache). ECS tasks in private subnet; VPN access via AWS Client VPN or existing office VPN gateway. Internal ALB routes /graphql to apollo-router task.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: subgraph schema lint via rover subgraph check, Node.js unit tests (subgraph resolver logic), Docker build + ECR push, ECS task definition update + service redeploy. Benchmark runner as a separate GitHub Actions workflow triggered manually: k6 script hitting Apollo Router /graphql endpoint with synthetic product page query, outputting P50/P95/P99 latency and request count metrics to JSON, uploaded to S3.
devops.cost_controls: Prototype estimated $180/month: 5 ECS Fargate tasks at t3-equivalent sizing ($120), ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.micro ($40), ALB ($15), CloudWatch logs ($5). Single environment, no staging/production overhead. Prototype will run for 4 weeks only — total cost approximately $45.
