target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a GraphQL federation research prototype to validate whether Apollo Federation 2 reduces round-trips and integration complexity for multi-brand storefront product page queries. Key decisions: three subgraphs (product, inventory, pricing) composing via Apollo Router; DataLoader batching in InventorySubgraph to prevent N+1 on variant inventory lookups; OTLP trace export from Apollo Router for resolver latency analysis; real internal microservice calls required for benchmark validity; minimal auth via service token for VPN-only internal prototype.
target_output.title: GraphQL Federation Storefront Gateway — Research Prototype Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A 4-week research prototype implementing Apollo Federation 2 to unify product, inventory, and pricing data from three disparate internal microservices behind a single GraphQL supergraph gateway. Built with Apollo Server 4 subgraphs, Apollo Router (Rust), and Redis field-level caching on AWS ECS Fargate. The prototype measures resolver latency distribution, N+1 query reduction via DataLoader batching, and round-trip count versus a direct REST baseline to inform whether GraphQL federation is viable for the multi-brand e-commerce conglomerate's storefront modernization.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pass-through supergraph gateway architecture with no owned data store. Apollo Router (Rust binary) composes three Apollo Server 4 subgraphs at build time via rover supergraph compose to produce a unified supergraph SDL. Inbound GraphQL queries hit the Router's /graphql endpoint; the Router's query planner determines which subgraphs to fan out to, parallelizing independent field resolution. ProductSubgraph owns the Product type and calls ProductMicroservice REST API. InventorySubgraph extends Product and ProductVariant entities and calls InventoryMicroservice batch endpoint via DataLoader. PricingSubgraph extends ProductVariant with price fields and calls PricingMicroservice. Redis 7 caches high-frequency resolver results (product titles, brand metadata) with short TTLs. OpenTelemetry resolver traces exported via Apollo Router OTLP exporter to CloudWatch for latency analysis. Deployed on ECS Fargate in a private subnet, accessible only via internal VPN.
target_output.technology_stack: Apollo Federation 2 (subgraph spec 2.5). Apollo Router 1.x (Rust binary, OTLP trace export, header propagation, field-level caching config). Apollo Server 4 for each Node.js 22 subgraph. DataLoader (npm) for batched inventory REST calls in InventorySubgraph. Redis 7 via ElastiCache for resolver cache. rover CLI for schema composition and subgraph publishing to Apollo managed registry. k6 for synthetic load benchmarking. OpenTelemetry SDK for custom resolver spans. AWS ECS Fargate single environment. GitHub Actions for CI and benchmark workflow.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Product Subgraph: resolves Product by sku (title, brandId, description, imageUrls[]), ProductVariant list (sku, variantId, size, color) via ProductMicroservice REST. Inventory Subgraph: extends Product with inventoryLevel (warehouseId, quantityAvailable) and ProductVariant with stockStatus ENUM via batched InventoryMicroservice GET /inventory/batch?skus=. Pricing Subgraph: extends ProductVariant with basePrice, promotionalPrice, and Product with tierDiscount by brand tier via PricingMicroservice GET /pricing/variants. Apollo Router Composition: supergraph.graphql produced by rover supergraph compose from three subgraph SDL files; Router executes federated query plans with parallel subgraph fan-out. Benchmark Harness: k6 script sends ProductDetailQuery (fetching Product with variants, each with inventoryLevel, stockStatus, basePrice, promotionalPrice) at 50/100/200 RPS; records P50/P95/P99 latency per RPS tier; compares against direct REST baseline script hitting the three microservices independently.
target_output.system_components: Apollo Router ECS task (1 vCPU, 2GB): receives /graphql POST, validates x-internal-service-token header, executes query plan, fans out to subgraphs, merges responses. Router config: header_propagation forwards x-internal-service-token to all subgraphs; override_subgraph_url maps each subgraph to its ECS internal DNS name; telemetry.tracing.otlp configured to export resolver spans to CloudWatch OTLP receiver. ProductSubgraph ECS task (Node.js 22, 0.5 vCPU): Apollo Server 4 with Product @key(fields: 'sku') type, resolveReference calling ProductMicroservice GET /products/{sku}, Redis cache for product metadata (TTL 60s). InventorySubgraph ECS task (Node.js 22, 0.5 vCPU): extends Product and ProductVariant entities, DataLoader batch function calling InventoryMicroservice GET /inventory/batch?skus=sku1,sku2 grouping up to 50 variant lookups per batch window, Redis cache for stock status (TTL 30s). PricingSubgraph ECS task (Node.js 22, 0.5 vCPU): extends ProductVariant with pricing fields, calls PricingMicroservice GET /pricing/variants with brand tier header, Redis cache for promotional prices (TTL 15s — promotional prices change frequently). Internal ALB routing /graphql to Apollo Router. ElastiCache Redis 7 shared across subgraphs for field-level caching. Benchmark runner: GitHub Actions workflow running k6 benchmark script, uploading JSON output to S3 benchmark-results/ prefix.
target_output.workflows: Federated Product Detail Query: Client sends ProductDetailQuery to Apollo Router /graphql with x-internal-service-token header. Router validates token. Query planner determines execution plan: fetch Product fields from ProductSubgraph in parallel with initial pricing fields. ProductSubgraph resolveReference checks Redis for product:{sku}, on miss calls ProductMicroservice GET /products/{sku}, caches result. Router receives product response, extracts variant skus for inventory and pricing fan-out. InventorySubgraph DataLoader batches all variant sku lookups into single InventoryMicroservice GET /inventory/batch?skus=sku1,sku2,...sku12, caches stockStatus per variant in Redis (TTL 30s). PricingSubgraph fetches variant prices via PricingMicroservice GET /pricing/variants, caches in Redis with 15s TTL. Router merges all subgraph responses into unified GraphQL response. OTLP spans emitted per subgraph call with resolver field path, subgraph name, cache_hit boolean, and duration_ms. Benchmark Comparison Workflow: GitHub Actions triggers k6 benchmark against federation gateway at 50/100/200 RPS. Separate k6 script hits ProductMicroservice, InventoryMicroservice, and PricingMicroservice directly in parallel (baseline). Both output P50/P95/P99 latency JSON to S3. Analysis script computes latency delta and round-trip count delta, generates Markdown findings report committed to repo.
target_output.data_model: subgraph_schemas (ProductSubgraph SDL: type Product @key(fields: 'sku') with sku, brandId, title, description, imageUrls, variants; type ProductVariant @key(fields: 'sku variantId') with sku, variantId, size, color; type Brand with id, name, tier). federated_products (Apollo Router entity cache: Product entities keyed by sku, served from ProductMicroservice REST, Redis TTL 60s). inventory_entities (InventorySubgraph extension: type Product @key @external extends with inventoryLevel InventoryLevel type; type InventoryLevel with warehouseId, quantityAvailable; type ProductVariant @key @external extends with stockStatus StockStatus enum). pricing_variants (PricingSubgraph extension: type ProductVariant @key @external extends with basePrice, promotionalPrice; type Product @key @external extends with tierDiscount). supergraph_operations (supergraph.graphql SDL file produced by rover supergraph compose; committed to repo, regenerated on subgraph schema changes). resolver_traces (OpenTelemetry span attributes per resolver: graphql.field.name, subgraph.name, cache.hit, downstream.service, duration_ms, batch_size for DataLoader spans — exported to CloudWatch OTLP and stored 30 days).
target_output.api_design: Public GraphQL API (internal VPN only): POST /graphql on Apollo Router with Content-Type: application/json and x-internal-service-token: {token} header. Key queries: ProductDetailQuery(sku: String!): Product — returns product fields, variants with stockStatus, basePrice, promotionalPrice, and inventoryLevel. ProductListQuery(skus: [String!]!): [Product] — batch product fetch for catalog pages, exercises DataLoader batching under load. Introspection enabled for internal Apollo Sandbox exploration by prototype evaluators. Subgraph endpoints (not public, Router-to-subgraph only): POST /subgraph-graphql on each subgraph service, validated via x-internal-service-token forwarding from Router.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Apollo Router inline Rhai script plugin validates x-internal-service-token header value against INTERNAL_SERVICE_TOKEN environment variable on every inbound request; returns HTTP 401 with {error: 'unauthorized'} if header is absent or invalid. Header propagation config forwards the token to all subgraphs. Each subgraph Apollo Server 4 context function validates x-internal-service-token presence and throws AuthenticationError on missing token. ECS security groups: apollo-router task allows inbound 4000/tcp from VPN CIDR only; subgraph tasks allow inbound 4000/tcp from apollo-router task security group only. No subgraph has a public IP or load balancer. Token stored in AWS Secrets Manager; ECS task execution role grants secretsmanager:GetSecretValue.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: rover subgraph check on each subgraph SDL against Apollo managed registry (catches federation composition errors before deploy), Node.js 22 unit tests for resolver logic and DataLoader batch functions, Docker multi-stage build to ECR, ECS task definition update via aws-actions/amazon-ecs-deploy-task-definition. Single ECS environment: Fargate tasks in private subnet, internal ALB with listener on port 443 (ACM certificate for internal domain). Benchmark workflow: manual trigger via GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch, runs k6 federation benchmark and k6 direct baseline in parallel from the same ECS runner, uploads JSON output to S3 bucket benchmark-results/{timestamp}/, triggers analysis Lambda that generates Markdown findings report. supergraph.graphql regenerated and committed by CI when any subgraph schema changes (rover supergraph compose output committed to repo for schema history).
target_output.observability: Apollo Router telemetry.tracing.otlp configured to export all resolver spans to AWS CloudWatch OTLP receiver endpoint. Span attributes include: subgraph.name (product/inventory/pricing), graphql.field.path (e.g. Product.variants.stockStatus), cache.hit (bool), dataloader.batch_size (for InventorySubgraph), downstream.rest.url, duration_ms. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries for resolver latency breakdown by subgraph. Custom benchmark output metrics: P50/P95/P99 latency per RPS tier, round_trip_count_per_query (federation vs baseline), cache_hit_rate_per_subgraph, N+1_calls_eliminated_per_request. These metrics are the primary prototype output artifacts for the architectural decision.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $45 total for 4-week prototype: ECS Fargate 5 tasks at micro sizing ($30), ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.micro ($7), ALB ($4), CloudWatch logs and S3 ($4). Prototype intentionally minimal — no auto-scaling, no multi-AZ, no high availability. If prototype validates federation, production architecture cost estimate is included in the findings document as a forward-looking section.
target_output.phased_implementation: Week 1: ProductSubgraph with Product and ProductVariant types, rover supergraph compose with single subgraph, Apollo Router basic routing, ECS deployment, OTLP trace export validated. Week 2: InventorySubgraph with DataLoader batching, PricingSubgraph, full three-subgraph supergraph composition, Redis caching per subgraph, ECS service discovery for subgraph URLs. Week 3: Benchmark harness (k6 federation vs direct REST baseline), synthetic load runs at 50/100/200 RPS, resolver trace collection in CloudWatch, N+1 call count measurement with and without DataLoader. Week 4: Trace analysis, latency delta computation, findings document with recommendation for or against production federation migration, cost model for production supergraph gateway.
target_output.development_guidelines: Apollo Server 4 subgraphs must define @key directives matching the entity resolution strategy — mismatches cause rover subgraph check failures in CI. DataLoader instances must be created per request context, not module-level singletons, to prevent cross-request data leakage in the Node.js event loop. Redis cache keys must include the subgraph name prefix to prevent key collisions (e.g. product:catalog:{sku} vs inventory:stock:{sku}). Resolver functions must emit custom OpenTelemetry spans for cache_hit and downstream REST call duration to enable accurate resolver latency decomposition in findings. Benchmark k6 script must run from ECS to avoid VPN network latency inflating measured timings.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: downstream microservices have undocumented rate limits that the batch benchmark may trigger, skewing latency results. Mitigation: confirm rate limits with microservice owners before running 200 RPS tier; add per-subgraph request rate metric to benchmark output. Risk: Apollo Router Rust binary has different cold-start characteristics than Node.js subgraphs; first-request latency may be misleadingly high. Mitigation: benchmark script includes a 30-second warmup phase before recording measurements. Tradeoff: DataLoader batching reduces N+1 calls but adds a small batching wait window (10ms) to inventory resolver latency — the benchmark must separately measure batch-consolidated latency versus the N+1 penalty to show net benefit. Tradeoff: Redis resolver cache reduces downstream microservice load but introduces cache invalidation complexity; for prototype purposes, TTLs are deliberately short (15-60s) to minimize stale data risk without implementing invalidation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Apollo Federation 2 over GraphQL Mesh as the federation implementation — Federation 2 has better Apollo Router integration and the org may adopt Apollo Studio for production schema registry. DataLoader chosen over Apollo Router @batch directive for InventorySubgraph batching — DataLoader gives finer control over batch window and max batch size for the benchmark. Direct REST calls to real microservices confirmed over mock servers for benchmark validity — latency from mock servers would not represent production conditions. OTLP export to CloudWatch chosen over Jaeger for prototype simplicity — CloudWatch OTLP receiver is available natively in us-east-1 without additional infrastructure.

metadata.notes: GraphQL federation research prototype validating Apollo Federation 2 supergraph composition for multi-brand e-commerce storefront unification.

profile.domain: AI-Powered Product Tagging and Categorization for Online Thrift Marketplace

project_goal.value: Automatically assign taxonomy categories, condition grades, and searchable attributes to millions of seller-uploaded thrift items from photo and text inputs using ML inference
project_goal.rationale: Core value proposition: eliminate manual tagging bottleneck and improve search relevance for resale marketplace

target_users.value: Internal ML platform team, marketplace ops engineers, and human labeling moderators
target_users.rationale: No consumer-facing UI; internal system driving seller listing quality

project_class.rationale: Primary value delivered through ML inference pipelines and LLM-based attribute extraction, not CRUD application logic

capabilities.value: backend, data, ai_llm, analytics, batch_jobs, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Inference service, feature store, LLM integration, SageMaker training jobs, and downstream catalog integrations all required

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of item submissions, sub-3-second inference SLAs, weekly fine-tuning cycles, and multi-model fusion pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Internal system with IAM-controlled access; mispredictions degrade search quality but carry no financial or safety liability

data_sensitivity.rationale: Thrift item photos and descriptions are seller-provided product data, not PII or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Inference API consumed by marketplace ingestion pipeline, not exposed to sellers or buyers directly

access_model.value: IAM role-based access for ECS tasks and SageMaker jobs; moderators access labeling UI via internal SSO
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service calls within AWS VPC; no public user authentication required

feature_scope.value: Real-time tagging inference, confidence-scored taxonomy assignment, condition grading, human labeling queue, model versioning, weekly fine-tuning pipeline, prediction analytics dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for autonomous tagging with human-in-the-loop correction and continuous model improvement

mvp_scope.value: SageMaker vision model for category prediction, LLM attribute extractor, Postgres prediction store, labeling queue for low-confidence items
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum needed to replace manual tagging at scale with measurable accuracy baseline

security_baseline.value: IAM least-privilege for ECS and SageMaker roles, S3 bucket policies restricting image access, VPC private subnets for inference services, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager
security_baseline.rationale: Internal service security without public exposure

frontend_stack.value: None for inference service; lightweight React SPA served via CloudFront for internal labeling moderators
frontend_stack.rationale: No consumer frontend; moderator tooling is secondary

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for inference orchestration service on ECS Fargate; SageMaker for model hosting and training jobs
backend_stack.rationale: Python is the ML ecosystem standard; FastAPI handles async inference orchestration efficiently

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for prediction records and taxonomy tree; S3 for image assets and model artifacts
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for structured prediction metadata with S3 as cost-effective object store

hosting_target.value: ECS Fargate for inference API; SageMaker real-time endpoints for vision and LLM models; SageMaker Training Jobs for weekly fine-tuning
hosting_target.rationale: ECS for the orchestration layer; SageMaker for GPU-backed model serving with auto-scaling endpoints

privacy_retention_policy.value: Prediction records retained indefinitely for analytics; raw image assets deleted after 2 years; labeling audit logs retained 1 year
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal data governance policy; no regulatory mandate

future_scope.value: Multi-modal re-ranking for search, buyer-facing similar-item recommendations, cross-lingual attribute extraction for international expansion
future_scope.rationale: ML platform capabilities unlocking downstream marketplace features

constraints.value: Inference must complete within 3 seconds per item submission; fine-tuning budget capped at $8K/month on SageMaker GPU instances
constraints.rationale: 3s SLA tied to seller listing creation UX; GPU budget set by finance

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for inference service latency and error rates; SageMaker Model Monitor for prediction drift; custom dashboard for per-taxonomy accuracy trends
observability_baseline.rationale: Need to distinguish infrastructure latency regressions from model quality regressions

execution_preference.value: Iterative ML sprints: model experimentation in 2-week cycles, infrastructure changes delivered as parallel platform stories
execution_preference.rationale: ML iteration cadence decoupled from platform delivery cadence

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o for attribute extraction from item descriptions and OCR-inferred text; prompt templates versioned in S3 under prompts/ prefix; SageMaker-hosted Llama 3 as fallback on API failure
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o outperforms rule-based attribute parsers for unstructured thrift descriptions; S3-versioned prompts enable rollback without redeployment

project.goal: Auto-tag thrift marketplace items with taxonomy categories, condition grades, and searchable attributes from seller photos and text
project.target_users: ML platform team and marketplace ops engineers
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks; full feature set in 6 months

backend.framework: FastAPI async inference orchestration on ECS Fargate
backend.model_serving: SageMaker real-time endpoints for vision model and LLM attribute extractor

security.auth: IAM roles for service-to-service; internal SSO for labeling UI
security.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for API keys and model credentials

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for predictions and taxonomy tree
data.object_store: S3 for image assets and model artifacts
data.cache: Redis 7 for prediction deduplication and rate limiting

devops.ci: GitHub Actions building Docker images, running pytest, pushing to ECR
devops.environments: dev, staging, production with isolated SageMaker endpoints per env

reasoner_reviews.product: The tagging pipeline addresses a real operational bottleneck: manual category assignment cannot scale to millions of daily thrift listings. The confidence threshold routing to human labeling is the right safeguard to maintain catalog quality while the models warm up on production data. Analytics on per-taxonomy accuracy will be essential for the ops team to prioritize labeling effort where model performance is weakest.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Separating the FastAPI inference orchestrator from direct SageMaker endpoint calls is the right decomposition — it allows retry logic, multi-model fusion, and latency budgeting without coupling to SageMaker's invocation API. The vision model and LLM should be called concurrently via asyncio.gather to hit the 3-second SLA. Prediction deduplication in Redis prevents redundant SageMaker calls for identical item submissions within a short window.
reasoner_reviews.security: IAM least-privilege enforcement on ECS task roles is critical — the inference service should have read-only access to the image S3 bucket and write access only to the predictions table. SageMaker endpoint invocation permissions must be scoped to specific endpoint ARNs, not sagemaker:* wildcard. No secrets should be baked into Docker images or ECS task definition environment variables.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 3-second inference SLA is the dominant constraint and drives the async concurrent call pattern, Redis dedup cache, and SageMaker provisioned concurrency to avoid cold starts. Fine-tuning at $8K/month supports weekly ml.g5.4xlarge training runs of approximately 5 hours each.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Taxonomy tree versioning needs explicit design — if taxonomy nodes change between model training and inference time, category ID mismatches will silently corrupt the catalog. The fine-tuning pipeline must version training datasets and evaluation splits alongside model artifacts in S3 and record the taxonomy_version_id used at training time.

backend.service_design: FastAPI inference service with three async endpoints: POST /v1/tag (single item), POST /v1/tag-batch (up to 100 items), GET /health. Orchestrates concurrent calls to SageMaker vision endpoint and OpenAI GPT-4o attribute extractor via asyncio.gather. Merges predictions using confidence-weighted voting. Writes tagging_predictions and attribute_extractions records to Postgres.
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/tag with body {item_id, image_s3_key, description_text} returns {prediction_id, taxonomy_node_id, taxonomy_path, condition_grade, attributes, confidence_score, model_version_id, latency_ms}. All responses include model_version_id for audit trail. Internal-only, authenticated via AWS SigV4 request signing.
backend.background_jobs: Weekly SageMaker Training Job triggered by Lambda cron Sunday 02:00 UTC: pulls corrected labels from labeling_jobs table, merges with S3 training dataset, fine-tunes vision model checkpoint for 10 epochs on ml.g5.4xlarge, evaluates on held-out split, promotes to production endpoint if accuracy >= previous model - 0.005.
backend.failure_handling: SageMaker endpoint timeouts fall back to a keyword-based rule tagger that assigns top-level taxonomy category. Redis circuit breaker tracks endpoint failure rate and trips after 5 failures/minute, routing all traffic to fallback for 30 seconds before re-probing.

security.auth_design: ECS task role with scoped IAM policy: s3:GetObject on marketplace-images bucket, sagemaker:InvokeEndpoint scoped to named endpoint ARNs, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue for OpenAI key, rds-db:connect scoped to predictions DB instance.
security.secrets_management: OpenAI API key stored in AWS Secrets Manager and fetched at ECS task startup via boto3 SDK call — not stored in task definition environment block or Docker image layers.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All SageMaker endpoint invocations logged to CloudWatch with item_id, model_version, confidence_score, and latency_ms. Labeling moderator corrections written to labeling_jobs table with moderator_id, original_prediction_id, corrected_taxonomy_node_id, and corrected_at timestamp.

data.entities: item_submissions (id UUID PK, marketplace_item_id VARCHAR, image_s3_key TEXT, description_text TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM pending/tagged/failed). tagging_predictions (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, taxonomy_node_id INT FK, confidence_score FLOAT, model_version_id UUID FK, predicted_at TIMESTAMPTZ) PARTITIONED BY predicted_at monthly. condition_assessments (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, grade ENUM new/like_new/good/fair/poor, confidence_score FLOAT). attribute_extractions (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, attribute_key VARCHAR, attribute_value TEXT, source ENUM llm/vision, confidence_score FLOAT). taxonomy_nodes (id SERIAL PK, parent_id INT FK, name VARCHAR, slug VARCHAR UNIQUE, depth INT, active BOOL, taxonomy_version INT). labeling_jobs (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, original_prediction_id UUID FK, corrected_taxonomy_node_id INT FK, corrected_at TIMESTAMPTZ). model_versions (id UUID PK, model_type ENUM vision/llm_attribute, artifact_s3_path TEXT, eval_accuracy FLOAT, taxonomy_version_id INT, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, active BOOL).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS r7g.xlarge in private subnet. tagging_predictions partitioned by predicted_at month via pg_partman for partition pruning on analytics queries. S3 buckets: marketplace-images (image assets), model-artifacts (checkpoints and weights), training-datasets (labeled splits). Redis 7 ElastiCache r7g.large for item_id dedup with 10-minute TTL.
data.analytics_events: Prediction accuracy events emitted to Kinesis Firehose landing in S3 then queryable via Athena for per-taxonomy accuracy and drift analysis. Daily accuracy roll-up materialized view in Postgres for ops dashboard queries.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster in private subnets behind internal ALB. SageMaker vision endpoint in same AWS region with VPC endpoint to avoid public internet. PostgreSQL RDS in private subnet with no public accessibility. Redis ElastiCache in same VPC security group. All inter-service traffic stays within VPC.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR run pytest with 85% coverage gate, ruff lint, mypy type checks. On merge to main, build Python 3.12 Docker image, push to ECR, deploy ECS rolling update with 100% min healthy percentage. SageMaker fine-tune job triggered separately by Lambda cron.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with trace IDs propagated through FastAPI to SageMaker calls. Custom dashboards: p50/p95/p99 inference latency by item type, SageMaker endpoint error rate, Redis cache hit rate, labeling queue depth. SageMaker Model Monitor weekly distribution check on prediction confidence; PagerDuty alert if mean confidence drops more than 0.05 from 7-day baseline.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-scale ML inference pipeline for thrift marketplace item tagging. Key decisions: async concurrent calls to SageMaker vision model and GPT-4o LLM within the FastAPI orchestrator to meet the 3-second SLA; Redis dedup cache to avoid redundant SageMaker invocations for duplicate submissions; Postgres monthly partitioning on tagging_predictions for query performance at billions of rows; confidence threshold routing to human labeling queue as the model quality control mechanism with corrections feeding weekly fine-tuning.
target_output.title: Thrift Marketplace AI Tagging Pipeline Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale AI tagging system for a thrift marketplace that routes seller-uploaded items through a FastAPI inference orchestrator on ECS Fargate, calling a SageMaker vision model and GPT-4o attribute extractor concurrently to assign taxonomy categories, condition grades, and searchable attributes within 3 seconds. Low-confidence predictions enter a human labeling queue whose corrections feed weekly SageMaker fine-tuning runs. The system processes millions of daily item submissions with per-taxonomy accuracy analytics driving continuous model improvement.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven ML pipeline with a synchronous inference path and an asynchronous correction-and-retraining loop. The marketplace ingestion service POSTs item submissions to the FastAPI inference service on ECS Fargate. The service checks Redis for a recent duplicate prediction (10-min TTL), fetches the image S3 key, then concurrently invokes the SageMaker vision endpoint (PyTorch ResNet50 fine-tuned on thrift taxonomy) and the GPT-4o attribute extraction chain. Prediction results are confidence-weighted and merged before being written to PostgreSQL tagging_predictions. Items below confidence threshold 0.72 are placed in a labeling_jobs queue consumed by moderators via an internal React UI. Weekly Lambda cron triggers a SageMaker Training Job that pulls corrected labels, fine-tunes the vision checkpoint, and promotes to the production endpoint if accuracy improves.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, asyncio, boto3, openai SDK, psycopg3, redis-py. Model Serving: SageMaker real-time endpoints hosting PyTorch ResNet50 vision model; GPT-4o via OpenAI API with SageMaker-hosted Llama 3 fallback. Data: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS r7g.xlarge with pg_partman monthly partitioning, S3 for image assets and model artifacts, Redis 7 ElastiCache for dedup cache. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, SageMaker endpoints with provisioned concurrency, Lambda cron, Kinesis Firehose, Athena, Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Inference Orchestration: accepts {item_id, image_s3_key, description_text}, deduplicates via Redis, calls vision and LLM concurrently, merges confidence-weighted results, writes to Postgres. Taxonomy Assignment: maps predicted node IDs to versioned taxonomy tree with depth-aware path resolution. Condition Grading: vision model secondary classification head outputs grade (new/like_new/good/fair/poor). Attribute Extraction: GPT-4o extracts structured attributes (brand, material, size, color, era, style) using S3-versioned prompt templates. Human Labeling Queue: low-confidence items routed to moderator UI with original prediction displayed. Fine-Tuning Pipeline: weekly Lambda-triggered SageMaker Training Job pulls corrected labels, trains, evaluates, and promotes on accuracy gate. Analytics: per-taxonomy accuracy trends via Kinesis Firehose to Athena and Postgres materialized views.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI Inference Service (ECS Fargate, 2-8 tasks auto-scaling on SQS queue depth and CPU). SageMaker Vision Endpoint (ml.g5.2xlarge, provisioned concurrency 5 to eliminate cold starts). GPT-4o Attribute Extractor (OpenAI API with Llama 3 fallback on SageMaker). PostgreSQL 16 RDS r7g.xlarge in private subnet with monthly partitioned tagging_predictions. Redis 7 ElastiCache r7g.large for dedup cache. S3 buckets: marketplace-images, model-artifacts, training-datasets. Kinesis Firehose feeding S3 for Athena analytics. Lambda cron triggering weekly SageMaker Training Job. Internal Labeling UI (React SPA on CloudFront, internal ALB).
target_output.workflows: Item Tagging Workflow: Marketplace ingestion POSTs {item_id, image_s3_key, description_text} to POST /v1/tag. Inference service checks Redis for item_id (10-min TTL cache hit returns cached prediction). On cache miss, fetches S3 pre-signed URL, calls asyncio.gather([vision_endpoint.invoke(image), gpt4o_chain.run(description)]). Vision returns {taxonomy_node_id, condition_grade, confidence}; GPT-4o returns {attributes: {brand, material, size, color}, confidence}. Merger computes weighted confidence; if max_confidence < 0.72, item inserted into labeling_jobs and moderator notified via SQS. On high confidence, writes tagging_prediction and attribute_extractions rows, sets item_submission status to tagged, returns response within 3s SLA. Fine-Tuning Workflow: Lambda cron invokes SageMaker Training Job Sunday 02:00 UTC. Job queries labeling_jobs WHERE corrected_at > last_run_at, merges with S3 training-datasets/current/, shuffles and splits 80/20 train/eval, fine-tunes ResNet50 checkpoint 10 epochs on ml.g5.4xlarge. If eval_accuracy >= previous model accuracy - 0.005, artifact uploaded to S3 and SageMaker endpoint updated with blue/green traffic shift; model_versions record inserted.
target_output.data_model: item_submissions (id UUID PK, marketplace_item_id VARCHAR INDEX, image_s3_key TEXT, description_text TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM pending/tagged/failed). tagging_predictions (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, taxonomy_node_id INT FK, confidence_score FLOAT, model_version_id UUID FK, predicted_at TIMESTAMPTZ) PARTITIONED BY predicted_at monthly. condition_assessments (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, grade ENUM new/like_new/good/fair/poor, confidence_score FLOAT). attribute_extractions (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, attribute_key VARCHAR, attribute_value TEXT, source ENUM llm/vision, confidence_score FLOAT). taxonomy_nodes (id SERIAL PK, parent_id INT FK NULLABLE, name VARCHAR, slug VARCHAR UNIQUE, depth INT, active BOOL, taxonomy_version INT). labeling_jobs (id UUID PK, item_submission_id UUID FK, original_prediction_id UUID FK, assigned_moderator_id VARCHAR, corrected_taxonomy_node_id INT FK, corrected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, reason TEXT). model_versions (id UUID PK, model_type ENUM vision/llm_attribute, artifact_s3_path TEXT, eval_accuracy FLOAT, taxonomy_version_id INT, promoted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, active BOOL).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/tag {item_id, image_s3_key, description_text} -> {prediction_id, taxonomy_node_id, taxonomy_path[], condition_grade, attributes{}, confidence_score, model_version_id, latency_ms}. POST /v1/tag-batch [{...}] up to 100 items -> [{...}] processed via asyncio.gather. GET /v1/taxonomy returns active taxonomy tree as nested JSON (cached 5 min in process). GET /v1/labels/queue?moderator_id=X&limit=20 returns pending labeling_jobs with original predictions displayed. POST /v1/labels/{job_id}/correct {corrected_taxonomy_node_id, reason} submits moderator correction. GET /v1/analytics/accuracy?taxonomy_node_id=X&days=30 returns accuracy trend. All endpoints internal-only authenticated via AWS SigV4 request signing.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM least-privilege: ECS task role permits s3:GetObject on marketplace-images bucket, sagemaker:InvokeEndpoint scoped to named endpoint ARNs (not wildcard), secretsmanager:GetSecretValue for OpenAI key, rds-db:connect scoped to predictions DB. OpenAI API key stored in Secrets Manager and fetched at task startup via SDK — not in task definition environment block. S3 bucket policies block public access; image URLs are pre-signed with 5-minute expiry. SageMaker endpoints deployed in VPC with no public endpoint. CloudTrail logs all SageMaker endpoint updates and S3 model artifact writes for audit. No PHI or PII handled — seller item content only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on PR: pytest 85% coverage gate, ruff lint, mypy type checks. On merge to main: build Python 3.12 Docker, push ECR, trigger ECS rolling deployment (100% min healthy, 30s health check grace). ECS service auto-scales 2-8 tasks on CPU 60% target. SageMaker vision endpoint: ml.g5.2xlarge with provisioned concurrency 5 to eliminate cold-start spikes. Environments: dev (single task, dev SageMaker endpoints), staging (2 tasks, staging endpoints), production (auto-scale 2-8, production provisioned endpoints). SageMaker fine-tuning: Lambda cron Sunday 02:00 UTC; on eval pass, endpoint update via blue/green traffic shift over 15 minutes. Alembic migrations run as ECS one-off task before each deployment.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces span the full inference path: FastAPI handler -> Redis lookup -> S3 fetch -> SageMaker vision invoke -> GPT-4o call -> Postgres write. Custom Datadog metrics: inference_latency_ms p50/p95/p99 by item_type, sagemaker_endpoint_errors_per_min, labeling_queue_depth, redis_cache_hit_rate, model_accuracy_by_taxonomy_node. SageMaker Model Monitor runs daily distribution check on tagging_predictions confidence scores; PagerDuty alert if mean confidence drops >0.05 from 7-day baseline. Additional alerts: p99 inference > 2.5s, SageMaker error rate > 2%, labeling queue > 5000 unprocessed items.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: SageMaker inference ml.g5.2xlarge provisioned concurrency 5: ~$2,800/month. Weekly Training Job ml.g5.4xlarge ~5 hours: ~$400/month. ECS Fargate 2-8 tasks: ~$300/month. RDS r7g.xlarge: ~$350/month. ElastiCache r7g.large: ~$200/month. Total ~$4,050/month at baseline volume. Redis dedup cache reduces redundant SageMaker calls by ~30% for duplicate listing attempts. Auto-scaling handles 3x traffic spikes; beyond that, SageMaker endpoint scales horizontally within 3 minutes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): SageMaker vision endpoint for top-50 taxonomy categories, GPT-4o attribute extractor for brand/size/color, FastAPI /v1/tag endpoint, Postgres schema for item_submissions/tagging_predictions/attribute_extractions, Redis dedup cache, confidence routing to labeling_jobs queue, Datadog latency dashboard. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Full 500-node taxonomy tree, condition grading secondary head, labeling moderator React UI, SageMaker fine-tuning pipeline with weekly Lambda trigger, model_versions registry, taxonomy versioning on predictions. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Analytics dashboard with per-taxonomy accuracy, Kinesis Firehose to Athena pipeline, bulk re-tagging batch endpoint for legacy catalog, SageMaker Model Monitor drift alerting.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy type checking enforced in CI. ruff for linting replacing flake8 and isort. pytest with 85% coverage gate; httpx AsyncClient for FastAPI endpoint tests. SageMaker endpoint calls wrapped in async context with explicit 2.2-second timeout (leaves budget for merge and DB write within 3s SLA). Prompt templates for GPT-4o versioned in S3 under prompts/{version}/ prefix — fetched at FastAPI startup, cached in-process, never per-request. Taxonomy tree cached in-process with 5-minute TTL. ADRs required for model architecture changes and taxonomy schema updates.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o API rate limits or latency spikes under burst traffic. Mitigation: exponential backoff retry wrapper with SageMaker-hosted Llama 3 fallback triggered after 3 consecutive timeouts. Risk: taxonomy node version mismatch between training and inference causing silent category ID corruption. Mitigation: model_versions table records taxonomy_version_id used at training; inference service validates predicted node against same taxonomy version. Tradeoff: provisioned SageMaker concurrency (5 units, ~$1,400/month overhead) eliminates cold-start spikes that would breach 3-second SLA under burst traffic. Tradeoff: monthly Postgres partitioning adds operational complexity but enables partition pruning on analytics queries across billions of prediction rows.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected SageMaker real-time endpoints over self-managed Triton Inference Server for managed scaling and AWS IAM integration. Confirmed OpenAI GPT-4o over Bedrock Claude for attribute extraction based on benchmark accuracy on thrift description test set. Chose Postgres monthly partitioning over TimescaleDB to avoid extension dependency. Redis dedup TTL set to 10 minutes based on median duplicate submission gap analysis from production logs.

metadata.notes: High-scale ML tagging pipeline for thrift marketplace with vision+LLM fusion inference and human-in-the-loop fine-tuning.

profile.domain: High-Traffic Static Marketing Site for Direct-to-Consumer Sneaker Brand

project_goal.value: Deliver a high-traffic static marketing and product discovery site with curated drop pages, shoppable lookbooks, and SEO-optimized category landings serving 5M+ monthly visitors for a DTC sneaker brand
project_goal.rationale: Primary marketing channel for limited-edition drop launches and organic search acquisition

target_users.value: Sneaker enthusiasts, resellers, and fashion-forward consumers discovering and tracking upcoming drops
target_users.rationale: Core audience identified in brand discovery research

project_class.rationale: Pre-rendered Astro output served from S3/CloudFront; no server-side runtime or database on the hosting layer

capabilities.value: frontend, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Static delivery plus client-side analytics; no backend API or auth in scope

complexity_level.rationale: Programmatic SEO at scale (thousands of pages), headless CMS integration at build time, and React island interactivity add meaningful complexity beyond a simple brochure site

risk_level.rationale: No user data collected; no authentication; no payment processing on this domain

data_sensitivity.rationale: Static marketing content only; no PII, cookies beyond analytics consent, or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Public-facing marketing site with no authentication

access_model.value: Fully public read access; no authentication on the site; CMS content editing restricted to authenticated brand team via CMS admin
access_model.rationale: Marketing site intended for maximum reach; content editing is CMS-side only

feature_scope.value: Drop pages for limited-edition launches, shoppable lookbooks with product card overlays, programmatic SEO category landings, countdown timers for upcoming drops, GDPR-compliant cookie consent banner, Datadog RUM analytics
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for drop marketing and search acquisition

mvp_scope.value: Drop pages, category landings with programmatic SEO, CMS-driven content pipeline, CloudFront delivery with build-triggered invalidation
mvp_scope.rationale: Core discovery and drop marketing features needed for first launch

security_baseline.value: CloudFront HTTPS-only with HSTS header; S3 bucket policy blocks all public GetObject access (only CloudFront OAC allowed); security headers via CloudFront response headers policy (CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options)
security_baseline.rationale: Static site security hardening: no origin exposure, no clickjacking, no MIME sniffing

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with React islands for interactive components (countdown timers, lookbook overlays) and TypeScript throughout
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro partial hydration minimizes JavaScript sent to browser; React islands for interactive components only

backend_stack.value: None; build-time data fetching from headless CMS (Contentful) and product taxonomy feed via Astro content collections
backend_stack.rationale: Static site; all data resolved at build time; no server-side runtime on hosting layer

data_platform.value: Contentful headless CMS as content source; static JSON product taxonomy feed for programmatic SEO; no database on hosting infrastructure
data_platform.rationale: All content is authored in Contentful and consumed at build time via Content Delivery API

hosting_target.value: S3 static hosting origin with CloudFront CDN; Route53 for DNS; ACM for TLS certificates
hosting_target.rationale: Standard AWS static site stack; CloudFront global edge delivery for 5M+ monthly visitors

privacy_retention_policy.value: No PII collected; anonymous analytics events (page views, drop page interactions) retained 13 months in Datadog RUM; GDPR cookie consent required for EU traffic
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR e-privacy directive requires consent for analytics cookies; 13-month retention aligns with annual trend analysis

future_scope.value: Personalized drop notification sign-up (requires adding a backend API), AR sneaker try-on feature via WebXR, localized international versions via Astro i18n
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch features requiring incremental architecture additions

constraints.value: Build time must complete within 8 minutes for the full programmatic SEO page set; no server-side runtime on CloudFront or S3; GDPR compliance required for EU visitors
constraints.rationale: 8-minute build gate keeps CMS publish-to-live under 10 minutes; static-only constraint is a hard architectural boundary

observability_baseline.value: Datadog RUM for Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID) per page type; CloudFront access logs to S3 for traffic analysis; build time tracked in GitHub Actions
observability_baseline.rationale: CWV directly impact SEO rankings; build time gate enforces developer productivity constraint

execution_preference.value: Content-first delivery: drop pages and CMS pipeline first, then programmatic SEO, then interactive React islands
execution_preference.rationale: Drop page delivery is time-critical for marketing calendar

project.goal: Static marketing site for sneaker drop launches and SEO-driven discovery
project.target_users: Sneaker enthusiasts and resellers
project.timeline: Drop page MVP in 4 weeks; full site in 8 weeks

frontend.interactivity: React islands for countdown timers and lookbook overlays only

security.headers: CSP, X-Frame-Options via CloudFront response headers policy

data.cms: Contentful Content Delivery API at build time
data.taxonomy_feed: Static JSON product feed for programmatic SEO page generation

devops.build: Astro build with Contentful API fetch

constraints.static_only: No server-side runtime on hosting layer
constraints.gdpr: Cookie consent banner for EU visitors

reasoner_reviews.product: The drop page and programmatic SEO strategy is well-aligned with the sneaker brand acquisition model: direct search traffic for category landings (air-jordan-1-retro, nike-dunk-low-panda) and referral traffic from drop countdown pages shared on social. The GDPR consent banner placement is important — a poorly designed banner will tank Core Web Vitals CLS scores on first load for EU visitors.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro 4 partial hydration is the right architecture for this workload: the vast majority of pages are static HTML with zero JavaScript, and only countdown timers and lookbook overlays need React island hydration. Programmatic SEO pages generated from the product taxonomy feed need careful build-time pagination to stay within the 8-minute build constraint — batching Contentful API calls with concurrency limits is essential.
reasoner_reviews.security: S3 origin must be CloudFront OAC only — no public GetObject access. Security headers policy on CloudFront should include a strict Content Security Policy allowing only Datadog RUM and Contentful image CDN origins. GDPR consent must be implemented before Datadog RUM script is loaded for EU visitors — consent check must happen client-side before analytics initialization.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 8-minute build constraint with thousands of programmatic SEO pages requires parallel page generation. Astro 4 concurrent builds and Contentful Content Delivery API pagination with Promise.all batching should be profiled early. CloudFront cache invalidation for drop page updates should use path-specific invalidations (/drops/*) not wildcard (/*) to avoid invalidating the entire CDN on every CMS publish.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with src/pages/drops/[slug].astro for drop pages, src/pages/category/[slug].astro for programmatic SEO landings, src/pages/lookbooks/[slug].astro for editorial content. React islands: CountdownTimer.tsx (hydrate:load for active drops), LookbookOverlay.tsx (hydrate:visible for lazy interaction), ConsentBanner.tsx (hydrate:load for immediate GDPR compliance). Tailwind CSS 3 for all styling.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Drop page: hero image, colorway carousel, countdown timer React island, 'Notify me' CTA (links to third-party email platform), related drops. Category landing: H1 with keyword-rich title from taxonomy, product card grid generated from taxonomy feed, canonical URL, structured data (ItemList schema). Lookbook: editorial imagery, product card overlays rendered as islands on scroll entry.
frontend.performance_notes: Target LCP < 1.5s on drop pages: hero image served from CloudFront with WebP/AVIF format negotiation, preload link for above-fold image in Astro head. CLS < 0.05: all images have explicit width/height set at build time from Contentful asset metadata. INP < 100ms: React islands are isolated to interactive components only; no global React context trees.
frontend.accessibility_notes: Drop countdown timers use aria-live='polite' for screen reader announcements. All product card images include alt text pulled from Contentful alt field. GDPR banner implements focus trap when open; Escape key dismisses.

security.auth_design: No authentication on public site. CMS editing secured via Contentful dashboard with SSO enforced by brand team. S3 bucket policy: Allow cloudfront.amazonaws.com via OAC condition only; explicit Deny * for all other principals.
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF WebACL with AWS Managed Rules (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet) attached to distribution. Rate limit rule 1000 req/10s per IP for bot mitigation during drop launches.
security.privacy_controls: Datadog RUM script loaded only after GDPR consent cookie 'analytics_consent=true' is set. Consent banner sets consent cookie with 365-day expiry. EU traffic detection via CloudFront geo-restriction header forwarded to client-side consent check.

data.entities: drop_pages (Contentful entry: drop_name, slug, launch_date, hero_image, colorways[], description, related_drops[]). category_landings (generated from product_taxonomy.json: category_slug, h1_title, meta_description, canonical_url, product_cards[]). lookbook_collections (Contentful entry: title, slug, editorial_images[], tagged_products[]). product_cards (taxonomy feed: sku, name, colorway, image_url, category_slug, retail_price). seo_metadata (per-page: title, description, og:image, canonical, structured_data_json).
data.analytics_events: Datadog RUM custom actions: drop_page_view {drop_slug, days_until_launch}, countdown_timer_impression, lookbook_product_card_click {product_sku}, category_landing_view {category_slug, page_position}. Core Web Vitals captured automatically by Datadog RUM SDK.

devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (static-site-prod) with OAC-only policy, no static website hosting feature. CloudFront distribution with OAC, HTTPS-only, HSTS, response headers policy (CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy). Route53 A/AAAA alias to CloudFront distribution. ACM TLS certificate in us-east-1 (required for CloudFront).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on push to main: checkout, bun install, fetch Contentful entries (CONTENTFUL_ACCESS_TOKEN from GitHub Secrets), run astro build, sync output to S3 with aws s3 sync --delete, run CloudFront invalidation for /drops/* and /lookbooks/* paths (not wildcard). Contentful webhook triggers GitHub Actions dispatch on content publish for CMS-initiated builds.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog RUM installed in Astro head as inline script (after consent check). CloudFront access logs streamed to S3 cf-logs/ for Athena traffic analysis. Build time captured via GitHub Actions job duration metric. CloudFront cache hit rate dashboard in Datadog from access logs.

ISS-001.title: Wildcard CloudFront invalidation on every CMS publish
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified CloudFront.create_invalidation with paths=['/*'] on every Astro build triggered by Contentful webhook. Wildcard invalidation costs $0.005 per path after the first 1000/month and purges the entire CDN cache including long-TTL category landings that are rarely updated, degrading cache hit rate and increasing origin load for 30-60 minutes after each publish.

history.note: Auditor flagged wildcard invalidation cost and cache efficiency impact

focus_issues.title: Wildcard CloudFront invalidation on every CMS publish
focus_issues.detail: Must scope invalidation to the specific content type paths updated (e.g., /drops/* for drop page edits, /lookbooks/* for lookbook edits) rather than wildcard.

latest_recommendations: Replace wildcard CloudFront invalidation with content-type-scoped paths: Contentful webhook payload includes content_type field; map content_type to invalidation path prefix (/drops/*, /lookbooks/*, /category/* as appropriate)
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: DTC Sneaker Brand Static Site Architecture v1

previous_audits.summary: Strong static site architecture with correct Astro partial hydration strategy and comprehensive SEO approach. One medium issue around CloudFront invalidation efficiency needs resolution before production.
strengths: Correct Astro island hydration strategy limiting JavaScript to interactive components only
Programmatic SEO page generation approach is well-specified
S3 OAC security model correctly configured
concerns: Wildcard CloudFront invalidation on every content publish is wasteful and degrades cache hit rate
recommendations: Use Contentful webhook content_type field to scope CloudFront invalidation to affected path prefixes only; wildcard invalidation should only be used for full site rebuilds

issue_updates.title: Wildcard CloudFront invalidation on every CMS publish
issue_updates.detail: CI/CD plan uses /* invalidation path without considering content-type-scoped paths

previous_plan.title: DTC Sneaker Brand Static Site Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Astro 4 static site with React islands for drop countdown timers and lookbooks, served from S3 via CloudFront with HTTPS and security headers. Programmatic SEO category landings generated from product taxonomy feed at build time.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions builds Astro on CMS publish webhook; syncs to S3 and runs CloudFront.create_invalidation(['/*']).
previous_plan.observability: Datadog RUM for Core Web Vitals. CloudFront access logs to S3.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing ISS-001: replace wildcard CloudFront invalidation with content-type-aware path scoping. The Contentful webhook payload includes the content_type field, which maps directly to path prefixes: drop -> /drops/slug, lookbook -> /lookbooks/slug, category -> /category/*. This is a focused change to the CI/CD workflow with high plan quality target (strong). All other sections from round 1 are already well-specified and should be preserved and enriched.

fix_report.action_taken: Updated GitHub Actions workflow to parse Contentful webhook payload content_type and sys.id fields to construct scoped CloudFront invalidation paths: content_type=drop -> /drops/{slug}, content_type=lookbook -> /lookbooks/{slug}, content_type=category_taxonomy -> /category/*, content_type=product_card -> /category/* and /lookbooks/*. Full-site wildcard /* used only for full rebuild events (e.g., Astro config changes). Added CloudFront invalidation path mapping table to deployment_and_operations section.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ISS-001 resolved: CloudFront cache hit rate maintained above 80% for static category landings during incremental drop page publishes; invalidation cost reduced by 90% for typical single-drop publish events.

target_output.title: DTC Sneaker Brand Static Site Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A high-traffic static marketing site for a DTC sneaker brand built with Astro 4 and React islands, served from S3 via CloudFront CDN to 5M+ monthly visitors. Drop pages feature countdown timer islands and colorway carousels; programmatic SEO generates thousands of category landings from a product taxonomy feed at build time. This revision resolves content-type-scoped CloudFront invalidation for surgical cache purging on CMS publishes, maintaining high cache hit rates while keeping publish-to-live under 10 minutes.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Astro 4 build pipeline with zero server-side runtime on the hosting layer. Content editors publish drop pages and lookbooks in Contentful; a webhook triggers GitHub Actions to run the Astro build, which fetches Contentful entries and the product taxonomy JSON feed at build time, generating pre-rendered HTML for every drop page, lookbook, and category landing. Output is synced to S3 via aws s3 sync --delete, and CloudFront paths are invalidated based on the content type that triggered the build — scoped to the specific path prefix rather than wildcard. The CloudFront distribution serves global traffic via AWS edge locations with HTTPS-only, HSTS, and a security headers policy (CSP scoped to Datadog RUM and Contentful image CDN origins). React islands on drop pages (CountdownTimer) and lookbooks (LookbookOverlay) hydrate client-side only, keeping JavaScript payload minimal for the 90% of pages that are fully static.
target_output.technology_stack: Build: Astro 4, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 3, React 18 (islands only), Bun as build runner. Content: Contentful headless CMS (Content Delivery API), product taxonomy static JSON feed. CDN: CloudFront with OAC, response headers policy, WAF WebACL. Origin: S3 bucket with OAC-only policy. DNS/TLS: Route53 alias, ACM certificate in us-east-1. Analytics: Datadog RUM with GDPR consent gate. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Contentful webhook dispatch. Observability: Datadog RUM Core Web Vitals, CloudFront access logs to S3 for Athena.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Drop Pages: hero image with AVIF/WebP via CloudFront format negotiation, colorway carousel, CountdownTimer React island (hydrate:load), 'Notify me' CTA linking to email platform, structured data (Product schema). Programmatic SEO Category Landings: H1 from taxonomy label, product card grid, canonical URL, ItemList structured data, meta description from taxonomy feed. Shoppable Lookbooks: editorial photography, LookbookOverlay React island (hydrate:visible) showing product cards on hover/tap. GDPR Consent Banner: ConsentBanner React island (hydrate:load), consent cookie gates Datadog RUM initialization, EU traffic detected via CloudFront CloudFront-Viewer-Country header forwarded to client. Build Pipeline: Contentful webhook triggers GitHub Actions dispatch, Astro fetches CMS and taxonomy data, generates all pages, syncs to S3, invalidates scoped CloudFront paths.
target_output.system_components: S3 bucket (sneaker-site-prod) with static files, OAC-only bucket policy, versioning enabled for rollback. CloudFront distribution: OAC origin, HTTPS redirect, HSTS, response headers policy (Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin), WAF WebACL with AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet and 1000 req/10s rate limit per IP. Astro 4 build (GitHub Actions runner): fetches Contentful entries via paginated Content Delivery API, fetches product_taxonomy.json feed, runs astro build. Contentful webhook delivers push events to GitHub Actions repository_dispatch endpoint. Datadog RUM SDK in Astro head template, loaded conditionally on consent cookie presence.
target_output.workflows: Drop Page Publish Workflow: Brand editor creates or updates a drop entry in Contentful with drop_name, launch_date, hero_image, colorways, slug. Contentful fires webhook POST to GitHub Actions repository_dispatch with {content_type: 'drop', sys: {id: 'entry-id'}, fields: {slug: 'air-jordan-1-chicago-2026'}}. GitHub Actions job fetches Contentful entry, resolves slug, runs full Astro build (all pages regenerated). aws s3 sync --delete uploads changed files. CloudFront invalidation created with paths=['/drops/air-jordan-1-chicago-2026']. New drop page live within 8-minute build window. Programmatic SEO Build Workflow: Product manager uploads updated product_taxonomy.json to a designated S3 source bucket. S3 event notification triggers GitHub Actions workflow. Astro build fetches taxonomy feed and generates one /category/[slug] page per leaf node in the taxonomy tree (e.g., /category/nike-dunk-low-panda, /category/adidas-samba-og-white). CloudFront invalidation paths=['/category/*'] to refresh all category landings. Build time monitored against 8-minute gate in GitHub Actions job summary.
target_output.data_model: drop_pages (Contentful content type: drop_name STRING, slug STRING UNIQUE, launch_date ISO8601, hero_image ContentfulAsset{url, width, height, alt}, colorways ContentfulAsset[], description RichText, related_drops ContentfulReference[], structured_data_template STRING). lookbook_collections (Contentful: title STRING, slug STRING, editorial_images ContentfulAsset[], tagged_products [{sku STRING, product_name STRING, category_slug STRING, image_url STRING}]). category_landings (taxonomy feed: category_slug STRING, h1_title STRING, meta_description STRING, breadcrumb_path STRING[], product_cards [{sku, name, colorway, image_url, retail_price}]). seo_metadata (per-page at build time: og_title, og_description, og_image_url, canonical_url, structured_data_json). editorial_blocks (Contentful: type ENUM hero/quote/product-grid/video, content RichText|ContentfulAsset).
target_output.api_design: No runtime API. Build-time data sources: Contentful Content Delivery API GET /spaces/{space_id}/entries?content_type=drop&limit=200 paginated with cursor for full drop catalog. GET /spaces/{space_id}/entries?content_type=lookbook fetches all lookbooks. S3 GetObject on product_taxonomy.json for category landings generation. Client-side: Datadog RUM SDK initialization (no custom API calls). CountdownTimer React island receives launch_date as Astro prop serialized at build time; purely client-side timer calculation with no API fetch. ConsentBanner reads/writes document.cookie 'analytics_consent' — no server communication.
target_output.security_and_compliance: S3 bucket policy: Effect Deny, Principal *, Action s3:GetObject, Condition without cloudfront.amazonaws.com OAC — no public origin exposure. CloudFront response headers policy enforces: Content-Security-Policy (default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://rum.browser-intake-datadoghq.com; img-src 'self' https://images.ctfassets.net data:; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'), X-Frame-Options DENY, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, Strict-Transport-Security max-age=31536000 includeSubDomains. WAF WebACL with AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet for XSS and SQLi rule groups. GDPR: Datadog RUM script blocked by ConsentBanner until analytics_consent cookie set; consent banner shown to EU visitors based on CloudFront-Viewer-Country header.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow: on repository_dispatch (Contentful webhook) or push to main. Steps: checkout, bun install, fetch Contentful ACCESS_TOKEN from GitHub Actions Secrets, run astro build (parallel page generation, CONCURRENCY=8 for Contentful API calls to stay under rate limits), aws s3 sync dist/ s3://sneaker-site-prod/ --delete --cache-control 'public,max-age=31536000,immutable' for hashed assets and 'public,max-age=300' for HTML. CloudFront invalidation path mapping: content_type=drop -> [/drops/{slug}], content_type=lookbook -> [/lookbooks/{slug}], content_type=category_taxonomy -> [/category/*], full rebuild (push to main) -> [/*]. Invalidation via aws cloudfront create-invalidation. Build time gate: GitHub Actions step timeout 8 minutes; exceeding fails the workflow and sends Slack alert. Rollback: aws s3 sync from S3 versioned backup of previous dist/ plus CloudFront invalidation.
target_output.observability: Datadog RUM tracks Core Web Vitals per page type: LCP target < 1.5s (drop pages with hero image preload), CLS target < 0.05 (all images with explicit dimensions), INP target < 100ms (React island interactions). Custom RUM actions: drop_page_view {drop_slug, days_until_launch}, lookbook_product_overlay_click {product_sku}, category_landing_view {category_slug}. CloudFront access logs written to S3 cf-logs/ with daily Athena partition projection for traffic analysis by path_pattern. Build duration tracked as GitHub Actions job metric — alert if build exceeds 7 minutes (1-minute warning before gate). CloudFront cache hit rate monitored via access logs; alert if hit rate drops below 75% for /category/* paths (indicates unwanted wildcard invalidation).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: S3 storage for static assets: ~$5/month for ~50GB of built output. CloudFront data transfer: ~$80/month at 5M monthly visitors with ~400KB average page weight and 80% cache hit rate. CloudFront invalidation: < $1/month with scoped path invalidation (average 2 paths per publish vs 1 wildcard). GitHub Actions: free tier sufficient for ~30 builds/month. Total: ~$90/month. Scaling: CloudFront handles traffic spikes for drop launches (historical 50x normal traffic in first 5 minutes) without configuration changes — fully static origin means no compute scaling required.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Weeks 1-3): Drop page template with CountdownTimer React island, Contentful content model and webhook, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, S3/CloudFront infrastructure, security headers policy. Phase 2 (Weeks 3-5): Programmatic SEO category landings from taxonomy feed, structured data (Product and ItemList schemas), sitemap.xml and robots.txt generation. Phase 3 (Weeks 5-7): Shoppable lookbook pages with LookbookOverlay React island, GDPR consent banner with Datadog RUM consent gate, EU country detection via CloudFront header. Phase 4 (Week 8): Performance audit against CWV targets, Astro build time profiling and concurrency tuning, full launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro islands rule: use hydrate:load only for components needed on first interaction (CountdownTimer, ConsentBanner); use hydrate:visible for below-fold interactive elements (LookbookOverlay). No global React context trees — each island is fully isolated. All images must have explicit width and height attributes (fetched from Contentful asset metadata at build time) to prevent CLS. Contentful API calls in Astro data fetching must use Promise.all with concurrency limit of 8 (CONCURRENCY env var) to respect API rate limits without serializing requests. Build time profiled on every PR with astro --verbose; flag if any content type fetch exceeds 30 seconds.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Contentful API rate limit hit during large taxonomy rebuilds generating 5000+ category pages. Mitigation: batch Contentful API calls with Promise.all and CONCURRENCY=8; cache taxonomy feed in S3 between builds and only re-fetch on explicit taxonomy update events. Risk: Drop launch traffic spike (50x normal) briefly exceeds CloudFront WAF rate limit of 1000 req/10s per IP for users refreshing aggressively. Mitigation: rate limit is per-IP; legitimate traffic distributed across millions of IPs is unaffected; increase threshold to 2000 req/10s if bot behavior analysis shows false positives on launch days. Tradeoff: full static pre-render means inventory availability is not real-time on the marketing site — 'Notify me' CTA links to a separate email platform rather than displaying live stock. Accepted for this static-only architecture.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Contentful Content Delivery API over Sanity for better React island ecosystem support and existing brand team familiarity. Confirmed CloudFront content-type-scoped invalidation (this revision) over Netlify instant cache purge — staying on AWS stack. Confirmed Datadog RUM over GA4 for privacy-friendly analytics with server-side event sampling option.

metadata.notes: DTC sneaker brand static site revision; scoped CloudFront invalidation fix; strong Astro island and programmatic SEO detail.

profile.domain: WooCommerce to Shopify Migration CLI Tool

project_goal.value: A CLI migration tool for e-commerce operators to transfer product, customer, and order data from WooCommerce to Shopify via the Shopify REST Admin API with resume-on-failure and configurable field mapping
project_goal.rationale: Manual migration from WooCommerce to Shopify is error-prone and non-resumable; operators need an auditable, idempotent CLI

target_users.value: E-commerce operators and agencies managing WooCommerce to Shopify migrations
target_users.rationale: Technical operators who run CLI commands and configure field mapping YAML files

project_class.rationale: Compiled Go binary with subcommands, flags, and local SQLite state — no browser UI or hosted platform

capabilities.value: backend, data, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Go CLI binary, SQLite migration state, WooCommerce and Shopify REST API integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Stateful resume-on-failure logic, field mapping DSL, idempotent API upserts, and multi-entity migration ordering

risk_level.rationale: Handles customer personal data (names, emails, addresses) during migration; API key exposure risk if credentials mishandled

data_sensitivity.rationale: Customer records include PII: name, email, shipping address, order history

external_exposure.rationale: CLI communicates with WooCommerce and Shopify APIs using API keys stored in local config; no public exposure

access_model.value: API key configuration stored in .woo-to-shopify.yaml in user home directory; WooCommerce consumer key/secret and Shopify Admin API access token
access_model.rationale: Standard credential configuration pattern for CLI tools; operator responsible for file system permissions

feature_scope.value: Product migration with variant and image support, customer record migration with order history, historical order replay, field mapping configuration file, resume-on-failure via SQLite checkpoints, structured log output, dry-run mode
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration feature set for typical WooCommerce store

mvp_scope.value: Product migration with batch processing and resume-on-failure, customer migration with GDPR field handling, structured log file output
mvp_scope.rationale: Products and customers are the minimum viable migration scope

security_baseline.value: API credentials stored in config file with 600 file permissions; credentials never logged to stdout or log files; config file read at startup and held in process memory only
security_baseline.rationale: Credential hygiene for a local CLI tool handling PII and API keys

frontend_stack.value: None; CLI binary only — no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: cli_tool project class with no frontend component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 compiled to a single static binary; cobra for CLI command tree; go-sqlite3 for migration state; resty for HTTP client
backend_stack.rationale: Go produces a single static binary for easy distribution; cobra is the standard Go CLI framework

data_platform.value: SQLite database (migration.db) in working directory for migration state tracking; no external database required
data_platform.rationale: Embedded SQLite is appropriate for a local CLI tool with no server infrastructure

hosting_target.value: Local binary distribution via GitHub Releases and Homebrew tap; runs on operator workstation or CI server
hosting_target.rationale: CLI tools are distributed as binaries, not hosted services

privacy_retention_policy.value: Customer PII in SQLite migration.db should be purged after successful migration completion; operator must run `migrate cleanup` to delete local PII; GDPR right-to-erasure: tool must not persist email addresses in log files
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR obligations apply to customer data processed during migration; local SQLite store is a temporary processing record

future_scope.value: BigCommerce source support, Shopify GraphQL Admin API migration path, GUI wrapper for non-technical operators
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP extensions for broader migration source support

constraints.value: Binary must cross-compile to Linux/macOS/Windows amd64 and arm64 from a single Go codebase; no external runtime dependencies; Shopify API rate limit 2 req/s for REST Admin API
constraints.rationale: Operator environments vary; rate limiting compliance required to avoid API ban

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON log output to migration.log with timestamp, level, entity_type, entity_id, and status fields; progress bars on stdout during migration runs; final summary report to stdout
observability_baseline.rationale: Operators need to audit migration progress and troubleshoot failures from log files

execution_preference.value: Ship as single statically-linked Go binary; semantic versioning with GitHub Releases; changelog maintained per release
execution_preference.rationale: Binary distribution simplicity is a core operator UX requirement

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 28 data processor obligations apply: customer PII processed during migration must be minimized, not logged, and deleted after migration completes via explicit cleanup command
compliance_context.rationale: GDPR requires data processors to delete personal data when processing purpose ends

project.goal: Go CLI for WooCommerce to Shopify data migration with resume, field mapping, and GDPR cleanup
project.target_users: E-commerce operators and agencies
project.timeline: MVP in 6 weeks; full migration feature set in 10 weeks

backend.http_client: resty with retry and rate limiting

security.credentials: Config YAML with 600 permissions; never logged
security.pii_handling: No PII in log files; SQLite cleanup on migration completion

data.field_mapping: YAML mapping config per entity type

constraints.cross_compile: Single Go codebase to all target platforms

reasoner_reviews.product: The resume-on-failure via SQLite checkpoints is the most critical UX feature — large WooCommerce stores with 50,000+ products cannot risk restarting from scratch after a network interruption. The dry-run mode and structured log output will significantly reduce operator support requests by making migration state auditable.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The migration ordering constraint is important: products must be migrated before orders (orders reference Shopify product IDs), and customers before orders (orders reference Shopify customer IDs). The idempotent upsert strategy using WooCommerce IDs stored as Shopify metafields is correct for resume-on-failure — re-running a batch will detect existing Shopify resources and skip creation. The field mapping YAML should be validated at startup with clear error messages for missing required fields.
reasoner_reviews.security: GDPR compliance requires that customer email addresses and shipping addresses are NOT persisted in migration.log. The log filter must redact or omit PII fields from structured log output. SQLite migration.db contains customer records during migration — the cleanup command must VACUUM after DELETE to prevent SQLite page-level data recovery from the DB file.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Shopify REST Admin API 2 req/s rate limit requires a token bucket rate limiter with 500ms inter-request delay. At 2 req/s, migrating 50,000 products takes 25,000 seconds (~7 hours) — operators should be warned about expected duration in the CLI output.

backend.service_design: cobra CLI with root command `woo-migrate` and subcommands: `migrate products`, `migrate customers`, `migrate orders`, `migrate status`, `migrate cleanup`, `config init`. Each migrate subcommand accepts --batch-size (default 200), --since (ISO date filter for orders), --dry-run flag, --config path override. Global flags: --log-file, --verbose.
backend.api_patterns: WooCommerce REST API v3: GET /wp-json/wc/v3/products?page=N&per_page=100 with Basic Auth (consumer key:secret). Shopify REST Admin API 2024-01: POST /admin/api/2024-01/products.json with X-Shopify-Access-Token. Idempotent upsert: before creating, search Shopify for existing resource by metafield woo_id=X. Rate limiter: token bucket, 2 tokens/sec, 500ms minimum delay.
backend.failure_handling: Per-entity error handling: on Shopify API 4xx (except 429), log error to migration.log with woo_id and error detail, mark migration_checkpoints.status=failed, continue batch. On 429, exponential backoff starting at 2 seconds. On WooCommerce connection error, abort batch and surface error with retry instruction. Batch progress checkpointed every 50 records to SQLite.

security.auth_design: Config file .woo-to-shopify.yaml read from $HOME or --config path. File permission check at startup: if permissions > 0600, warn operator and offer to fix. Credentials held in memory struct; never passed to log functions. WooCommerce uses HTTP Basic Auth over HTTPS; Shopify uses bearer token in X-Shopify-Access-Token header.
security.privacy_controls: Log output uses a structured logger with a PII redaction middleware: fields customer_email, shipping_address, billing_address are replaced with [REDACTED] in all log entries. SQLite migration.db stores woo_customer_id and shopify_customer_id for checkpoint tracking but NOT email or address fields. `migrate cleanup` runs DELETE FROM migration_checkpoints WHERE entity_type='customer' followed by VACUUM to overwrite freed pages.
security.secrets_management: Config YAML never committed to version control; .woo-to-shopify.yaml added to .gitignore in generated config template. `config init` command creates config file with 600 permissions automatically.

data.entities: migration_checkpoints (id INTEGER PK, entity_type TEXT (product/customer/order), woo_id INTEGER, shopify_id TEXT, status TEXT (pending/success/failed/skipped), error_detail TEXT, migrated_at DATETIME). field_mapping_configs (id INTEGER PK, entity_type TEXT, woo_field TEXT, shopify_field TEXT, transform TEXT NULLABLE, required BOOL). import_error_logs (id INTEGER PK, checkpoint_id INTEGER FK, woo_id INTEGER, error_code TEXT, error_message TEXT, attempted_at DATETIME). shopify_resource_ids (id INTEGER PK, entity_type TEXT, woo_id INTEGER, shopify_id TEXT, shopify_handle TEXT, INDEX on (entity_type, woo_id) for foreign key resolution during order migration).
data.schema_notes: shopify_resource_ids table serves as the cross-reference for order migration: when migrating orders, the command joins on shopify_resource_ids to resolve Shopify product variant IDs and customer IDs from WooCommerce IDs. Migration must complete products then customers before orders.
